604	The gap (1) estimate is anchored to Bruns--Herzog,
605	\emph{Cohen--Macaulay Rings}, Construction~1.5.18 together with the
606	minimal-resolution induction (\S1.5): the trimming algorithm is a
607	finite-rank linear-algebra computation followed by induction on the
608	length of the resolution. The gap (2) estimate reflects the chain
609	Bruns--Herzog Theorem~1.4.13 \(\to\) Eagon--Northcott
610	\(\to\) Buchsbaum--Eisenbud's exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
611	\texttt{proposition-what-exact}), which characterises exactness of a
612	complex of finite free modules in terms of depths of the ideals of
613	\(r\)-minors of the transition matrices. Gap (2) is the largest of
614	the three open substrates and is a natural candidate for an upstream
615	Mathlib contribution rather than a project-internal proof.
616	
617	\paragraph{Gap (3) OBVIATED iter-200.} The iter-200 ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB
618	pivot (\ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}) replaces the literal
619	\(\texttt{ChainComplex}\,\mathbb N\) carrier with the abstract
620	\(\texttt{HasProjectiveDimensionLT}\) SES-descent pattern; under this
621	pattern, minimality of the resolution is \emph{not} used as input
622	anywhere in the inductive step. Consequently the snake-lemma carving
623	of the minimal resolution (gap (3)) is no longer a substrate
624	requirement: only gap (2) (\(\texttt{pd}\,M > 0 \Rightarrow
625	\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)) remains the binding gap for
626	the closure of \ref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}, and the
627	per-syzygy step of gap (1) is consumed only through the SES at
628	\(0 \to \ker f \to R^n \to M \to 0\) (no minimality input required).
629	See \ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot} for the SES-descent details.
630	
631	\paragraph{Gap (2) Stacks 00MF proof recipe.} The
632	Buchsbaum--Eisenbud exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
633	\texttt{proposition-what-exact}) reads: a complex
634	\(\dots \to R^{r_2} \xrightarrow{\varphi_2} R^{r_1}
635	 \xrightarrow{\varphi_1} R^{r_0}\) of finite free modules over a
636	Noetherian local ring \(R\) is exact at \(R^{r_i}\) (for \(i \geq 1\))
637	if and only if \(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi_{i+1} + \texttt{rank}\,\varphi_i
638	= r_i\) and \(\texttt{depth}\,I(\varphi_i) \geq i\), where
639	\(I(\varphi)\) denotes the ideal of \(r\)-minors of \(\varphi\) for
640	\(r = \texttt{rank}\,\varphi\). The recipe for an iter-201+ prover:
641	(a) define \(I(\varphi) \subseteq R\) as the ideal of all
642	\(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi\)-minors of \(\varphi\) (Mathlib supports
643	this via \(\texttt{Matrix.minors}\) and \(\texttt{Ideal.span}\) over the
644	finite index set of \(\binom{n}{r}\) tuples); (b) prove the depth
645	lower bound via induction on \(i\) using a Koszul-complex argument
646	on the regular sequence implicit in the exactness data; (c) derive
647	\(\texttt{pd}\,M > 0 \Rightarrow \texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)
648	as a corollary by taking the start \(\dots \to R^{r_1} \to R^{r_0} \to
649	M\) of a minimal resolution and applying the criterion at \(i = 1\).
650	Estimated \(\sim 150-200\) LOC project-side; Mathlib upstream PR
651	candidate.
652	
653	\paragraph{Closure assembly (iter-200 ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB pivot).} The
654	iter-200 substrate landing in \ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}
655	replaces the original (1)+(3)-dependent assembly with an SES-descent
656	recipe: given an \(M\) with \(\texttt{pd}_R(M) = k+1\), the
657	per-syzygy step of gap (1)
658	(\ref{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}) supplies a
659	surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\); the SES
660	\(0 \to \ker f \to R^n \to M \to 0\) plus the iter-200 helpers
661	\(\texttt{hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_ker\_of\_surjection}\) +
662	\(\texttt{hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_succ\_of\_hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_ker}\)
663	give \(\texttt{pd}_R(\ker f) = k\) exactly; gap (2) (the
664	Buchsbaum--Eisenbud criterion at the start of a minimal resolution)
665	delivers \(\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\); the iter-200
666	helper
667	\(\texttt{depth\_ker\_ge\_min\_of\_surjection\_finite\_localRing}\)
668	gives \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f \geq \min(\texttt{depth}\,R,
669	\texttt{depth}\,M + 1) = \texttt{depth}\,M + 1\); a complementary LES
670	upper bound (or the IH applied to \(\ker f\) under the strict
671	descent of \(\texttt{pd}\)) gives the matching upper bound. Combine
672	to get \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,M + 1\), then apply
673	the IH (\(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = k\)) for
674	\(k + \texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\), which rearranges
675	to \((k+1) + \texttt{depth}\,M = \texttt{depth}\,R\) as required.
676	Estimated closure-assembly cost (gaps in hand): \(\sim 50-80\) LOC
677	inductive code.
678	
679	\paragraph{Iter budget refinement.} Aggregating the per-gap
680	estimates with the closure assembly: gap (1) at 1--2 iters, gap (2)
681	at 2--3 iters, gap (3) at 1--2 iters, and the closure assembly
682	at 1 iter gives a total remaining iter budget of \textbf{5--8 iters}
683	for \cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}. The schedule may
684	parallelise across the gap (2) branch and the gap (1) \(\to\) gap (3)
685	chain --- so wall-clock could be as low as
686	\(\max(\text{gap (2)},\, \text{gap (1)} + \text{gap (3)}) +
687	\text{closure}\), i.e.\ \(\max(2\text{--}3,\, 2\text{--}4) + 1\),
688	yielding 3--5 iters in the parallel-best case. The substrate
689	sequencing constraint (gap (3) requires gap (1)) is hard; the gap
690	(2) branch is genuinely independent of that chain and could be
691	dispatched as a parallel prover lane the moment a free slot opens.
692	This estimate refines the earlier ``~6--12 iters'' coarse budget
693	recorded in \texttt{STRATEGY.md}: the refined window is somewhat
694	tighter on the upper end (5--8 vs.\ 6--12 sequential) and is
695	substantially tighter under parallel dispatch (3--5 parallel-best
696	vs.\ 6--12).

736	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
737	    (\texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}).}
738	  Let \(R\) be a local ring (Noetherian-ness NOT required at this
739	  step), \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\) the maximal ideal,
740	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\) the residue field, and \(M\) a finite
741	  \(R\)-module. Then there exist \(n \in \mathbb N\) and an
742	  \(R\)-linear surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\) such that:
743	  \begin{enumerate}
744	    \item \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\) (the minimal
745	      generator count of \(M\), via Nakayama);
746	    \item \(\ker f \subseteq \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) (the
747	      ``minimality'' condition: every entry of every relation lives
748	      in the maximal ideal, so the chosen lift has no trivial
749	      summands).
750	  \end{enumerate}
751	\end{lemma}
752	\begin{proof}
753	
754	\leanok
755	  Pick a \(\kappa\)-basis
756	  \(b : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \kappa \otimes_R M\) via
757	  \texttt{Module.finBasis} with
758	  \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\). Surjectivity of the
759	  canonical map \(M \twoheadrightarrow \kappa \otimes_R M\)
760	  (composition of \texttt{TensorProduct.mk\_surjective} and
761	  \texttt{Ideal.Quotient.mk\_surjective}) gives lifts
762	  \(m_i \in M\) with \(1 \otimes m_i = b_i\). Define
763	  \(f : R^n \to M\) by \(f x := \sum_i x_i \cdot m_i\) via
764	  \texttt{(Pi.basisFun R (Fin n)).constr R m}.
765	
766	  Surjectivity: \(\mathrm{range}\, f = \mathrm{span}_R(m_i)\) by
767	  \texttt{Module.Basis.constr\_range}, and
768	  \(\mathrm{span}_R(m_i) = M\) by Nakayama via
769	  \texttt{IsLocalRing.span\_eq\_top\_of\_tmul\_eq\_basis}.
770	
771	  Kernel containment: for \(x \in \ker f\) the relation
772	  \(\sum_i x_i m_i = 0\) tensors to
773	  \(\sum_i (1 \otimes x_i \cdot m_i) = 0\); using
774	  \texttt{TensorProduct.tmul\_smul} and the linear independence of
775	  \(b\) (\texttt{Module.Basis.linearIndependent}) we obtain
776	  \(\mathrm{residue}_R(x_i) = 0\) for every \(i\), i.e.\
777	  \(x_i \in \mathfrak m\). Decomposing
778	  \(x = \sum_i x_i \cdot \mathrm{Pi.single}\,i\,1\) then yields
779	  \(x \in \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) by
780	  \texttt{Submodule.smul\_mem\_smul} and
781	  \texttt{Submodule.sum\_mem}.
782	\end{proof}
783	
784	This lemma is the \emph{per-syzygy} step of gap (1)'s
785	minimal-resolution carving: given a finite \(R\)-module \(M\)
786	over a local ring \(R\), it produces a minimal surjection
787	\(R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\). Iter-200's ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB pivot
788	(\ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}) replaces the original
789	\(\mathbb N\)-recursive \(\texttt{ChainComplex}\) assembly target
790	with abstract \(\texttt{HasProjectiveDimensionLT}\) SES descent: only
791	the per-syzygy SES is consumed; the iterated chain-complex carrier
792	is not needed.

917	\((\sim 50\)--\(80\) LOC).} From

925	\(\texttt{depth\_of\_short\_exact}\) part (2). Upper bound

948	\[

950	  e \circ \texttt{Ext.mk}_0\,(\texttt{ofHom}\,A) = 0
951	  \quad \text{in } \texttt{Ext}^{p+0}(\kappa, R^n)
952	  \text{ when } A_{ij} \in \texttt{Ann}_R\,\kappa.

955	sending \(\texttt{Pi.single}\,j\,1 \mapsto \texttt{Pi.single}\,i\,1\)

958	(\(A = \sum_{i,j} A(\texttt{Pi.single}\,j\,1)\,i \cdot
959	   \texttt{elemMap}_{ij}\)), and the closure helper

962	\(\texttt{ModuleCat.hom\_sum}\) + \(\texttt{Ext.mk}_0\texttt{\_sum}\)
963	+ \(\texttt{Ext.comp\_sum}\) and the scalar siblings
964	\(\texttt{ModuleCat.hom\_smul}\) + \(\texttt{Ext.mk}_0\texttt{\_smul}\)
965	+ \(\texttt{Ext.comp\_smul}\) (\emph{not}
966	\(\texttt{Ext.bilinearCompOfLinear}\) as originally projected; the

972	gives

974	  \texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R - 1}(\kappa, M)
975	  \xrightarrow{\;\delta\;}

985	(\(m \geq 1\), \(\texttt{depth}\,R^m = \texttt{depth}\,R\)).

990	free).

1032	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
1033	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
1034	
1035	\begin{definition}
1036	
1037	\leanok
1038	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
1039	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
1040	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
1041	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
1042	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
1043	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1044	  % "A Noetherian local ring \(R\) is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
1045	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
1046	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
1047	  A Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
1048	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\) (equivalently, if \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
1049	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite \(R\)-module \(M\) means
1050	  \(\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)\), cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
1051	\end{definition}
1052

1061	% technical-bridge sorry inside `regularLocal_inductive_step` for the
1062	% `R⧸(x)`-vs-`QuotSMulTop x R` linear-equiv upgrade (~10-20 LOC iter-186 close
1063	% via `Submodule.ideal_span_singleton_smul` + `QuotSMulTop.mem_annihilator`).
1064	% Neither has a `\lean{...}` pin in this chapter (the lean-vs-blueprint-checker

1069	% (~300 LOC); (b) Koszul-homology bypass via `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`;

1071	\begin{corollary}
1072	
1073	\leanok
1074	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
1075	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
1076	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
1077	  
1078	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
1079	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
1080	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1081	  % "Let \(R\) be a regular local ring and let
1082	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) be a minimal set of generators
1083	  %  for the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\). Then
1084	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) is a regular sequence, and
1085	  %  each \(R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)\) is a regular local ring
1086	  %  of dimension \(d - c\). In particular \(R\) is Cohen-Macaulay."
1087	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
1088	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is
1089	  regular local of Krull dimension \(d\), then \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\).
1090	\end{corollary}
1091	
1092	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1093	% "Note that \(R/x_1R\) is a Noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\)
1094	%  by Lemma REF with \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\)
1095	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
1096	%  Since \(R\) is a domain by Lemma REF
1097	%  \(x_1\) is a nonzerodivisor."
1098	\begin{proof}
1099	  
1100	  
1101	  
1102	  
1103	  
1104	\leanok
1105	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be regular of dimension \(d\) and pick a minimal generating set
1106	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\). By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), \(R\) is
1107	  an integral domain, so \(x_1\) is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient \(R/x_1 R\) is a Noetherian
1108	  local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\) (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
1109	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by \(x_2, \dots, x_d\), hence
1110	  has dimension exactly \(d - 1\) and is again regular of dimension \(d - 1\). Inducting on \(d\),
1111	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) is an \(R\)-regular sequence, so
1112	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq d\). The reverse inequality \(\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d\)
1113	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
1114	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\), i.e.\
1115	  \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay.
1116	
1117	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
1118	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} as follows: take \(M := R\) itself. Then \(M\) is
1119	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank \(1\)), so \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\). The
1120	  formula gives \(\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)\), a tautology. The non-tautological
1121	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension \(d\), every
1122	  finitely generated module \(M\) has \(\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d\) (Stacks tag 00O2,
1123	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an \(M\) then
1124	  relates \(\text{depth}(M)\) to \(d - \text{pd}_R(M)\), which is the form most often used in
1125	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
1126	  \(R\) Cohen--Macaulay itself.
1127	\end{proof}
1128	
1129	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
1130	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
1131	
1132	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
1133	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from a regular projective surface \(S\) to an abelian variety \(A\)
1134	across a closed point \(x \in S\) that is missing from the domain of definition \(U \subseteq S\).
1135	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
1136	
1137	\begin{enumerate}
1138	  \item The local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
1139	    dimension \(2\) (because \(S\) is regular and \(\dim S = 2\)).
1140	  \item By Corollary~REF it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
1141	    \(\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2\). Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
1142	    \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\)-regular sequence of length \(2\) (any two transverse generators).
1143	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S\) on \(U = S \setminus \{x\}\) extend
1144	    uniquely across \(x\) because \(H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) and
1145	    \(H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
1146	    \(\geq 2\) at \(x\) by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
1147	  \item For a rational map \(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) with codimension-\(\geq 2\) indeterminacy
1148	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-\(\geq 2\) input produces a sheaf-theoretic
1149	    extension of \(f\) to all of \(S\): pull back any sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_A\)-affine-locally,
1150	    obtain sections over \(U\), and extend across \(x\) by the above.
1151	\end{enumerate}
1152	
1153	So Corollary~REF is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
1154	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
1155	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~REF and
1156	Corollary~REF are formalized in
1157	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
1158	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.
1159

43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-\(1\) extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	
94	
95	\leanok
96	  [Indeterminacy locus of a rational map]
97	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}
98	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus}
99	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let \(V\) and \(W\)
100	  % be varieties over \(k\)\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
101	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
102	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi\) is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
103	  % \(v\) of \(V\) if \(v \in U\) for some \((U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi\). The set \(U_1\) of
104	  % \(v\) at which \(\varphi\) is defined is open, and there is a regular map
105	  % \(\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W\) such that \((U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi\) --- clearly,
106	  % \(U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U\) and we can define \(\varphi_1\) to be
107	  % the regular map such that \(\varphi_1 | U = \varphi_U\) for all $(U, \varphi_U) \in
108	  % \varphi$."
109	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S I.3, p.~16.}
110	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
111	  The \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\) is the open set
112	  \[
113	    \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;:=\; \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in f} U \;\subseteq\; X,
114	  \]
115	  i.e.\ the union of the dense opens of all representatives of \(f\). The
116	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of \(f\) is the closed complement
117	  \[
118	    Z(f) \;:=\; X \smallsetminus \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;\subseteq\; X.
119	  \]
120	  The map \(f\) is regular (i.e.\ defined on all of \(X\)) if and only if \(Z(f) = \emptyset\).
121	
122	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
123	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} is a method on the
124	  Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure pinned at
125	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; it returns a
126	  \texttt{Set X} (the underlying carrier of the closed complement of
127	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain}, which is already exposed by Mathlib). The
128	  closedness of \(Z(f)\) is a separate \texttt{IsClosed} witness, packaged either as a
129	  field of \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} or as a standalone lemma
130	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus}, whichever is cleaner with
131	  Mathlib's existing API at commit \texttt{b80f227}.
132	\end{definition}
133	
134	\begin{definition}
135	
136	
137	\leanok
138	  [Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps]
139	  \label{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}
140	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree}
141	  
142	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 --- the conclusion
143	  % "an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)"
144	  % is the standing predicate we abstract (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
145	  % PDF page 22).
146	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow W\) from a normal
147	  % variety \(V\) to a complete variety \(W\) is defined on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose
148	  % complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)."
149	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
150	  conclusion).}
151	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
152	  We say \(f\) is \emph{codim-1-indeterminacy-free} (or has \emph{codimension-\(\geq 2\)
153	  indeterminacy}) if every irreducible component of the closed subset \(Z(f) \subseteq X\)
154	  has codimension \(\geq 2\) in \(X\). Equivalently, no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in
155	  \(Z(f)\); equivalently, every point \(\eta \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\)
156	  lies in \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\).
157	
158	  \paragraph{Why the abstraction.} Milne's Theorem~3.1 (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}
159	  below) and Lemma~3.3 (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} below) both produce
160	  conclusions about the codimension of \(Z(f)\). Phrasing the codim-\(\geq 2\) condition
161	  as a named predicate keeps the cited statements concise and lets the consumer
162	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} read off the combination
163	  (\(Z(f)\) has codim \(\geq 2\) from \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} \emph{and} \(Z(f)\) is
164	  empty or pure codim 1 from \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} \(\Rightarrow\) \(Z(f)\)
165	  is empty) as a single line.
166	
167	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
168	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} is a \texttt{Prop}-valued
169	  predicate, encoded via Mathlib's \texttt{Order.coheight} on the specialisation
170	  preorder of \texttt{X.carrier} as in
171	  \cref{def:prime_divisor}: \(f\) is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if and only if for every
172	  \(\eta \in X\) with \(\texttt{Order.coheight\ }\eta = 1\), $\eta \in
173	  \texttt{f.domain}\(. The encoding matches the standing \)\mathrm{Order.coheight}$
174	  convention pinned in \cref{def:prime_divisor}, so prime divisors of \(X\) and the
175	  hypothesis ``no codim-\(1\) indeterminacy'' use the same Mathlib idiom.
176	\end{definition}

178	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
179	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
180	
181	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
182	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
183	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
184	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
185	
186	The smooth-implies-regular-local-stalk implication itself is the Jacobian-criterion
187	direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}; we package it as a separate sub-lemma so that
188	\cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} below reads cleanly as ``regular local of dimension
189	\(1\) is a DVR'', with the regularity prerequisite supplied by the sub-lemma.
190	
191	\subsection{Stages 5a/5b: K\"ahler-differentials localisation substrate (iter-193)}
192	\label{subsec:kaehler_localisation_substrate}
193	
194	The Jacobian-criterion direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}
195	(\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} below) factors on the Lean side
196	through a six-stage chain. Stages~1--2 (smooth \(\Rightarrow\) flat at a
197	stalk; smooth \(\Rightarrow\) admits a standard-smooth presentation
198	locally) are axiom-clean since iter-191; Stages~3--4 (the
199	scheme-to-algebra bridge for standard-smooth presentations; freeness of
200	\(\Omega_{S/R}\) for a standard-smooth algebra \(R \to S\)) are
201	axiom-clean since iter-192. The remaining Stages~5--6 reduce the
202	Stacks~00TT conclusion to a freeness plus rank computation on K\"ahler
203	differentials, transported through localisation at the prime defining
204	the stalk. Iter-193 packaged Stages~5a (transport of freeness) and 5b
205	(matching rank under localisation) as the two standalone helpers below;
206	Stage~6 (the remaining Stacks~00OE smooth-algebra dimension formula
207	plus Stacks~02JK cotangent--K\"ahler-over-a-field bridge) is the open
208	Mathlib gap, decomposed iter-198 into the three named sub-lemmas
209	\cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (6.A),
210	\cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (6.B), and
211	\cref{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly} (6.C) in
212	\cref{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition} immediately following.
213	
214	\begin{lemma}
215	
216	
217	\leanok
218	  [Stage 5a --- K\"ahler-differentials freeness transports through localisation]
219	  \label{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.module_free_kaehlerDifferential_localization}
221	  Let \(R\) and \(S\) be commutative rings equipped with an \(R\)-algebra
222	  structure on \(S\), and assume that the module of K\"ahler differentials
223	  \(\Omega_{S/R}\) is free as an \(S\)-module. Let \(M \subseteq S\) be a
224	  submonoid and let \(S_M\) be a localisation of \(S\) at \(M\), so that
225	  \(R \to S \to S_M\) is a tower of \(R\)-algebras. Then the module of
226	  K\"ahler differentials \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) is free as an
227	  \(S_M\)-module.
228	\end{lemma}
229	
230	\begin{proof}
231	  The canonical comparison map
232	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) induced by the localisation map
233	  \(S \to S_M\) realises \(\Omega_{S_M/R}\) as the localisation of the
234	  \(S\)-module \(\Omega_{S/R}\) at \(M\); i.e.\ it satisfies Mathlib's
235	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} predicate along the underlying ring
236	  localisation. This is Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, packaged in Mathlib as
237	  \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule\_map}. Freeness of a
238	  module is preserved under localisation of modules via Mathlib's
239	  \texttt{Module.free\_of\_isLocalizedModule}; combining the two yields
240	  the conclusion.
241	\end{proof}
242	
243	\begin{lemma}
244	
245	
246	\leanok
247	  [Stage 5b --- K\"ahler-differentials rank under localisation equals
248	  the relative dimension]
249	  \label{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
250	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.rank_kaehlerDifferential_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
251	  \uses{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
252	  With notation as in \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization},
253	  assume in addition that the \(R\)-algebra \(S\) is \emph{standard
254	  smooth of relative dimension \(n\)} (Mathlib's
255	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,\(n\)\,\(R\)\,\(S\),
256	  the algebra-side packaging of Stacks tag \texttt{00T7}(2)) and that
257	  the localisation \(S_M\) is non-trivial as a ring. Then the rank of
258	  \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) over \(S_M\) equals \(n\).
259	\end{lemma}
260	
261	\begin{proof}
262	  Mathlib's
263	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.\linebreak rank\_kaehlerDifferential}
264	  asserts \(\mathrm{rank}_S(\Omega_{S/R}) = n\) under the
265	  standard-smooth hypothesis. The localisation map
266	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) is an
267	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} along \(S \to S_M\) (Stacks tag
268	  \texttt{02JK}; cf.\ the proof of
269	  \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}), and Mathlib's
270	  \texttt{Module.lift\_rank\_of\_isLocalizedModule\_of\_free} transports
271	  module rank through an \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} up to
272	  \(\mathrm{Cardinal.lift}\). Since \(n\) is a natural number both
273	  ranks equal \(n\), so
274	  \(\mathrm{rank}_{S_M}(\Omega_{S_M / R}) = n\) as required.
275	\end{proof}
276	
277	\subsection{Stage 6 sub-decomposition: 00OE + 02JK chain (iter-198)}
278	\label{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition}
279	
280	Stage 6 is the residual gap inside
281	\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}'s six-stage chain: given Stages
282	1--5b (a standard-smooth presentation of an affine chart
283	\(R \to S\) at a point \(z\), with \(\Omega_{S/R}\) free of rank \(n\)
284	and \(\Omega_{S_\mathfrak q / R}\) free of rank \(n\) on the stalk
285	\(S_\mathfrak q = \mathcal O_{X, z}\)), one must conclude that
286	\(S_\mathfrak q\) is a regular local ring. The Stacks proof of tag
287	\texttt{00TT} ultimately reduces to two ingredients which Mathlib does
288	not yet ship at commit \texttt{b80f227}: (6.A) the smooth-algebra
289	Krull-dimension formula (Stacks tag \texttt{00OE}, in the algebra
290	chapter as Lemma~\texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}),
291	and (6.B) the cotangent / K\"ahler-differential bridge over a field
292	base (Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, in the algebra chapter as
293	Lemma~\texttt{lemma-differential-seq} plus the separable-residue-field
294	refinement of Lemma~\texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}). The two
295	ingredients combine (6.C) into the regularity conclusion via
296	\(\dim S_\mathfrak q = \mathrm{rank}_{S_\mathfrak q}\Omega_{S_\mathfrak
297	q / R} = \dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\),
298	which is the cotangent-space characterisation of regularity that
299	Mathlib's \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.iff\_finrank\_cotangentSpace}
300	already exposes. The next three lemmas package the three sub-gaps as
301	named iter-199+ prover targets.
302	
303	\begin{lemma}
304	  [Stage 6.A --- Smooth-algebra Krull-dimension formula (Stacks 00OE)]
305	  \label{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
306	  \lean{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.ringKrullDim_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
307	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
308	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field
309	  % (canonically known as tag 00OE; tag-to-label cross-check not in the
310	  % local `tags/tags` file, see iter-198 writer report note) (read from
311	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L28206--28217 in section
312	  % "Dimension theory of finitely generated $k$-algebras").
313	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $k$ be a field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$
314	  % algebra. Let $X = \Spec(S)$. Let $\mathfrak p \subset S$ be a prime
315	  % ideal, and let $x \in X$ be the corresponding point. Then we have
316	  % $\dim_x(X) = \dim(S_{\mathfrak p}) + \text{trdeg}_k\
317	  % \kappa(\mathfrak p)$."
318	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~00OE (\(=\) \texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}).}
319	  Let \(k\) be a field, let \(S\) be a finite-type \(k\)-algebra, and let
320	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\) be a prime ideal corresponding to a point
321	  \(x \in \mathrm{Spec}(S)\). Then
322	  \[
323	    \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)
324	    \;=\; \dim S_{\mathfrak q} + \mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q).
325	  \]
326	  In particular, if \(\mathfrak q\) is a maximal ideal (so \(x\) is a
327	  closed point) and \(k\) is algebraically closed, then
328	  \(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = k\) (Nullstellensatz), so
329	  \(\mathrm{trdeg}_k\,\kappa(\mathfrak q) = 0\) and
330	  \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q} = \dim_x(\mathrm{Spec}\,S)\).
331	
332	  \paragraph{Geometric application.} Applied to the iter-193 Stage~3
333	  setup --- \(k = \bar k\), \(S = \Gamma(X.\mathrm{left}, V)\) a
334	  standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra of relative dimension \(n\) (Stage
335	  3), and \(\mathfrak q\) the prime defining the point \(z \in V\) --- the
336	  formula yields \(\dim(\mathcal O_{X, z}) = \dim S_{\mathfrak q} = n -
337	  \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(z)\). For \(z\) a closed point this is
338	  simply \(n\); for the generic point of a codim-\(1\) subvariety
339	  (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) it is \(1\).
340	\end{lemma}
341	
342	\begin{lemma}
343	  [Stage 6.B --- Cotangent \(\leftrightarrow\) K\"ahler over a field (Stacks 02JK)]
344	  \label{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
345	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangent_iso_residue_tensor_kaehler_of_formallySmooth_residue}
346	  % \lean{...} updated iter-199 review: iter-199 prover landed the
347	  % closed-point iso as a project-local axiom-clean helper (private
348	  % theorem in AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme namespace). Companion siblings:
349	  %   cotangent_iso_maximalIdeal_residue_tensor_kaehler_of_formallySmooth_residue
350	  %   finrank_cotangentSpace_of_formallySmooth_residue
351	  %   finrank_cotangentSpace_of_bijective_algebraMap_residue (closed-point bundled)
352	  \uses{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}
353	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], lemma-differential-seq (canonically
354	  % known as the right-exact conormal portion of tag 02JK; the
355	  % short-exact / split refinement is the proof step inside
356	  % lemma-separable-smooth) (read from
357	  % references/stacks-algebra.tex, L34087--34104 and L38724--38766).
358	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "In diagram (\ref{equation-functorial-omega}),
359	  % suppose that $S \to S'$ is surjective with kernel $I \subset S$, and
360	  % assume that $R' = R$. Then there is a canonical exact sequence of
361	  % $S'$-modules
362	  % $$I/I^2 \longrightarrow \Omega_{S/R} \otimes_S S' \longrightarrow
363	  % \Omega_{S'/R} \longrightarrow 0$$
364	  % The leftmost map is characterized by the rule that $f \in I$ maps
365	  % to $\text{d}f \otimes 1$."
366	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Hence we get
367	  % $$\dim_{\kappa} \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa = \dim_\kappa
368	  % \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 + \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) \geq \dim R +
369	  % \text{trdeg}_k (\kappa) = \dim_{\mathfrak q} S$$
370	  % (see Lemma \ref{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field} for
371	  % the last equality) with equality if and only if $R$ is regular."
372	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag~02JK (right-exact half = \texttt{lemma-differential-seq}; separable-residue refinement inside \texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}).}
373	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(R\) be a Noetherian local
374	  \(k\)-algebra with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\) and residue field
375	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\). The canonical exact sequence
376	  \[
377	    \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
378	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa
379	    \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{\kappa / k}
380	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
381	  \]
382	  identifies the cotangent space \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) (a
383	  \(\kappa\)-vector space) with the kernel of the right map. When
384	  \(\kappa / k\) is separable (in particular when \(k\) is
385	  algebraically closed and \(\kappa = k\), so \(\Omega_{\kappa / k}
386	  = 0\)) the leftmost map is injective and the sequence is short exact
387	  --- in fact split short exact when there is a \(k\)-algebra section
388	  \(\kappa \to R\) (e.g.\ at a closed point of a \(\bar k\)-variety).
389	  In particular, over an algebraically closed field \(k = \bar k\) and
390	  at a closed point \(\mathfrak m\),
391	  \[
392	    \Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa \;\simeq\; \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2
393	  \]
394	  as \(\kappa\)-vector spaces; hence
395	  \(\dim_\kappa(\Omega_{R/k} \otimes_R \kappa)
396	   = \dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\).
397	\end{lemma}
398	
399	\begin{lemma}
400	  [Stage 6.C --- Assembly: smooth standard-smooth stalk is regular local]
401	  \label{lem:stage6_regular_stalk_assembly}
402	  % NOTE (iter-199): the Lean target for this 6.C assembly is the body
403	  % of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isRegularLocalRing\_stalk\_of\_smooth}
404	  % itself (see \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}); the iter-198
405	  % blueprint named a planned \texttt{...\_aux} extraction that has not
406	  % been created in the Lean file. Treat the lemma below as the in-body
407	  % closure pattern that the existing helper consumes once Stages 6.A and
408	  % 6.B land, rather than as a separately-pinned Lean declaration.
409	  \uses{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim,
410	        lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula,
411	        lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field}
412	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, let
413	  \(R \to S\) be a standard-smooth \(\bar k\)-algebra presentation of
414	  relative dimension \(n\) on an affine chart \(\Spec S \subseteq X\),
415	  and let \(z \in \Spec S\) be a point corresponding to a prime
416	  \(\mathfrak q \subset S\). Then the stalk \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) is a
417	  regular local ring of Krull dimension
418	  \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\). In the
419	  closed-point case (\(\kappa(\mathfrak q) = \bar k\),
420	  \(\mathrm{trdeg} = 0\)) the dimension equals \(n\); in the
421	  codim-\(1\) generic-point case (\(\mathrm{trdeg} = n - 1\)) the
422	  dimension equals \(1\).
423	\end{lemma}
424	
425	\begin{proof}
426	  Combine the three sub-lemmas:
427	  \begin{enumerate}
428	    \item By \cref{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim} (Stage~5b)
429	      the localised K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q} / R}\)
430	      is free of rank \(n\) over \(S_{\mathfrak q}\).
431	    \item By \cref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula} (Stage 6.A,
432	      Stacks 00OE) the Krull dimension of \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) equals
433	      \(\dim_z \Spec S - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\),
434	      which for a standard-smooth presentation of relative dimension
435	      \(n\) equals \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\).
436	      (For the standard-smooth presentation the dimension
437	      \(\dim_z \Spec S = n\) follows from the global-complete-intersection
438	      structure: \(n\) variables modulo \(n - \dim\) equations, see
439	      Stacks \texttt{lemma-relative-global-complete-intersection-smooth}.)
440	    \item By \cref{lem:cotangent_kahler_over_field} (Stage 6.B,
441	      Stacks 02JK) the residue-field tensor \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak q}/R}
442	      \otimes_{S_{\mathfrak q}} \kappa(\mathfrak q)\) is isomorphic to
443	      \(\mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) as \(\kappa(\mathfrak q)\)-vector
444	      spaces. Combined with (1), \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak
445	      m / \mathfrak m^2 = n\) at the closed-point case (and in general
446	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\) by base-change
447	      of the standard-smooth presentation to the closed-point fibre).
448	    \item Compare (2) and (3): both \(\dim S_{\mathfrak q}\) and
449	      \(\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\) equal
450	      \(n - \mathrm{trdeg}_{\bar k}\,\kappa(\mathfrak q)\); the
451	      regularity criterion for Noetherian local rings (the equality
452	      \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2 = \dim R\) is the
453	      defining condition for a regular local ring, Stacks
454	      \texttt{definition-regular-local} \(=\) Mathlib
455	      \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing}) then gives \(S_{\mathfrak q}\)
456	      regular. The scheme-level transfer
457	      \(S_{\mathfrak q} = \mathcal O_{X, z}\) (Stage 3
458	      \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} on the affine chart) completes
459	      the conclusion.
460	  \end{enumerate}
461	\end{proof}

580	\subsection{Stage 6.A iter-200 substrate: Stacks 00OE 3-step decomposition}
581	\label{subsec:stage6_iib_substrate_iter200}
582	\textit{Source: \texttt{coe-stacks00oe} mathlib-analogist
583	cross-domain-inspiration recipe (iter-200, persistent
584	\texttt{analogies/coe-stacks00oe.md}); 7 axiom-clean
585	\texttt{private} substrate theorems landed in

588	
589	The iter-200 Lane COE prover phase decomposed Stage 6.A
590	(\ref{lem:smooth_algebra_krull_dim_formula}, Stacks 00OE) into a
591	3-step Mathlib-chain. Steps 1 and 2 landed axiom-clean as
592	project-local \texttt{private} theorems; Step 3 landed in an
593	additive parameterised form awaiting the Jacobian-regular-sequence

595	
596	\paragraph{Step 1 --- Krull-dim \(\Rightarrow\) height (trivial,
597	\(\sim 1\) line).} Re-export of Mathlib's
598	\(\texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}\) under
599	the project-local name

603	\paragraph{Step 2 --- Polynomial-ring maximal-ideal height
604	(\(\sim 60\) LOC).} For \(\texttt{MvPolynomial}\,(\texttt{Fin}\,n)\,k\)
605	with \(k\) a field, every maximal ideal has height \emph{exactly}
606	\(n\). Lower bound:
607	\(\texttt{MvPolynomial.maximalIdeal\_height\_ge\_card\_of\_field}\),
608	induction on \texttt{Fin n} via \(\texttt{MvPolynomial.finSuccEquiv}\)
609	+ Mathlib's \(\texttt{Polynomial.height\_eq\_height\_add\_one}\) plus
610	the Jacobson contraction

612	bound:
613	\(\texttt{MvPolynomial.maximalIdeal\_height\_le\_natCard\_of\_field}\)
614	via \(\texttt{Ideal.height\_le\_ringKrullDim\_of\_ne\_top}\) +
615	\(\texttt{MvPolynomial.ringKrullDim\_of\_isNoetherianRing}\) +
616	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim\_eq\_zero\_of\_field}\). Combined: the
617	\texttt{Fin n}-form
618	\(\texttt{MvPolynomial.maximalIdeal\_height\_eq\_card}\) and the
619	general \texttt{[Finite ι]}-form
620	\(\texttt{MvPolynomial.maximalIdeal\_height\_eq\_natCard}\) via
621	\(\texttt{MvPolynomial.renameEquiv}\) +
622	\(\texttt{RingEquiv.height\_map}\). Capstone packaging the
623	Steps 1+2 composition for any localisation at a maximal ideal:
624	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim\_localization\_atMaximal\_MvPolynomial}\).
625	
626	\paragraph{Step 3 --- Drop by regular-sequence length (additive form
627	in hand, witness pending).} Step 3 generally:
628	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim}\,(R'_{m'} / (f_j)_{j \in \sigma}) +
629	 |\sigma| = \texttt{ringKrullDim}\,R'_{m'}\). The additive form is
630	landed as
631	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim\_quotient\_add\_eq\_of\_regular\_sequence}\)
632	via Mathlib's
633	\(\texttt{ringKrullDim\_add\_length\_eq\_ringKrullDim\_of\_isRegular}\),

18	%   2. \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} -- opens contain

32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:coheight_setup}
34	
35	A point \(z\) of a scheme \(X\) has two natural ``dimension'' invariants attached
36	to it. The \emph{Krull dimension of the local ring at \(z\)}, written
37	\(\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z}\), is an algebraic quantity: the supremum of the lengths
38	of chains of prime ideals in the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\). The
39	\emph{coheight} of \(z\) in \(X\), written \(\operatorname{coheight}_X(z)\), is a
40	topological quantity attached to the specialisation preorder on the underlying
41	topological space of \(X\): the supremum of the lengths of strict-generisation
42	chains \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in \(X\), where \(\prec\) is the
43	strict specialisation order \(x \prec y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x \wedge y \neq x\).
44	
45	These two invariants agree:
46	\[
47	  \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_X(z),
48	\]
49	which is the headline theorem \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} of this
50	chapter. The identification is folklore in algebraic geometry --- it is the
51	statement that the dimension of the local ring at \(z\) counts irreducible
52	closed subsets of \(X\) containing \(z\) --- but at the project's pinned Mathlib
53	commit (\texttt{b80f227}) the equation is not packaged as a single declaration
54	and must be assembled project-side from four Mathlib API pieces.
55	
56	\paragraph{Why the bridge is needed.} Two downstream files in the project
57	currently work around the missing bridge by threading explicit
58	\texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk \_)]} instance arguments at every
59	codim-\(1\) use site:
60	\begin{itemize}
61	  \item \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} carries an internal conjunction
62	    \[
63	      \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing\ } \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;\wedge\;
64	      \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1
65	    \]
66	    inside the private helper
67	    \texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot};
68	    the Krull-dim conjunct is supplied by \emph{this chapter's}
69	    \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}, leaving only the
70	    irreducible \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing} half (which is the separate
71	    Stacks tag \texttt{00TT} sub-project, out of scope here).
72	  \item \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}'s order-at-a-prime-divisor map
73	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} currently carries an explicit
74	    \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} instance argument;
75	    after \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} lands, the instance is
76	    synthesisable from the codim-\(1\) hypothesis on \(Y\) alone, so the explicit
77	    argument can be dropped.
78	\end{itemize}
79	
80	\paragraph{Chapter structure.} \cref{sec:coheight_topology} proves the
81	topology-only lemma that coheight is preserved by open immersions
82	(\cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}). \cref{sec:coheight_spec_duality}
83	identifies coheight on a \(\Spec\) with the height of the underlying prime ideal
84	(\cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}). \cref{sec:coheight_bridge}
85	assembles the bridge \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}. The closing
86	\cref{sec:coheight_le_one_instance} packages the codim-\(1\) specialisation
87	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} that downstream files consume.
88	
89	\section{Coheight is preserved by open immersions}
90	\label{sec:coheight_topology}
91	
92	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
93	% L246--257 (the recipe extracts the lemma from a generic
94	% \texttt{Specializes.mem\_open} chain-lifting argument). The lemma is a
95	% generic topological fact about the specialisation preorder; no external
96	% reference shipped at \texttt{b80f227} states it under this exact
97	% formulation.
98	
99	\begin{lemma}
100	
101	\leanok
102	  [Coheight on opens equals coheight in the ambient space]
103	  \label{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
104	  \lean{Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
105	  Let \(X\) be a topological space carrying the specialisation preorder, and
106	  let \(U \subseteq X\) be an open subset. For any \(z \in U\),
107	  \[
108	    \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr),
109	  \]
110	  where on the right \(U\) carries the subspace topology and $\langle z, \,
111	  z \in U\rangle\( denotes \)z\( viewed as a point of \)U$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	\begin{proof}
115	
116	\leanok
117	  Both sides are by definition the supremum, over natural numbers \(n\), of the
118	  set of \(n\) for which there exists a strictly increasing chain
119	  \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in the relevant
120	  specialisation preorder (with \(\prec\) denoting strict specialisation). It
121	  therefore suffices to give an order-preserving bijection between the
122	  strict-generisation chains starting at \(z\) in \(X\) and those starting at the
123	  corresponding point of \(U\).
124	
125	  \emph{Chains in \(U\) embed into \(X\).} The inclusion \(U \hookrightarrow X\) is
126	  continuous, so it preserves specialisation: if \(y \rightsquigarrow x\) in \(U\)
127	  then \(y \rightsquigarrow x\) in \(X\), and distinct points of \(U\) remain
128	  distinct in \(X\). Hence every strict-generisation chain in \(U\) starting at
129	  \(z\) lifts to one in \(X\) of the same length.
130	
131	  \emph{Chains in \(X\) starting at \(z\) all lie in \(U\).} Conversely, suppose
132	  \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) is a strict-generisation chain
133	  in \(X\) with \(a_0 = z \in U\). For each \(i \geq 0\), the relation $a_i \succeq
134	  z\( (in the specialisation preorder) is \)z \rightsquigarrow a_i$, i.e.\
135	  \(a_i\) is in the closure of \(\{z\}\) \emph{viewed from above}; in our
136	  convention the strict order \(a \prec b\) records \(b \rightsquigarrow a\)
137	  (the generic point is the strict maximum), so \(a_i \succeq z\) in \(X\) means
138	  \(a_i \rightsquigarrow z\) in \(X\). By the Mathlib lemma
139	  \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} (any specialisation
140	  \(a \rightsquigarrow z\) with \(z\) in an open set \(U\) forces \(a \in U\), since
141	  \(U\) is open and contains \(z\), hence contains every point whose closure
142	  contains \(z\) --- equivalently every \(a\) with \(a \rightsquigarrow z\)),
143	  every \(a_i\) lies in \(U\). Thus the chain descends to a strict-generisation
144	  chain $\langle z\rangle = \langle a_0\rangle \prec \langle a_1\rangle \prec
145	  \cdots \prec \langle a_n\rangle\( in \)U$ of the same length.
146	
147	  These two operations are mutually inverse on the set of strict-generisation
148	  chains starting at \(z\) (resp.\ at \(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\)) and
149	  preserve length, so the suprema agree.
150	\end{proof}
151	
152	\section{Coheight on Spec equals height in PrimeSpectrum}
153	\label{sec:coheight_spec_duality}
154	
155	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
156	% L259--268. The convention reconciliation step traces to Mathlib's
157	% \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} in \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace} L438--447
158	% and to \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} +
159	% \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} in \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}.
160	
161	\begin{lemma}
162	
163	\leanok
164	  [Coheight on \(\Spec R\) equals height in \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R\)]
165	  \label{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
166	  \lean{Order.coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
167	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(p \in \Spec R\) (viewed as a point of
168	  the scheme spectrum, with the specialisation preorder). Then
169	  \[
170	    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(p) \;=\; \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(p),
171	  \]
172	  where the right-hand side is the supremum of the lengths of strict
173	  prime-ideal chains \(\mathfrak{p}_0 \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{p}\)
174	  ending at the prime \(\mathfrak{p}\) corresponding to \(p\).
175	\end{lemma}
176	
177	\begin{proof}
178	
179	\leanok
180	  The specialisation preorder on \(\Spec R\) is the dual of the natural
181	  inclusion preorder on \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\). Concretely, for
182	  \(p, q \in \Spec R\) with underlying primes \(\mathfrak{p}, \mathfrak{q}\),
183	  the relation \(p \leq q\) in the specialisation preorder (which is
184	  \(q \rightsquigarrow p\) in topological notation) corresponds to the prime
185	  containment \(\mathfrak{q} \subseteq \mathfrak{p}\). This is the content of
186	  the Mathlib lemma \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} (located in
187	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace}, lines 438--447 at commit
188	  \texttt{b80f227}, with the explicit remark that the preorder on \(\Spec R\)
189	  equals the order-dual of the preorder on \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R\)).
190	
191	  Under this order anti-isomorphism, coheight of \(p\) in \(\Spec R\)
192	  (= supremum of strict-generisation chain lengths starting at \(p\),
193	  i.e.\ ascending chains \(p \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in the
194	  specialisation preorder) translates to height of \(\mathfrak{p}\) in
195	  \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\) (= supremum of strict descending prime-ideal
196	  chains $\mathfrak{p} \supsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \supsetneq \cdots \supsetneq
197	  \mathfrak{q}_n$, equivalently strict ascending chains
198	  $\mathfrak{q}_n \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \subsetneq
199	  \mathfrak{p}$). The transport is mechanised by the Mathlib lemmas
200	  \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} (coheight is invariant under order
201	  isomorphisms) and \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} (height in the order-dual
202	  equals coheight in the original order), applied to the order-dual
203	  identification \(\Spec R \simeq_o (\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R)^{\operatorname{op}}\)
204	  furnished by \texttt{spec\_le\_iff}.
205	\end{proof}
206	
207	\section{The coheight \(=\) Krull-dim-of-stalk bridge}
208	\label{sec:coheight_bridge}
209	
210	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
211	% L280--296 (the 5-step assembly recipe). Mathlib API surface consumed:
212	%   - \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
213	%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:271})
214	%   - \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}, \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
215	%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:806--813})
216	%   - \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
217	%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:341--348})
218	%   - \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight}
219	%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:45})
220	%   - \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}, \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
221	
222	\begin{theorem}
223	
224	\leanok
225	  [Coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge for a scheme point]
226	  \label{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
227	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
228	  
229	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(z \in X\) be any point. Then
230	  \[
231	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z),
232	  \]
233	  i.e.\ the Krull dimension of the stalk at \(z\) equals the coheight of \(z\) in
234	  the underlying topological space of \(X\) (with respect to the specialisation
235	  preorder).
236	\end{theorem}
237	
238	\begin{proof}
239	  
240	  \leanok
241	  The proof proceeds in five steps, assembling Mathlib API rather than
242	  arguing from a single textbook reference.
243	
244	  \emph{Step 1: pick an affine open neighbourhood of \(z\).} By
245	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
246	  applied to the open set \(X\) itself, there exists an affine open
247	  \(U \subseteq X\) with \(z \in U\). Write \(\langle z, \, z \in U \rangle \in U\)
248	  for \(z\) viewed as a point of the subscheme \(U\).
249	
250	  \emph{Step 2: name the prime corresponding to \(z\) inside \(U\).} Since
251	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)\) via the affine equivalence
252	  \(U \simeq \Spec R\) (with \(R := \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)\)), the point
253	  \(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\) corresponds to a prime ideal $\mathfrak{p}
254	  \subseteq R$. In Mathlib this prime is named
255	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}\,\(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\).
256	
257	  \emph{Step 3: the stalk is the localisation of \(\Gamma(U)\) at
258	  \(\mathfrak{p}\).} The classical affine-stalk identification says
259	  \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z} \cong R_{\mathfrak{p}}\). In Mathlib this is the
260	  instance \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}, which witnesses
261	  \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\) as a localisation of \(R\) at \(\mathfrak{p}\) in the
262	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} sense.
263	
264	  \emph{Step 4: Krull dim of the localisation equals the height of
265	  \(\mathfrak{p}\).} The Mathlib lemma
266	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} gives
267	  \[
268	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\;
269	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R_{\mathfrak{p}}) \;=\;
270	    \operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p}),
271	  \]
272	  where \(\operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p})\) is the prime-ideal height in
273	  \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\). By
274	  \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight} this matches the
275	  \(\operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}\) used in
276	  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}.
277	
278	  \emph{Step 5: lift back from \(U\) to \(X\).} By
279	  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum} applied to \(R\) and the
280	  affine identification \(U \simeq \Spec R\),
281	  \[
282	    \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(\mathfrak{p}) \;=\;
283	    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(\langle\text{image of } z\rangle) \;=\;
284	    \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr).
285	  \]
286	  Then \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding} applied to the open
287	  \(U \subseteq X\) promotes the right-hand side back to
288	  \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z)\). Concatenating the equalities from
289	  Steps~4--5 gives the conclusion.
290	\end{proof}
291	
292	\section{Codim-1 specialisation: synthesising \texorpdfstring{\(\operatorname{KrullDimLE} 1\)}{KrullDimLE 1}}
293	\label{sec:coheight_le_one_instance}
294	
295	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2
296	% recipe scaffold L298--307 + Decision 2 hreg\_dim refactor sketch
297	% L319--340. This block is the consumer-facing wrapper of the bridge
298	% \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} for the codim-\(1\) case used in
299	% \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and
300	% \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}.
301	
302	\begin{lemma}
303	
304	\leanok
305	  [Coheight \(= 1\) forces stalk \(\operatorname{Krull\,dim} \leq 1\)]
306	  \label{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
308	  
309	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(z \in X\) be a point with
310	  \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\). Then the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\)
311	  has Krull dimension at most \(1\), equivalently $\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,
312	  \mathcal{O}_{X,z}$ holds.
313	\end{lemma}
314	
315	\begin{proof}
316	  
317	  \leanok
318	  By \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight},
319	  \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\).
320	  The Mathlib predicate \(\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,R\) unfolds to
321	  \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R) \leq 1\), so the inequality \(1 \leq 1\)
322	  discharges the goal.
323	\end{proof}

325	\section{Consumer wiring and the Stacks 00TT carve-out}
326	\label{sec:coheight_consumers}
327	
328	\paragraph{Consumer 1: the \texttt{hreg\_dim} refactor in
329	\texorpdfstring{\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}}{the codim-1 extension chapter}.}
330	The private helper
331	\texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot}
332	inside that chapter's Lean target currently carries an internal
333	\texttt{sorry} of the form
334	\[
335	  \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\;\wedge\;
336	  \operatorname{ringKrullDim}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z} = 1.
337	\]
338	After this chapter's
339	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} (and its underlying
340	\cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}) land, the conjunction splits into
341	two independent goals:
342	\begin{itemize}
343	  \item The right conjunct \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1\)
344	    is discharged by
345	    \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\) (the standing codim-\(1\) hypothesis
346	    on \(z\)) plus \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}; the antisymmetric
347	    pair \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim} \leq 1\) (\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one})
348	    and \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim} \geq 1\) (also from the bridge, since
349	    \(1 \leq 1\)) gives equality.
350	  \item The left conjunct \(\operatorname{IsRegularLocalRing}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\)
351	    is the genuinely-irreducible \emph{Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}} statement
352	    ``smooth ring map over a field forces regular stalks''. It is a separate,
353	    out-of-scope sub-project (Mathlib does \emph{not} ship the implication
354	    \texttt{Algebra.Smooth k A \(\to\) IsRegularLocalRing \(A_{\mathfrak{p}}\)} at
355	    \texttt{b80f227}; the project-side build is \(\sim 200\)--\(300\)~LOC of
356	    cotangent-space and pure-dimension machinery, not packaged in this
357	    chapter).
358	\end{itemize}
359	Thus the present chapter \emph{halves} the \texttt{hreg\_dim} obligation in
360	\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}: one conjunct closes via the bridge,
361	one remains as a named Mathlib gap.
362	
363	\paragraph{Consumer 2: the \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} threading
364	hygiene in \texorpdfstring{\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}}{the Weil-divisor chapter}.}
365	The order-at-a-prime-divisor map currently carries an explicit instance
366	argument
367	\[
368	  [\,\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,(X.\text{presheaf.stalk}\,Y.\text{point})\,]
369	\]
370	at every use site. Because \(Y\) is a prime divisor of \(X\) (so
371	\(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(Y.\text{point}) = 1\) by the definition of a prime
372	divisor as a codim-\(1\) irreducible closed subset),
373	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} synthesises the instance
374	automatically from the codim-\(1\) hypothesis on \(Y\), so the explicit instance
375	argument can be dropped from \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order}'s signature.
376	
377	\paragraph{Consumer 3: future codim-\(1\) infrastructure.} Any subsequent
378	project lemma that needs to discharge ``the stalk at a codim-\(1\) point has
379	Krull dim \(\leq 1\)'' --- the implicit hypothesis behind every DVR-at-codim-\(1\)
380	argument (cf.\ \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} of
381	\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}) --- now has a one-liner consequence.
382	The instance form
383	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} is the right shape for
384	typeclass-search to find it without ceremony.

1092	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-\(\geq 2\) extension}
1093	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
1094	
1095	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
1096	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-\(1\)
1097	point.
1098	
1099	\begin{theorem}
1100	
1101	
1102	\leanok
1103	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-\(\geq 2\) extension to a complete target]
1104	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
1105	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
1106	  
1107	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
1108	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
1109	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
1110	  % \dashrightarrow W\( from a normal variety \)V\( to a complete variety \)W$ is defined
1111	  % on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension
1112	  % \(\geq 2\).}"
1113	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
1114	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety over
1115	  \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the
1116	  indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
1117	  \(\geq 2\) in \(X\); equivalently, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
1118	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
1119	
1120	  Specialising further: if in addition \(f\) \emph{is} codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
1121	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in \(Z(f)\)), then
1122	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\) and \(f\) extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
1123	  \to Y\(. The extension is unique because \)X\( is reduced and \)\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
1124	  in \(X\).
1125	\end{theorem}

1136	% \to U\( are isomorphisms. Therefore, \)Z \to V$ is a surjective map from a complete

1138	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by

1143	% \smallsetminus U\( has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor \)Z\( in \)V
1144	% \smallsetminus U\(. Because \)V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
1145	% valuation ring \(\mathcal O_Z\) with field of fractions \(k(V)\). The map \(\varphi\)
1146	% defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W\), which the
1147	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
1148	% morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W\). This implies that \(\varphi\)
1149	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets \(Z\) in a nonempty set,
1150	% which is a contradiction."
1151	\begin{proof}
1152	  
1153	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
1154	
1155	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-\(1\) components.} Suppose for contradiction that \(Z(f)\)
1156	  contains a prime divisor \(W \subseteq X\) (i.e.\ a codim-\(1\) irreducible component).
1157	  Let \(\eta \in W\) be its generic point, so \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). By
1158	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
1159	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\). Any representative
1160	  \((U, \varphi_U) \in f\) has \(U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset\) and so determines
1161	  a \(\bar k\)-algebra map from the function field \(K(Y)\) of \(Y\) to \(K(X)\): explicitly,
1162	  \(\varphi_U\) defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y\) (since
1163	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))\) is the generic point of \(X\)). By the valuative criterion
1164	  of properness applied to the complete variety \(Y\)
1165	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
1166	  extends to a morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y\). Standard
1167	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(\eta\) in
1168	  \(X\) on which \(f\) has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
1169	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)\(, contradicting \)\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)\(. Hence no codim-\)1$
1170	  component of \(X\) lies in \(Z(f)\), i.e.\ \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\).
1171	
1172	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-\(\geq 2\) points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
1173	  \(\Rightarrow\) extension" half).} Suppose now that \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)
1174	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) under the additional input
1175	  that ``\(f\) is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
1176	  point \(x \in Z(f)\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2\). By
1177	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
1178	  \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\), we have
1179	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\). The sheaf
1180	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on the punctured neighbourhood \(V \smallsetminus \{x\}\)
1181	  (any small affine neighbourhood \(V \ni x\)) has \(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\)
1182	  and \(H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) (the depth-\(\geq 2\) characterisation of
1183	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
1184	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of \(Y\) around the
1185	  closure \(\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}\) along \(f\); the resulting sections of
1186	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) extend uniquely to sections on \(V\),
1187	  yielding a regular representative of \(f\) on \(V\). Hence \(x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\),
1188	  contradicting \(x \in Z(f)\). Therefore \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) and \(f\) extends to a
1189	  regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\).
1190	
1191	  Uniqueness of \(\widetilde f\) is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
1192	  principle: two morphisms \(X \to Y\) that agree on the dense open \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\)
1193	  agree on all of \(X\).
1194	\end{proof}
1195	
1196	\begin{remark}
1197	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
1198	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
1199	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
1200	  Step~2 uses the depth-\(\geq 2\) extension property at a codim-\(\geq 2\) regular
1201	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
1202	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension \(d\)
1203	  \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(d\)). For our application the source \(X\) is a
1204	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
1205	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
1206	
1207	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
1208	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), the codim-\(\geq 2\) closed points of
1209	  \(X\) are exactly the closed points of \(X\) (because \(\dim X = 2\)), and at each such
1210	  point \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(2\), hence Cohen--Macaulay of
1211	  depth \(2\). The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
1212	  symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) along the Abel--Jacobi
1213	  birational equivalence to \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
1214	\end{remark}

1216	\section{Milne Lemma 3.3: indeterminacy locus of a map to a group variety is codim 1}
1217	\label{sec:milne_lem33}
1218	
1219	The second input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is structural: when the target \(Y\) is a group
1220	variety, the indeterminacy locus is either empty or of \emph{pure} codimension \(1\).
1221	Combined with \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s ``codimension \(\geq 2\)'' conclusion,
1222	this forces the locus to be empty.

1242	  \(\bar k\) (a smooth group scheme of finite type), and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow
