467	\subsection*{The main \(\chi\)-identity}
468	
469	\begin{theorem}
470	
471	
472	\leanok
473	[Euler-characteristic identity for \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)]
474	  \label{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}
475	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus}
476	  
477	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Theorem~1.3 (the reduction inside the
478	  % proof: ``Thus we have to show that for any \(D\), $\chi(\mathscr L(D))
479	  % = \deg D + 1 - g$'') (read from
480	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
481	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
482	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
483	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
484	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
485	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
486	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
487	  % is the Euler characteristic
488	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
489	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295.}
490	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
491	  \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). Then
492	  \[
493	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g.
494	  \]
495	\end{theorem}
496	
497	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "First we consider the case \(D = 0\). Then our
498	% formula says
499	% \(\dim H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = 1 - g\),
500	% because \(H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) = k\) for any projective variety
501	% (I, 3.4), and \(\dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = g\) by (1.1).
502	% Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point. We will show
503	% that the formula is true for \(D\) if and only if it is true for
504	% \(D + P\). Since any divisor can be reached from \(0\) in a finite number
505	% of steps by adding or subtracting a point each time, this will show
506	% the result holds for all \(D\).
507	% We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its structure sheaf is
508	% the skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\), which we denote by
509	% \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\) by (II, 6.18).
510	% Therefore we have an exact sequence
511	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
512	% Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
513	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
514	% (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
515	% does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\)). Now the Euler characteristic is
516	% additive on short exact sequences (III, Ex. 5.1), and $\chi(k(P))
517	% = 1$, so we have
518	% \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\).
519	% On the other hand, \(\deg D + P = \deg D + 1\), so our formula is true
520	% for \(D\) if and only if it is true for \(D + P\), as required."
521	\begin{proof}
522	  \leanok
523	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:l_invariant,
524	    lem:euler_char_shortExact_add, lem:euler_char_iso,
525	    lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
526	
527	  The proof is by induction on the structure of \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as the free
528	  abelian group on closed points (REF,
529	  REF). Both sides of the asserted
530	  equation are additive in \(D\) (the right-hand side by linearity of
531	  \(\deg\), REF; the left-hand side by the
532	  inductive step proved below), so it suffices to verify the formula
533	  for \(D = 0\) and to show that it transports across the elementary
534	  modification \(D \mapsto D + [P]\) at any closed point \(P \in C\).
535	
536	  \emph{Base case \(D = 0\).} The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(0)\) is the
537	  structure sheaf \(\mathcal O_C\), and the formula
538	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\) reads
539	  \[
540	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
541	    - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)
542	    \;=\; 1 - g.
543	  \]
544	  The second term is the definition of the genus,
545	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g\) (REF). The
546	  first term is \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\): \(C\) is a
547	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over the algebraically
548	  closed field \(\bar k\), so its connected components agree with its
549	  geometrically connected components, and $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
550	  \cong \bar k\( as \)\bar k$-vector spaces (the Hartshorne~I.3.4
551	  statement that ``\(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X) = k\) for any projective
552	  variety'' over an algebraically closed field; the Mathlib home is
553	  the project's \(H^0\)-bridge in
554	  \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}). Substituting these
555	  two values gives \(1 - g = 1 - g\), as required.
556	
557	  \emph{Inductive step (Hartshorne's ``\(D \leftrightsquigarrow D + P\)''
558	  step).} Let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. We follow Hartshorne's
559	  argument: view \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(C\); its structure
560	  sheaf is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\) supported at \(P\),
561	  and its sheaf of ideals is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\)
562	  (the locally principal ideal sheaf cutting out \(P\) in \(C\)). Hence
563	  the short exact sequence
564	  \[
565	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
566	  \]
567	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\) (Hartshorne~II.6.18). Tensoring with the
568	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) (a locally free
569	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module of rank \(1\), so tensoring preserves exactness
570	  and leaves the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged) yields
571	  \[
572	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(D)
573	    \to \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
574	    \to k(P) \to 0.
575	  \]
576	  (Strictly: tensoring \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\) with
577	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) produces a sheaf isomorphic — but not
578	  syntactically equal — to \(\mathcal O_C(D)\), and the skyscraper
579	  factor \(k(P)\) is preserved up to isomorphism because
580	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is locally free of rank \(1\). We transport
581	  along this isomorphism via iso-invariance of \(\chi\),
582	  REF.)
583	  Additivity of \(\chi\) on the short exact sequence
584	  (REF, the project-side
585	  factoring of the ``Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
586	  exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1)'' input of Hartshorne~IV.1.3) gives
587	  \[
588	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]))
589	    \;=\;
590	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + \chi(k(P)).
591	  \]
592	  The skyscraper-sheaf input
593	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) is the standalone substrate lemma
594	  REF (factored from the
595	  same Hartshorne~IV.1.3 line); its proof splits into the
596	  H\(^0\) identification \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) and the
597	  H\(^1\) vanishing \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\), pursued
598	  independently by the prover (see the lemma's two-paragraph proof
599	  body). Therefore
600	  \[
601	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1.
602	  \]
603	  On the other hand, \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
604	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), so by
605	  REF
606	  \[
607	    \deg(D + [P]) + 1 - g \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g + 1.
608	  \]
609	  Comparing the two displayed identities, the formula
610	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) holds for \(D\) if and only
611	  if it holds for \(D + [P]\). Since the closed-point divisors \([P]\)
612	  generate \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as a free abelian group, the formula propagates
613	  from \(D = 0\) to every \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) by induction on the (signed)
614	  number of generators in the expression of \(D\).
615	
616	  \emph{Lean reference note on the three substrate lemmas.} The
617	  inductive step of this proof consumes three substantive substrate
618	  lemmas, each pinned to its own Lean declaration in
619	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}: the additivity
620	  REF (\(\chi\) on a short
621	  exact sequence, gated on the project-side
622	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured Ext-LES carrier + Grothendieck
623	  vanishing + six-term rank-counting), the iso-invariance
624	  REF (\(\chi\) on
625	  isomorphism classes, axiom-clean against Mathlib's
626	  \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; closed iter-186), and the skyscraper
627	  identity REF
628	  (\(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), splitting into an H\(^0\) half closed against
629	  the project's existing \(H^0\)-bridge and an H\(^1\) half gated on
630	  Mathlib flasque-cohomology — see the lemma's annotated proof body).
631	  Mathlib \texttt{b80f227} provides the Euler-characteristic API
632	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.EulerCharacteristic}
633	  (\texttt{HomologicalComplex.eulerChar},
634	  \texttt{GradedObject.eulerChar}) but does \emph{not} ship the
635	  specific additivity statement
636	  \texttt{CategoryTheory.ShortExact.eulerChar\_additive} at the
637	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf-cohomology level the project
638	  consumes; the additivity lemma is the project-side bridge.
639	\end{proof}
640	
641	\section{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero}
642	
643	\begin{theorem}
644	
645	
646	\leanok
647	[Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero]
648	  \label{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}
649	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero}
650	  
651	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Example~1.3.5 (genus-\(0\) specialisation
652	  % of Riemann--Roch; the curve-is-rational consequence) (read from
653	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
654	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
655	  % if it is birational to \(\mathbf P^1\) (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in
656	  % this chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve
657	  % \(X\) is rational if and only if \(X \cong \mathbf P^1\) (I, 6.12). Now
658	  % using (1.3) we can show that \(X\) is rational if and only if \(g = 0\).
659	  % We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so
660	  % conversely suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two
661	  % distinct points of \(X\) and apply Riemann--Roch to the divisor
662	  % \(D = P - Q\). Since \(\deg K - D = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have
663	  % \(l(K - D) = 0\), and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of
664	  % degree 0, so by (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words $P \sim
665	  % Q\(. But this implies \)X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
666	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
667	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
668	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) with
669	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). Then
670	  \[
671	    \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
672	  \]
673	\end{theorem}
674	
675	\begin{proof}
676	  \leanok
677	  
678	  Specialising
679	  REF to the genus-\(0\)
680	  hypothesis \(g = 0\) yields
681	  \[
682	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
683	  \]
684	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF and the definition
685	  \(\ell(D) = \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\)
686	  (REF), this rewrites as
687	  \[
688	    \ell(D) - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
689	    \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
690	  \]
691	  Under the standing genus-\(0\) hypothesis and the assumption
692	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\), the higher-cohomology term vanishes:
693	  \[
694	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; 0
695	    \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0.
696	  \]
697	  Substituting yields \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\), as required.
698	
699	  \emph{The \(H^1\)-vanishing input.} On a smooth proper geometrically
700	  irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), the vanishing
701	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) is the Hartshorne
702	  formulation Lemma~1.2 read covariantly: \(\ell(D) \neq 0\) forces
703	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\); equivalently, for a curve where every
704	  degree-\(0\)-or-higher invertible sheaf is generated by global
705	  sections after a single point-twist, \(H^1\) of the twist must vanish
706	  by the long exact sequence of the skyscraper extension at any
707	  closed point. In the chain of sub-build chapters, this \(H^1\)
708	  vanishing is the natural content of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
709	  \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added), where the line
710	  bundle \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) and its cohomology are constructed; once
711	  \texttt{RR.3} is closed, the present theorem inherits the
712	  vanishing as an instance, and the proof closes verbatim as
713	  displayed. Pending \texttt{RR.3}, the Lean signature
714	  \texttt{l\_eq\_degree\_plus\_one\_of\_genus\_zero} threads the
715	  \(H^1\)-vanishing hypothesis explicitly as a named premise; the
716	  iteration that closes \texttt{RR.3} then drops the premise in a
717	  follow-up signature simplification.
718	
719	  \emph{Sub-build note for \texttt{RR.4}.} The downstream consumer of
720	  this theorem is the genus-\(0\) classification
721	  REF of
722	  \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}: with \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\)
723	  and the degree-zero principal-divisor identity
724	  (REF), Hartshorne's Example~1.3.5 chain
725	  produces a degree-\(1\) morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) via
726	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} applied to the two-dimensional
727	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\), which is then upgraded to an
728	  isomorphism in \texttt{RR.4}.
729	\end{proof}
730	
731	\section{Out of scope}
732	
733	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.2}
734	and are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
735	
736	\begin{itemize}
737	  \item The general-\(g\) Riemann--Roch theorem
738	    \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) (Hartshorne~IV.1.3 in
739	    its full form). This requires the canonical divisor \(K\), the
740	    canonical sheaf \(\Omega^1_{C / \bar k} = \omega_C\), and
741	    Serre duality $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes
742	    \mathcal L^{-1})^*$. Mathlib has none of these for schemes, and
743	    the genus-\(0\) critical path does not require them; the general
744	    statement is queued for a follow-up chapter (post-RR-bridge).
745	  \item The construction of the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)
746	    from a Weil divisor \(D\), the linear-equivalence isomorphism
747	    \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when \(D \sim D'\), and
748	    the computation of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \cong \bar k \cdot
749	    1 \oplus \bar k \cdot t$ (\emph{a non-constant rational function
750	    \(t\) exists}) live in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
751	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added). Inside the proof
752	    of REF the dependency on
753	    \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is reduced to its \(H^0\) functor and the short
754	    exact sequence of the closed point.
755	  \item The vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0\) for
756	    \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) on a genus-\(0\) curve is consumed as a named
757	    premise by REF; its closure is
758	    part of \texttt{RR.3} (where the cohomology of
759	    \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(d)\) is computed) and is recovered for
760	    a general smooth proper genus-\(0\) curve via the isomorphism
761	    \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\) of \texttt{RR.4} or directly via the
762	    base-change argument inside \texttt{RR.3}.
763	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
764	    curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g = 0\) is isomorphic to
765	    \(\mathbb P^1\)'' (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5 proper) is the
766	    headline of \texttt{RR.4} (sibling chapter
767	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added). The
768	    present chapter supplies one of its two inputs (the $\ell(D) =
769	    \deg(D) + 1\( identity); the other input is the degree-\)0$
770	    principal-divisor identity REF from
771	    \texttt{RR.1}.
772	  \item Higher-cohomology bookkeeping (\(\chi\) as an alternating sum
773	    over all \(i \geq 0\), the Grothendieck vanishing
774	    \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i > \dim C = 1\)) is not separately
775	    formalised here; the chapter consumes only the two-term form
776	    \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\) which is the standing
777	    curve specialisation.
778	\end{itemize}

3	\chapter{The invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of a Weil divisor (RR.2\(_\ast\))}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OcOfD}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II~\S6, pp.~144--145
7	% (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\); Proposition 6.13; Remark 6.17.1;
8	% Proposition 6.18) and IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive-step short exact
9	% sequence). Read directly from
10	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162 and
11	% 313, by rendering the scanned page images and transcribing the
12	% statements character-by-character (the PDF has no text layer). Stacks
13	% pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (Weil divisors), 0AUW
14	% (the sheaf \(\mathcal O_X(D)\) from a Weil divisor), 0BE3 (degree-zero of
15	% a principal divisor on a complete nonsingular curve).
16	
17	\section{Setup and motivation}
18	
19	This chapter is a satellite of \texttt{RR.2}
20	(REF); together with \texttt{RR.1}
21	(REF), \texttt{RR.3}
22	(REF), and \texttt{RR.4}
23	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex}) it forms the four-stage
24	sub-build closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
25	\[
26	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
27	  \,\colon\,
28	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
29	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
30	\]
31	The Hartshorne~IV.1.3.5 chain consumed by REF
32	of \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex} relies on the invertible-sheaf
33	functor
34	\[
35	  \mathcal O_C(-) \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}(C) \;\longrightarrow\;
36	  \mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})
37	\]
38	sending a Weil divisor \(D = \sum_i n_i [P_i]\) to its associated invertible
39	sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) on \(C\). The Lean entry-point
40	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} of
41	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean} (line~168) currently
42	encodes this functor as a typed-\texttt{sorry} \texttt{noncomputable def}
43	with no chapter pinning. This chapter supplies the missing pinning by
44	blueprinting the Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}
45	(to be created in iter-183), where the body of \texttt{sheafOf} and its
46	three immediate corollaries will live.
47	
48	\paragraph{Why this chapter is gated to \texttt{RR.2}.} The
49	\(\chi\)-identity proof of REF
50	in \texttt{RR.2} is an induction on the Weil divisor \(D\) via the
51	``\(D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]\)'' step of Hartshorne~IV.1.3. That step
52	intrinsically references the body of \texttt{sheafOf}: it asks for the
53	short exact sequence
54	\[
55	  0 \to \mathcal O_C(D) \to \mathcal O_C(D + [Y]) \to k(Y)^{[\text{at } Y]}
56	  \to 0
57	\]
58	to be exact in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\), which is not a statement on the
59	\emph{signature} of \texttt{sheafOf} but on its \emph{body}. The iter-181
60	Lane H factoring of \texttt{RR.2} consequently
61	landed two helper sorries
62	(\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_zero},
63	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_succ}) that are
64	\emph{intrinsically gated} on the body of \texttt{sheafOf}. Until this
65	chapter pins the body, those helpers remain vacuous regardless of how
66	much \(\chi\)-machinery \texttt{RR.2} adds on top.
67	
68	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.3}.} The
69	existing \texttt{RR.3} chapter (REF) constructs
70	the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) associated to a \emph{closed point}
71	\(P \in C\) directly, bypassing the Weil-divisor packaging. The
72	REF below makes this bypass mathematically
73	coherent by exhibiting \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) at \(D = [P]\) as canonically
74	isomorphic to REF. With this isomorphism in
75	hand, \texttt{RR.3}'s downstream consumption of
76	\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) via the
77	\(\chi\)-identity routes through \texttt{sheafOf} cleanly.
78	
79	\paragraph{Why this chapter is the entry-point for \texttt{RR.4}.} The
80	two pins consumed by the \texttt{RR.4} chapter
81	(\texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex})
82	that translate Riemann--Roch outputs into the function-field/degree
83	language of \(C\) both reference \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) for general \(D\) (not
84	just \(D = [P]\)): the function-field-degree bridge takes a divisor
85	\(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) and reads off \(\deg(D)\) from the
86	\texttt{sheafOf}-associated invertible sheaf, while the
87	Weil-divisor / line-bundle correspondence is the substance of
88	Hartshorne's Proposition~6.15 (which the present chapter pins via
89	REF).
90	
91	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
92	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
93	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
94	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean}) carry the same
95	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
96	\begin{quote}
97	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
98	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
99	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
100	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
101	\end{quote}
102	The Weil divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) is the one defined in
103	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex}; the
104	prime-divisor data type is REF; the order
105	\(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor \(Q\) is REF; the
106	principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a non-zero rational function is
107	REF; the closed-point divisor \([P]\) is
108	REF. The line bundle at a closed point
109	already constructed in \texttt{RR.3} is
110	REF, and the present chapter's
111	REF makes the comparison
112	\(\mathcal O_C([P]) = \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}\) explicit.
113	
114	\paragraph{Route alignment with iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom}
115	analogy.} The iter-182 api-alignment analogy
116	\texttt{analogies/ocofp-sheaf-internalhom.md} surveyed three candidate
117	Mathlib paths for the closed-point specialisation \(\mathcal O_C([P])\):
118	(i)~\texttt{Sheaf-internal-Hom} on \(\mathbf{SheafOfModules}\) (rejected:
119	no \texttt{IdealSheafData \(\to\) SheafOfModules} realiser in Mathlib,
120	no \(\mathbf{SheafOfModules}\) dual functor in Mathlib);
121	(ii)~the abstract dual $\mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
122	\mathcal O_C)$ in a new \texttt{IdealSheafDual.lean} (rejected: high
123	LOC cost in exchange for a single sheaf the project can build directly);
124	(iii)~Hartshorne's \emph{subsheaf-of-\(\mathcal K_C\)} description (accepted).
125	The present chapter adopts route~(iii) for the general
126	\texttt{sheafOf \(D\)} as well, and REF verifies
127	that the general construction at \(D = [P]\) recovers the closed-point
128	specialisation of \texttt{RR.3} on the nose. The subsheaf-of-\(\mathcal K_C\)
129	recipe is mathematically Hartshorne's original (II~\S6, the definition of
130	\(\mathscr L(D)\)) and was equally available for the closed-point case; the
131	project's choice to use it both at the general \(D\) and at the \(D = [P]\)
132	specialisation is what makes the comparison
133	\(\mathcal O_C([P]) \cong \mathcal O_C(D)|_{D = [P]}\) a direct unfolding
134	rather than a non-trivial isomorphism.
135	
136	\section{The invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)}
137	
138	\begin{definition}\leanok
139	[Invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of a Weil divisor]
140	  \label{def:sheafOf}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf}
142	  
143	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
144	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor on a scheme),
145	  % and Remark~6.17.1, Proposition~6.18 + Proposition~6.15, p.~145
146	  % (Cartier \(\leftrightarrow\) Weil correspondence on an integral
147	  % locally factorial scheme; identification of \(\mathscr L(D)\) as a
148	  % subsheaf of the constant sheaf at the function field \(K\)). Read from
149	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162,
150	  % transcribed character-by-character from rendered scanned page
151	  % images (the PDF has no text layer).
152	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
153	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
154	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
155	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
156	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
157	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), so \(f_i^{-1}\) and
158	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
159	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
160	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.15. \emph{If \(X\) is an integral
161	  % scheme, the homomorphism \(\mathrm{CaCl}\, X \to \mathrm{Pic}\, X\) of
162	  % (6.14) is an isomorphism.} Proof. We have only to show that every
163	  % invertible sheaf is isomorphic to a subsheaf of \(\mathscr K\), which
164	  % in this case is the constant sheaf \(K\), where \(K\) is the function
165	  % field of \(X\)."
166	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Remark 6.17.1. Clearly this gives a 1-1
167	  % correspondence between effective Cartier divisors on \(X\) and
168	  % \emph{locally principal} closed subschemes \(Y\), i.e., subschemes
169	  % whose sheaf of ideals is locally generated by a single element.
170	  % Note also that if \(X\) is an integral separated noetherian locally
171	  % factorial scheme, so that the Cartier divisors correspond to Weil
172	  % divisors by (6.11), then the effective Cartier divisors correspond
173	  % exactly to the effective Weil divisors."
174	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (the definition of
175	  \(\mathscr L(D)\)), Proposition~6.15 (the constant-sheaf-at-\(K\)
176	  identification on an integral scheme), and Remark~6.17.1 (Weil
177	  divisors and Cartier divisors agree on an integral separated
178	  noetherian locally factorial scheme).}
179	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
180	  \(\bar k\) and let \(D = \sum_{Q} n_Q [Q] \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor
181	  on \(C\), with the formal sum indexed by prime divisors \(Q\) of \(C\) and
182	  supported on a finite set \(\{Q : n_Q \neq 0\}\)
183	  (REF). The \emph{invertible sheaf
184	  \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\)}, also written
185	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) := \mathscr L(D)\) in Hartshorne's notation, is the
186	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the constant sheaf
187	  \(\mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\) (Proposition~6.15: on an integral scheme the
188	  sheaf of total quotient rings is the constant sheaf at the function
189	  field) defined section-wise by
190	  \[
191	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(D)\bigr) \;:=\;
192	    \{\, 0 \,\} \;\cup\;
193	    \bigl\{\, f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\;
194	      \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; -\,n_Q \text{ for every prime divisor }
195	      Q \text{ of } C \text{ with } Q \in U \bigr\}
196	  \]
197	  for each open \(U \subseteq C\). Restriction along \(V \hookrightarrow U\)
198	  is the identity on the underlying carrier \(K(C)\), which preserves
199	  membership (a smaller open imposes \emph{fewer} order constraints, so
200	  the constraint set on \(U\) embeds into the constraint set on \(V\)). The
201	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on
202	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) is restricted from the
203	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on \(K(C)\) (a \(\bar k\)-scalar multiple of a
204	  rational function has the same order at every prime divisor).
205	
206	  \paragraph{Equivalence with the Cartier-divisor description.} The
207	  formal sum \(D = \sum_Q n_Q [Q]\) packages, on a curve where prime
208	  divisors are exactly closed points and the local rings at prime
209	  divisors are DVRs (smoothness \(+\) dimension~\(1\)), the Cartier-divisor
210	  data \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) of Hartshorne's definition: locally on a small
211	  open \(U_i\) containing exactly one prime divisor \(Q\) in the support of
212	  \(D\), pick a uniformiser \(\pi_Q \in K(C)\) at \(Q\) and set
213	  \(f_i := \pi_Q^{n_Q}\); on opens disjoint from the support of \(D\), set
214	  \(f_i := 1\). The transitions on overlaps \(U_i \cap U_j\) are units in
215	  \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}^{\times}\) for each prime \(Q\) in the overlap (any
216	  two uniformisers differ by a unit, by the DVR structure), so the
217	  \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) define a Cartier divisor whose Weil-divisor image
218	  under Hartshorne's (6.11) is the original \(D\). The
219	  ``\(f_i^{-1}\)-generated'' subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\) from Hartshorne's
220	  definition then coincides with the section-wise description above:
221	  the local generator \(f_i^{-1} = \pi_Q^{-n_Q}\) has order \(-n_Q\) at \(Q\),
222	  so the local sections are exactly \(f \in K(C)\) with
223	  \(\ord_Q(f \cdot \pi_Q^{n_Q}) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q\), on
224	  \(U_i\).
225	
226	  Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) (quoted in
227	  REF of REF)
228	  then guarantees that the resulting subsheaf $\mathcal O_C(D) \subset
229	  \mathcal K_C\( is an invertible sheaf on \)C$, independently of the
230	  choice of uniformisers \(\pi_Q\) (any two choices differ by a unit, so
231	  they generate the same \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule).
232	
233	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
234	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} takes a Weil
235	  divisor \(D : C.\text{left.WeilDivisor}\) and returns a
236	  \(\texttt{Sheaf}~J~(\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\) on
237	  \(C.\text{left.toTopCat}\), where
238	  \(J = \texttt{Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat}\) is the
239	  standard topology on the topological space underlying \(C\). The
240	  closed-point specialisation \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) is
241	  REF of
242	  REF; their agreement at \(D = [P]\) is
243	  REF below. The construction follows
244	  the iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom} recipe in
245	  \texttt{analogies/}: presheaf-by-hand via per-open
246	  \(\bar k\)-submodules of the function field, sheaf property reduced to
247	  the locality of the order conditions at the stalks of the prime
248	  divisors. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
249	\end{definition}

252	
253	\begin{lemma}\leanok
254	[Sheaf of the zero divisor is the structure sheaf]
255	  \label{lem:sheafOf_zero}
256	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_zero}
257	  
258	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
259	  \(\bar k\). Then the invertible sheaf associated to the zero divisor
260	  \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is canonically the structure sheaf:
261	  \[
262	    \mathcal O_C(0) \;=\; \mathcal O_C
263	  \]
264	  as sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\), equivalently as
265	  objects in \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
266	\end{lemma}
267	
268	\begin{proof}
269	  
270	  At \(D = 0\) the coefficient \(n_Q = 0\) at every prime divisor \(Q\), so the
271	  section condition of REF reduces, on every open
272	  \(U \subseteq C\), to
273	  \[
274	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(0)\bigr)
275	    \;=\; \{ 0 \} \cup \{ f \in K(C)^{\times} \;|\;
276	      \ord_Q(f) \geq 0 \text{ for every prime divisor } Q \in U \}.
277	  \]
278	  By the local description of the structure sheaf on the integral
279	  scheme \(C\) (the regular functions on \(U\) are exactly the rational
280	  functions whose order is non-negative at every prime divisor of
281	  \(C\) landing in \(U\); this is the standard ``non-negative order \(=\)
282	  regular'' identification, e.g.\ Hartshorne~II~\S6 immediately before
283	  Proposition~6.11), the right-hand side is precisely
284	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)\). The restriction maps on both sides are
285	  the identity on \(K(C)\), so the equality of presheaves promotes to an
286	  equality of sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\).
287	\end{proof}
288	
289	\begin{lemma}\leanok
290	[Sheaf at a single closed point is the line bundle of
291	REF]
292	  \label{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint}
293	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_singlePoint}
294	  
295	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
296	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point, viewed as a Weil divisor
297	  \([P] \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) via REF. Then the
298	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) of REF agrees on
299	  the nose with the line bundle of the closed point
300	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) of REF:
301	  \[
302	    \mathcal O_C(D)\bigl|_{D = [P]}
303	    \;=\; \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}_{P}
304	  \]
305	  as sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\), equivalently as
306	  objects in \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
307	\end{lemma}
308	
309	\begin{proof}
310	  
311	  Unfold both definitions. On the \texttt{sheafOf} side, the formal sum
312	  \([P]\) has coefficients \(n_Q = 1\) if \(Q = P\) and \(n_Q = 0\) otherwise
313	  (where \(P\) is identified with the prime divisor it determines by
314	  REF). Thus
315	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) contains \(0\) and those
316	  \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) satisfying \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\) for every prime
317	  divisor \(Q \in U\) with \(Q \neq P\), plus \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\) if
318	  \(P \in U\).
319	
320	  On the REF side, the same conditions
321	  are exactly the ones extracted by
322	  REF of
323	  \texttt{RR.3}, at every open \(U\) (not only at the global section
324	  \(U = C\)): a rational function \(f \in K(C)\) is a section of
325	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) on \(U\) iff it lies in the structure sheaf at every
326	  \(Q \in U \setminus \{P\}\) and has at most a simple pole at \(P\) when
327	  \(P \in U\).
328	
329	  Restriction maps on both sides are the identity on \(K(C)\). The two
330	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\) are therefore equal
331	  section-by-section as subsets of \(K(C)\), and the equality propagates
332	  to an equality of sheaves of \(\mathcal O_C\)-modules. The
333	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on both sides is the restriction of the
334	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on \(K(C)\), so the identification is
335	  \(\bar k\)-linear on each section.
336	\end{proof}

338	\begin{lemma}\leanok
339	[Short exact sequence for \(D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]\)]
340	  \label{lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add}
341	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_ses_single_add}
342	  
343	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive step of the proof
344	  % of Theorem~1.3, ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any
345	  % point...''). Read from
346	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313,
347	  % transcribed character-by-character from the rendered scanned page
348	  % image (the PDF has no text layer).
349	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
350	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
351	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
352	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
353	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
354	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
355	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\).
356	  % (Since \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by
357	  % it does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\).)"
358	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
359	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
360	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
361	  \(\bar k\), let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor, and let \(P \in C\) be
362	  a closed point (equivalently, a prime divisor
363	  REF on a smooth curve). Then the inclusion
364	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) of
365	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\) fits into a short exact
366	  sequence
367	  \[
368	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
369	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
370	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
371	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
372	  \]
373	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\) (equivalently, in
374	  \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\)), where \(k(P)\) denotes the
375	  skyscraper sheaf at \(P\) with stalk \(\bar k\) (the residue field of \(P\)
376	  is \(\bar k\) since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and \(P\) is a
377	  \(\bar k\)-rational closed point).
378	\end{lemma}
379	
380	\begin{proof}
381	  
382	  Hartshorne's argument (IV.1.3, the inductive step quoted above)
383	  proceeds in two beats:
384	
385	  \emph{Beat 1 (the ideal-sheaf SES at \(P\)).} The closed-subscheme
386	  inclusion \(P \hookrightarrow C\) has structure sheaf
387	  \(\mathcal O_P \cong k(P)\) (a skyscraper at \(P\) with stalk \(\bar k\)),
388	  and Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.18 identifies its ideal sheaf as
389	  \(\mathcal I_P = \mathcal O_C(-[P])\) (the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
390	  \(\mathcal K_C\) generated by a local uniformiser \(\pi_P\) at \(P\) and by
391	  \(1\) away from \(P\), i.e.\ the locally-principal ideal of the locally
392	  principal closed subscheme \(P\)). This produces the structural short
393	  exact sequence
394	  \[
395	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P])
396	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C
397	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
398	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
399	  \]
400	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\).
401	
402	  \emph{Beat 2 (tensor with \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\)).} The line bundle
403	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is locally free of rank~\(1\)
404	  (Hartshorne~II.6.13(a), REF), so $- \otimes_{\mathcal O_C}
405	  \mathcal O_C(D + [P])\( is exact on \)\mathbf{Coh}(C)$ and preserves
406	  the short exact sequence of Beat~1:
407	  \[
408	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes
409	      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
410	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
411	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
412	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0.
413	  \]
414	  Three identifications collapse this to the claimed sequence:
415	  \begin{itemize}
416	    \item $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
417	      \mathcal O_C(D)$ (tensor product of line bundles is the
418	      \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\)-additive operation $D' + D'' \mapsto \mathcal O_C(D' +
419	      D'')$ via Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(b), which is in turn the
420	      ring-theoretic identity
421	      $\pi_P^{-1} \cdot \pi_P^{1 + n_P^{D}} \cdot \prod_{Q \neq P}
422	      \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}} = \prod_Q \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}}$ on the generating
423	      rational functions, where \(n_Q^{D}\) is the coefficient of \(Q\)
424	      in \(D\));
425	    \item $\mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
426	      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ (the structure sheaf is the tensor unit);
427	    \item \(k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong k(P)\) (tensoring a
428	      skyscraper at \(P\) by a locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf preserves the
429	      skyscraper; concretely, at the stalk over \(P\) the line bundle
430	      \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])_P\) is a free \(\mathcal O_{C,P}\)-module of
431	      rank one, and $k(P) \otimes_{\mathcal O_{C,P}} \mathcal O_{C,P}
432	      \cong k(P)\( is just \)k(P)\(; away from \)P$ the skyscraper is the
433	      zero sheaf, on which the tensor is the zero sheaf).
434	  \end{itemize}
435	  Substituting yields
436	  \[
437	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
438	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
439	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
440	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
441	  \]
442	  exact in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\), as required.
443	
444	  \emph{Compatibility with the structural inclusion.} The first map
445	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is the
446	  inclusion of subsheaves of \(\mathcal K_C\): at the level of sections,
447	  the constraint \(\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}\) of
448	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) implies the weaker constraint
449	  \(\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D} - 1\) of \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))\)
450	  when \(P \in U\) (constraints on \(Q \neq P\) are unchanged), and the map
451	  is identity on \(K(C)\) at the carrier level. The second map
452	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \twoheadrightarrow k(P)\) is the evaluation
453	  ``leading coefficient at the pole at \(P\)'' (i.e.\ the
454	  \(\pi_P^{-(n_P^{D} + 1)}\)-coefficient in the Laurent expansion of \(f\)
455	  at \(P\)), which is well-defined on \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))\)
456	  modulo the subgroup of sections with \(\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}\) ---
457	  i.e.\ modulo \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\).
458	\end{proof}

460	\section{Sheaf-property correctness}
461	
462	The construction of REF is presheaf-by-hand: a
463	per-open \(\bar k\)-submodule of \(K(C)\), with restriction along
464	\(V \hookrightarrow U\) given by the identity on \(K(C)\). This section
465	sketches the verifications that make the construction land in the
466	target category \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
467	
468	\paragraph{Functoriality of the carrier.} For \(V \subseteq U\), the
469	constraint set defining \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) is a \emph{subset}
470	of the constraint set defining \(\Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))\) (fewer
471	prime divisors to constrain at), so the identity on \(K(C)\) restricts
472	to a well-defined inclusion
473	\(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D)) \hookrightarrow \Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))\).
474	Composition \(W \subseteq V \subseteq U\) is again identity-on-\(K(C)\), so
475	strict functoriality holds.
476	
477	\paragraph{\(\bar k\)-linearity.} A \(\bar k\)-scalar \(\lambda \in \bar k\)
478	acts on \(K(C)\) by multiplication, and
479	\(\ord_Q(\lambda \cdot f) = \ord_Q(f)\) for \(\lambda \neq 0\)
480	(units have order zero); for \(\lambda = 0\), \(\lambda \cdot f = 0\) is
481	included by convention. Thus the constraint set is a
482	\(\bar k\)-submodule and the restriction maps are \(\bar k\)-linear.
483	
484	\paragraph{Sheaf property.} The order condition
485	``\(\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q\) for every prime divisor \(Q \in U\)'' is
486	\emph{stalk-local} at each \(Q\): it depends only on the germ of \(f\) at
487	the point of \(C\) underlying \(Q\), equivalently on the image of \(f\) in
488	the DVR \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}\). Consequently the sheaf property reduces
489	to two ingredients:
490	\begin{enumerate}
491	  \item \emph{The presheaf-of-sets underlying \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is a
492	    subpresheaf of the constant presheaf at \(K(C)\).} The constant
493	    presheaf at \(K(C)\) is a sheaf (every locally-constant section of a
494	    constant presheaf on a topological space is globally constant when
495	    the space is irreducible, which the topological space underlying an
496	    integral scheme is). A subpresheaf of a sheaf is itself a sheaf iff
497	    its membership condition is local; the order conditions are local
498	    at each prime divisor's underlying point, so the membership
499	    condition is local across an open cover and a section glued from
500	    locally-defined sections inherits the order condition at every
501	    prime divisor.
502	  \item \emph{The forget-functor bridge from
503	    \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\) to
504	    \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Set})\).} The categorical forget
505	    \(\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k} \to \mathbf{Set}\) creates limits, hence
506	    reflects the sheaf property: a presheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules is a
507	    sheaf iff its underlying presheaf of sets is. Combined with~(i),
508	    this delivers the sheaf property for the
509	    \(\bar k\)-module-valued \(\mathcal O_C(D)\).
510	\end{enumerate}
511	
512	\paragraph{Invertibility.} On any open \(U \subseteq C\) disjoint from
513	the support of \(D\), the constraint set is exactly the structure-sheaf
514	constraint \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\) for every prime divisor \(Q \in U\); hence
515	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U = \mathcal O_C|_U\), and the trivialisation
516	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \cong \mathcal O_C|_U\) is the identity. On a small
517	open \(U \subseteq C\) containing exactly one prime divisor \(Q\) from the
518	support of \(D\), the local generator \(\pi_Q^{-n_Q} \in K(C)\) trivialises
519	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U\) as a free rank-\(1\) \(\mathcal O_C|_U\)-module: a
520	section \(f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) satisfies $\ord_Q(f) \geq
521	-n_Q\( iff \)\pi_Q^{n_Q} \cdot f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)$, and the
522	``multiplication by \(\pi_Q^{n_Q}\)'' map is an
523	\(\mathcal O_C|_U\)-linear isomorphism
524	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal O_C|_U\). The two types
525	of trivialising opens cover \(C\) (any prime divisor lies in at most
526	finitely many supports across a covering, and the support of \(D\) is
527	finite); patching the trivialisations on overlaps amounts to changing
528	uniformiser, which is unit-times the original (by the DVR property), so
529	the cocycle is in \(\mathcal O_C^{\times}\) and the sheaf is invertible.
530	
531	The closure of (i)+(ii) in Lean follows the project's existing
532	\texttt{Scheme.toModuleKPresheaf} /
533	\texttt{toModuleKPresheaf\_isSheaf} template in
534	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/}, which
535	landed the analogous bottom-up
536	\(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\)-valued construction for the
537	structure sheaf. The order-condition locality is closed via the project's
538	\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} stalk bridge of
539	\texttt{RR.1} (REF and surrounding lemmas).
540

580	\section{Out of scope}
581	
582	\begin{itemize}
583	  \item \textbf{Linear-equivalence isomorphism.} The natural
584	    isomorphism \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when
585	    \(D \sim D'\) (i.e.\ \(D - D' = \div(f)\) for some
586	    \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)) is \emph{not} formalised in this chapter.
587	    It is not needed for the genus-\(0\) critical path: \texttt{RR.2}
588	    consumes only \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D))\) and the SES additivity at
589	    closed points; \texttt{RR.3} consumes only the closed-point
590	    specialisation; \texttt{RR.4} consumes the function-field /
591	    degree bridge, which is divisorial in \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) rather than
592	    in \(\mathrm{Pic}(C) = \mathrm{Div}(C) / \sim\). The
593	    Cartier-divisor \(\leftrightarrow\) \texttt{Pic} isomorphism is
594	    queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter.
595	  \item \textbf{Sheaf-multiplication
596	    $\mathcal O_C(D) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D') \cong \mathcal O_C(D +
597	    D')$.} The tensor-product identity of Hartshorne's
598	    Proposition~6.13(b) is invoked inside the proof of
599	    REF at the single instance
600	    $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
601	    \mathcal O_C(D)$, but the \emph{statement} of
602	    Proposition~6.13(b) as a separate lemma is not formalised in this
603	    chapter. The instance used inside the SES proof is local to that
604	    proof; a generalised tensor-additivity lemma is queued for the
605	    post-RR-bridge follow-up.
606	  \item \textbf{Pullback of \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) along a morphism.} The
607	    pullback \(f^* \mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_{C'}(f^* D)\) for
608	    a morphism of curves \(f : C' \to C\), used in degree-computation
609	    arguments on a base change, is \emph{not} formalised in this
610	    chapter and is not needed for the genus-\(0\) critical path.
611	  \item \textbf{Global-sections identification as the Riemann--Roch
612	    space.} The identification
613	    \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \cong L(D)\) for a general Weil divisor
614	    \(D\) (i.e.\ the analogue of
615	    REF for general
616	    \(D\)) is queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up. The closed-point
617	    case \(D = [P]\) is the only specialisation consumed by the
618	    genus-\(0\) critical path, and it is handled in
619	    \texttt{RR.3} via REF.
620	  \item \textbf{Serre duality and the canonical sheaf.} As recorded in
621	    the \texttt{RR.2} and \texttt{RR.3} chapters, Mathlib (snapshot
622	    \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme
623	    and no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) critical path
624	    does \emph{not} require either; the present chapter accordingly
625	    does not engage them.
626	\end{itemize}

3	\chapter{The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of \(\mathscr L(D)\)); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,
11	% 174, 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks
12	% below were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images
13	% of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
23	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\), the degree map $\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z\(, and the divisor \)[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, REF); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~\(0\)
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, REF); the global-sections
35	calculation \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a smooth
36	proper genus-\(0\) curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve \(\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1\)'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) associated to a closed point \(P \in C\)
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its \(\bar k\)-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\)
50	in the genus-\(0\) specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that \(C\) admits a non-constant rational function \(f\)
52	having at most a simple pole at \(P\) and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	\(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) whose degree-\(1\) identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% \(H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*\)
64	% to vanish \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) from the degree count
65	% \(\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0\). Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\) is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-\(0\) hypothesis
75	% \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0\) and the skyscraper
76	% identity \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\). The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-\(g\)
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
82	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus \(g(C)\) is the one defined in REF of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor \([P]\) of a closed point is the one of
94	REF of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor \(\div(f)\) of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order \(\ord_Q(f)\) at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	REF and REF.

110	\begin{definition}
111	
112	\leanok

114	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf}

116	  For each open \(U \subseteq C\), the \(\bar k\)-submodule
117	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U) \subset K(C)\) of section candidates
118	  satisfying the order conditions ``\(\mathrm{ord}_Q f \ge 0\) for all
119	  prime divisors \(Q \in U\) other than \(P\), and additionally
120	  \(\mathrm{ord}_P f \ge -1\) when \(P \in U\)'' assembles into a
121	  \(\bar k\)-linear functor
122	  \[
123	    \texttt{carrierPresheaf}_P : (\Opens\, |C|)^{\op}
124	    \longrightarrow \ModuleCat_{\bar k},
125	    \qquad U \longmapsto \mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U),
126	  \]
127	  with restriction \(V \subseteq U \mapsto \texttt{Submodule.inclusion}\,
128	  (\texttt{carrierSet\_mono})\) on each pair. The functor laws follow from
129	  the antitone monotonicity \(U \mapsto \mathrm{carrierSet}_P(U)\) (witness
130	  \(\texttt{carrierSet\_mono}\) at L181-192 of the Lean file) by
131	  \(\texttt{ext}\,\langle x, \_\rangle\); \texttt{rfl}.

134	\begin{lemma}
135	
136	\leanok

138	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}

140	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf,

145	  the locality + gluing conditions for \(\ModuleCat_{\bar k}\)-valued sections

147	  condition on the underlying Set-valued functor: each
148	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}_P(U)\) is a subset of the constant

156	\begin{definition}
157	
158	
159	\leanok
160	[Carrier-submodule refinement enforcing \(F(\bot)=0\)]
161	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
162	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.carrierSubmoduleSheaf}
163	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf}
164	  Iter-188 Lane A discovery: the raw \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P\) of
165	  \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf} satisfies
166	  \(\mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(\bot) = K(C)\) (since the order conditions
167	  are vacuously satisfied on the empty open). This violates the sheaf
168	  axiom on the Grothendieck topology of opens: the empty sieve covers
169	  \(\bot\), forcing \(F(\bot) =\) terminal \(= 0\) on
170	  \(\ModuleCat_{\bar k}\)-valued functors.
171	  The refinement
172	  \[
173	    \mathrm{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}_P(U) \;:=\;
174	    \mathrm{carrierSubmodule}_P(U) \;\sqcap\; \mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U,
175	    \qquad \mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U \;:=\;
176	    \{\, f \;\mid\; U \ne \bot \,\lor\, f = 0 \,\}.
177	  \]
178	  forces \(F(\bot) = \bot\) (intersection annihilates) while preserving
179	  \(F(U)\) on non-empty \(U\) (since then \(\mathrm{trivAtBot}\,U = \top\)).
180	  The case-based restriction map (zero target on \(\bot\); inclusion
181	  otherwise) gives a well-formed sheaf-condition substrate consumed by
182	  \cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}. Downstream
183	  consumers \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}, \texttt{toFunctionField},
184	  \texttt{globalSections\_iff\_*} are updated to supply the
185	  \texttt{trivAtBot} witness at \(\op\,\top\) via the \texttt{htop\_ne\_bot}
186	  helper.
187	\end{definition}

189	\begin{definition}
190	
191	
192	
193	\leanok
194	[Line bundle of a closed point]
195	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
196	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
197	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf, lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierPresheaf_isSheaf}
198	  
199	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
200	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
201	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
202	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
203	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
204	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
205	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
206	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
207	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
208	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
209	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
210	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let \(X\) be a scheme. Then:
211	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor \(D\), \(\mathscr L(D)\) is an invertible
212	  % sheaf on \(X\).}"
213	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
214	  \(\mathscr L(D)\) and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
215	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
216	  closed point. Let \(\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}\) denote the
217	  maximal ideal of the local ring at \(P\); by smoothness of \(C\),
218	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation ring and \(\mathfrak m_P\)
219	  is generated by any uniformizer \(f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\) (an
220	  element of \(\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2\)). The Cartier
221	  divisor associated to the closed point \(P\) is the effective
222	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer \(f_P\) on an open
223	  affine neighbourhood of \(P\), and by the unit \(1\) on the complement
224	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). The \emph{line bundle of the closed point \(P\)},
225	  denoted \(\mathcal O_C(P)\), is the locally free \(\mathcal O_C\)-module
226	  of rank \(1\) defined by Hartshorne's construction \(\mathscr L(D)\)
227	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
228	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
229	  \(\mathcal K_C\) (the constant sheaf of the function field \(K(C)\) on the
230	  integral scheme \(C\)) that is generated by \(f_P^{-1} \in K(C)\) on a
231	  neighbourhood of \(P\) and by \(1 \in K(C)\) on the complement
232	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\). Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
233	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
234	  uniformizer \(f_P\) (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
235	  \(\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}\), so they generate the same
236	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule of \(\mathcal K_C\)).
237	
238	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
239	  \(\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C\) denote the ideal sheaf of the
240	  closed point \(P\) (locally generated by the uniformizer \(f_P\) near
241	  \(P\), and equal to \(\mathcal O_C\) on the complement). Then
242	  \(\mathcal I_P\) is the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(-P)\) associated to the
243	  Weil divisor \(-[P]\), and \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the
244	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-linear dual:
245	  \[
246	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
247	    \mathcal O_C)
248	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
249	  \]
250	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
251	  ``subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\)'' description above is the one used in
252	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
253	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
254	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
255	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
256	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
257	
258	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
259	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
260	  scheme \(C\), a point \(P : C\), and a closedness witness
261	  \(\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}\) (the same input shape as
262	  REF), and returns an invertible
263	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module \(\mathcal O_C(P)\). The well-definedness as an
264	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
265	  smoothness of \(C\) (delivering the DVR stalk at \(P\)) and the standard
266	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
267	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
268	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
269	  closed plus dimension-\(1\)-integral delivers \(\mathrm{codim}_C \{P\} = 1\)
270	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
271	\end{definition}
272	
273	\begin{definition}
274	
275	
276	\leanok
277	[Inclusion of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) into the function field]
278	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}
279	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}
280	  
281	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\) as a
282	  % subsheaf of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\); the
283	  % canonical inclusion \(\mathscr L(D) \hookrightarrow \mathscr K\) is part
284	  % of the construction) (read from
285	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
286	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
287	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
288	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
289	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
290	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
291	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), and \(f_i^{-1}\) and
292	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
293	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
294	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of \(\mathscr L(D)\)
295	  as a subsheaf of \(\mathscr K\)).}
296	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)
297	  with \(C\) integral, and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. The line bundle
298	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) of REF is by
299	  construction a sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the sheaf of total quotient
300	  rings \(\mathcal K_C\). Since \(C\) is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the
301	  constant sheaf at the function field \(K(C)\), and taking global sections
302	  of the structural inclusion \(\mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow \mathcal K_C\)
303	  produces a canonical \(\bar k\)-linear map
304	  \[
305	    \iota \;\colon\; H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
306	      \;\longrightarrow\; H^0(C, \mathcal K_C) \;\cong\; K(C)
307	  \]
308	  sending a global section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to the rational
309	  function on \(C\) obtained by composing \(s\) with the inclusion of
310	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) into \(\mathcal K_C\) at each point. The map \(\iota\) is
311	  injective (the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module $\mathcal O_C(P) \subset
312	  \mathcal K_C\( is torsion-free of rank \)1$, so taking global sections
313	  preserves injectivity on the integral scheme \(C\)).
314	
315	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
316	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.toFunctionField}
317	  takes the same inputs as REF (a curve
318	  \(C\), a point \(P\), and the closedness witness
319	  \texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C.left)}) and produces, for each global

322	  element \(\iota(s) \in K(C)\) of the project's function-field carrier

328	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
329	
330	\begin{lemma}
331	
332	
333	
334	\leanok
335	[Global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) as rational functions with a
336	controlled pole]
337	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
338	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
339	  
340	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
341	  % divisors linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) are the divisors of zeros of
342	  % global sections of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\), with the explicit
343	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
344	  % \(f\) satisfying \((f) \geq -D_0\)) (read from
345	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
346	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let \(X\) be a nonsingular
347	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field \(k\). Let
348	  % \(D_0\) be a divisor on \(X\) and let \(\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)\)
349	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
350	  % (a) for each nonzero \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\), the divisor of
351	  % zeros \((s)_0\) is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\);
352	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) is \((s)_0\)
353	  % for some \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\); and
354	  % (c) two sections \(s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\) have the same
355	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a \(\lambda \in k^*\) such
356	  % that \(s' = \lambda s\).}"
357	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If \(D \geq 0\) and \(D = D_0 + (f)\), then
358	  % \((f) \geq -D_0\). Thus \(f\) gives a global section of
359	  % \(\mathscr L(D_0)\) whose divisor of zeros is \(D\)."
360	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
361	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
362	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\), let \(P \in C\) be a
363	  closed point, and let \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) be the line bundle of
364	  REF. The \(\bar k\)-vector space of
365	  global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is naturally identified with the
366	  Riemann--Roch space
367	  \[
368	    L([P]) \;:=\;
369	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
370	    \text{ in } \mathrm{Div}(C)\bigr\}
371	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
372	  \]
373	  Equivalently, the order conditions
374	  \[
375	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
376	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
377	    \qquad
378	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1
379	  \]
380	  on \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) are equivalent to the existence of a global
381	  section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) whose image \(\iota(s)\) under
382	  the canonical inclusion $\iota : \mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow
383	  \mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\( equals \)f\( (the iff binds \)s\( to \)f$
384	  explicitly — vacuous-in-\(f\) readings such as ``some nonzero global
385	  section exists'' are excluded by construction).
386	
387	  Under this identification, the inclusion
388	  \(\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of constant functions
389	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
390	  \mathcal O_C(P)\( exhibiting \)\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
391	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor \([P]\).
392	\end{lemma}
393	
394	\begin{proof}
395	  
396	  
397	  
398	  
399	  By construction, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
400	  the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathcal K_C\) generated locally by
401	  \(f_P^{-1}\) near \(P\) and by \(1\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), where \(f_P\) is
402	  a uniformizer at \(P\) (REF). Since \(C\)
403	  is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the constant sheaf at the function field
404	  \(K(C)\), and a global section of any sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module
405	  \(\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C\) is precisely an element of \(K(C)\)
406	  whose germ at every point lies in \(\mathcal F\) at that point.
407	  Therefore \(f \in K(C)\) is a global section of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) if
408	  and only if:
409	
410	  \begin{itemize}
411	    \item For every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\), the germ of
412	      \(f\) at \(Q\) lies in \(\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}\)
413	      (the structure sheaf, since \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) agrees with
414	      \(\mathcal O_C\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\)); equivalently,
415	      \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\).
416	    \item At the closed point \(P\), the germ of \(f\) at \(P\) lies in
417	      \(\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}\);
418	      equivalently, \(f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\), i.e.\
419	      \(\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\).
420	  \end{itemize}
421	  In divisorial language (REF,
422	  REF), the two conditions combine to
423	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
424	  \(L([P])\). The zero element of \(K(C)\) is included by convention as the
425	  zero global section.
426	
427	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
428	  taking \(D_0 = [P]\) effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
429	  rational function \(f\) with \((f) \geq -D_0\) defines a global section
430	  of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\). The condition \((f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]\) is exactly
431	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\). The compatibility with the constant inclusion
432	  follows by taking \(f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}\): then \(\div(1) = 0\), so
433	  \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), and the corresponding section of
434	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
435	  \cong \bar k\( under the structural inclusion \)\mathcal O_C
436	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
437	\end{proof}

439	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
440	
441	\begin{lemma}
442	
443	
444	
445	\leanok
446	[Vanishing of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
447	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
448	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
449	  
450	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
451	  % step ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point.''):
452	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
453	  % k(P) \to 0\( and its tensor with \)\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
454	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\)
455	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
456	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
457	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
458	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
459	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
460	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
461	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
462	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
463	  % (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
464	  % does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\))."
465	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
466	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
467	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
468	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
469	  \[
470	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
471	  \]
472	  i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\).
473	\end{lemma}
474	
475	\begin{proof}
476	  
477	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at \(D = 0\). The standard short
478	  exact sequence
479	  \[
480	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
481	  \]
482	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
483	  subscheme \(P\) is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P])\); the quotient is the structure
484	  sheaf of \(P\), which is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\)
485	  supported at \(P\) since \(P\) has residue field \(\bar k\) by closedness
486	  over the algebraically closed base \(\bar k\)). Tensoring with the
487	  locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\)
488	  (REF) preserves exactness and leaves
489	  the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged, giving
490	  \[
491	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
492	  \]
493	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\). Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
494	  cohomology (REF, the project's $\Module \bar
495	  k\(-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that \)\dim C = 1$ kills
496	  \(H^i\) for \(i \geq 2\) (Grothendieck vanishing on a \(1\)-dimensional
497	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
498	  \begin{multline*}
499	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
500	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
501	  \end{multline*}
502	  Two ingredients close the proof:
503	
504	  \emph{(a) \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\).} This is the genus-\(0\)
505	  hypothesis: by REF,
506	  \[
507	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
508	  \]
509	  so \(g(C) = 0\) is exactly \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\),
510	  i.e.\ \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
511	
512	  \emph{(b) \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).} The skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) is
513	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
514	  on an open \(U \subset C\) either restricts to \(0\) (if \(P \notin U\)) or
515	  takes values in the stalk \(k(P)_P \cong \bar k\) (if \(P \in U\)), and
516	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
517	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
518	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
519	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\), and \emph{a fortiori}
520	  \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
521	
522	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
523	  segment ending at \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to
524	  \[
525	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
526	  \]
527	  whence \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\), as required.
528	
529	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
530	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
531	  \(\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)\) at the abelian-group level
532	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
533	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline
534	  (REF) inherits the long exact sequence by
535	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
536	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\) is closed by either the flasque
537	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
538	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
539	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
540	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and a small affine open around \(P\) (the
541	  skyscraper has zero sections on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), so the Čech
542	  complex degenerates).
543	\end{proof}

545	\section{The dimension formula in genus zero}
546	
547	% NOTE (iter-194 reviewer): The Lean body is now structurally
548	% sorry-free (closed transitively through two named substrate
549	% helpers). The substrate helpers `h0_sub_h1_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_eq_two`
550	% (the Int-valued $H^0 - H^1 = 2$ \(\chi\)-bridge; gated on
551	% `OcOfD.lean` STRUCTURALLY BLOCKED + `RRFormula.lean` $\chi$-additivity)
552	% and `h1_vanishing_genusZero` (gated on Lane H \texttt{H1Vanishing.lean}
553	% substrate) carry the remaining typed sorries.  Future consumers
554	% reading the body should consult those.
555	\begin{theorem}
556	
557	
558	
559	\leanok
560	[\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
561	  \label{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
562	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
563	  
564	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the Riemann--Roch chain inside
565	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with \(D = [P]\): $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
566	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
567	  % \(H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0\) (read from
568	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
569	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
570	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
571	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
572	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
573	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
574	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
575	  % is the Euler characteristic
576	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
577	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
578	  \(\chi\)-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the
579	  genus-\(0\) specialisation chain).}
580	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
581	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
582	  \[
583	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 2.
584	  \]
585	\end{theorem}
586	
587	\begin{proof}
588	  
589	  
590	  
591	  Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
592	  REF of \texttt{RR.2} to
593	  the divisor \(D = [P]\) of REF. The
594	  degree of \([P]\) is \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
595	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), and the
596	  genus hypothesis \(g(C) = 0\) (REF) gives
597	  \[
598	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
599	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
600	  \]
601	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF as a two-term Euler
602	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
603	  \[
604	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
605	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
606	    \;=\; 2.
607	  \]
608	  By
609	  REF, the second
610	  term vanishes:
611	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\). Substituting yields
612	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), as required.
613	
614	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
615	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
616	  REF computes
617	  the dimension of \(H^0\) in one stroke without invoking
618	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
619	  \[
620	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
621	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
622	  \]
623	  together with \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\)
624	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: \(H^0\) of the structure sheaf on a proper
625	  geometrically irreducible variety over \(\bar k\) is \(\bar k\)),
626	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) (skyscraper at a \(\bar k\)-rational
627	  point), and \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) (genus-\(0\)), the connecting
628	  map \(H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) has zero codomain and the
629	  segment yields the short exact sequence
630	  \[
631	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
632	  \]
633	  of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
634	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
635	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
636	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses
637	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in
638	  line with the iter-173 RR sub-build sequencing.
639	\end{proof}

811	        def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}
812	  Unfold the witness section \(s\) along the linear-equivalence chain
