818	  with \(\widetilde f = f\) (from the hypothesis \(\iota(s) = f\)) and
819	  \(p\) a membership witness. By
820	  \cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_carrierSubmoduleSheaf}, membership
821	  in \(\mathrm{carrierSubmoduleSheaf}_P(\op\top)\) unfolds as the
822	  conjunction of (i) order \(\ge 0\) at every prime divisor \(Q\) of
823	  \(\top\) other than \(P\), and (ii) order \(\ge -1\) at \(P\)
824	  (intersected with the trivial \texttt{trivAtBot} factor, which is
825	  \(\top\) on the top open). Projecting \(p\) onto its first and
826	  second components returns exactly the pair of order bounds claimed
827	  (the auxiliary \(Q.\mathrm{point}\in \mathrm{Set.univ}\) premises
828	  are discharged by \(\mathrm{Set.mem\_univ}\)). The nonzero hypothesis
829	  on \(f\) is carried for downstream typing but is not consumed in the
830	  membership-projection step.
831	\end{proof}
832	
833	\begin{lemma}
834	
835	
836	\leanok
837	[Sub-claim (c) substrate: a rational function with zero order at every
838	prime divisor on a complete curve is constant
839	(Hartshorne~I.3.4 / Stacks~\texttt{02P0})]
840	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_functionField_const_of_complete_curve_of_orderZero}
841	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.functionField_const_of_complete_curve_of_orderZero}
842	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
843	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible integral curve
844	  over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\). Let \(f \in K(C)\) be
845	  a nonzero rational function such that
846	  \(\ord_Q(f) = 0\) for every prime divisor \(Q \in C\). Then \(f\)
847	  lies in the image of the constant inclusion
848	  \(\bar k \hookrightarrow K(C)\): there exists \(c \in \bar k\) with
849	  \[
850	    f \;=\; \texttt{algebraMap}\,\bar k\,K(C)\,c.
851	  \]
852	\end{lemma}
853	
854	% NOTE (iter-197 rename — substrate vs.\ contrapositive). The original
855	% iter-196 phrasing of sub-claim~(c) was the contrapositive form
856	% ``principal divisor of a non-constant rational function on a complete
857	% curve is non-zero'':
858	%   \mathtt{Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}\,f\,h_f \;\ne\; 0
859	% whenever \(s \notin \bar k\cdot s_1\) and \(f = \iota(s)\). The two
860	% forms are mathematically equivalent: \(\mathrm{principal}\,f =
861	% \sum_Q \ord_Q(f)\cdot[Q]\), so \(\mathrm{principal}\,f = 0\) iff
862	% \(\ord_Q(f) = 0\) for every prime divisor \(Q\), and the substrate
863	% above then forces \(f \in \bar k\). The iter-196 prover chose to
864	% extract the Hartshorne~I.3.4 / Stacks~\texttt{02P0} form as the
865	% standalone named substrate — it cleanly isolates the substantive
866	% Mathlib gap (``\(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) on a complete
867	% geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)'') — and inlined the
868	% contrapositive ``\(s \notin \bar k\cdot s_1 \Rightarrow
869	% \mathrm{principal}\,f \ne 0\)'' wrapper inside
870	% \texttt{exists\_nonconstant\_rational\_from\_dim\_eq\_two}
871	% (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean} lines~1596--1635).
872	% The contrapositive runs as follows: assume \(\mathrm{principal}\,f
873	% = 0\); apply the substrate to obtain \(f = c \in \bar k\); use
874	% \(\bar k\)-linearity of \(\iota\) plus \(\iota(s_1) = 1\) and
875	% injectivity of \(\iota\)
876	% (\cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField_injective}) to
877	% conclude \(s = c\cdot s_1 \in \bar k\cdot s_1\), contradicting
878	% \(s \notin \bar k\cdot s_1\).
879	
880	\begin{proof}
881	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
882	  This is the classical Hartshorne~I.3.4 statement
883	  ``\(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) on a complete geometrically
884	  irreducible reduced variety over the algebraically closed field
885	  \(\bar k\)'', combined with the
886	  ``no-zeros-no-poles \(\Rightarrow\) regular'' content of
887	  Stacks~\texttt{02P0}, in two ingredients:
888	
889	  \emph{Step (i) — algebraic Hartogs.} On the normal Noetherian scheme
890	  \(C\), a rational function regular at every codimension-\(1\) point
891	  extends uniquely to a global section of the structure sheaf
892	  (Stacks tag~\texttt{0BCK}: $\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) =
893	  \bigcap_{Q\in C^{(1)}} \mathcal O_{C, Q}$). On the smooth curve
894	  \(C\) every prime divisor \(Q\) is a codimension-\(1\) point and
895	  \(\ord_Q\) is the standard discrete valuation on
896	  \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}\); the hypothesis \(\ord_Q(f) = 0\) for every
897	  \(Q\) places \(f\) in the intersection
898	  \(\bigcap_Q \mathcal O_{C, Q} = \Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C)\).
899	
900	  \emph{Step (ii) — global sections are constants.} On a proper
901	  geometrically irreducible reduced variety over the algebraically
902	  closed field \(\bar k\), the global-sections ring of the structure
903	  sheaf coincides with the base field,
904	  \(\Gamma(C, \mathcal O_C) = \bar k\) (Hartshorne~I.3.4; or
905	  cohomologically: \(\Gamma\) is finite-dimensional over \(\bar k\)
906	  by Hartshorne~III.5.2, hence integral over \(\bar k\), hence
907	  algebraic, hence \(\bar k\) by algebraic closure). Combining
908	  step~(i) with step~(ii) places \(f\) in the image of the constant
909	  inclusion \(\bar k \hookrightarrow K(C)\), as required.
910	
911	  \emph{Mathlib status.} At snapshot \texttt{b80f227} both ingredients
912	  are project-bespoke Mathlib gaps: the Lean substrate helper carries
913	  a single typed sorry pending upstream development; the named-helper
914	  extraction makes the upstream dependency explicit for future
915	  iterations.
916	\end{proof}

918	\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
919	
920	\begin{corollary}
921	
922	
923	
924	\leanok
925	[A genus-\(0\) curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
926	single simple pole]
927	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
928	  % Alias label consumed under the shorter name by
929	  % \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex} (RR.4):
930	  \label{cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero}
931	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
932	  
933	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
934	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
935	  % point on a genus-\(0\) curve, as a direct consequence of the
936	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
937	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
938	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let \(X\) be a curve, and let \(P \in X\) be a
939	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
940	  % K(X)\(, which is regular everywhere except at \)P$."
941	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
942	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
943	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
944	  there exists a non-constant rational function \(f \in K(C)\) such that:
945	  \begin{itemize}
946	    \item \(f\) is regular on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
947	      for every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\);
948	    \item \(f\) has at most a simple pole at \(P\), i.e.\
949	      \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\);
950	    \item \(f \notin \bar k\), equivalently
951	      \(\div(f) \neq 0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\).
952	  \end{itemize}
953	  In particular, the pair \((1, f)\) spans
954	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
955	\end{corollary}
956	
957	\begin{proof}
958	  
959	  
960	  
961	  By
962	  REF,
963	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\). The constant subspace
964	  \(\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\), obtained from the
965	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
966	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
967	  \(f = 1\), with \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), under the
968	  identification of
969	  REF), is a one-dimensional
970	  \(\bar k\)-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension \(2\),
971	  the quotient \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1\) is a
972	  non-zero one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector space; any lift to
973	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of a non-zero element of the quotient is
974	  an element of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1\).
975	
976	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
977	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
978	  REF the section
979	  \(s\) corresponds to a rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)
980	  satisfying \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), equivalently \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
981	  for every \(Q \neq P\) and \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\). The choice
982	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) means \(f\) is not the image of a constant,
983	  so \(f \notin \bar k\) (every non-zero element of
984	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k\) corresponds, under the
985	  identification, to a constant element of \(K(C)\)). The three bullets
986	  of the statement are now immediate.
987	
988	  Spanning. Linear independence of \(1\) and \(f\) over \(\bar k\) in
989	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) is the same condition as
990	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) above; combined with the dimension count
991	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), the pair \((1, f)\) is a
992	  \(\bar k\)-basis of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\).
993	
994	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
995	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists\_nonconstant\_genusZero}
996	  returns an element \(f\) of the project's function-field carrier
997	  \(K(C)\) together with proofs of regularity on \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and
998	  the simple-pole bound at \(P\), packaged in the same shape as
999	  REF's output. The
1000	  non-constancy is recorded as a separate field; the spanning
1001	  statement is downstream and is consumed by \texttt{RR.4} via
1002	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}.
1003	\end{proof}
1004

12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	This chapter is the fourth and final sub-build chapter
14	\texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4} closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
15	\[
16	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
17	  \,\colon\,
18	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
19	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
20	\]
21	The chain assembled across the four sub-build chapters is:
22	\begin{itemize}
23	  \item \texttt{RR.1} (\texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}): the Weil-divisor
24	    group \(\Div(C)\), the degree map
25	    \(\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z\) (\texttt{def:divisor_degree}), and the
26	    degree-zero principal-divisor identity
27	    (\texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero}).
28	  \item \texttt{RR.2} (\texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}): the Riemann--Roch
29	    formula in genus zero \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\)
30	    (\texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}).
31	  \item \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter, dispatched parallel iter-174):
32	    the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\), its global-sections identification
33	    \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) = 2\), and the corollary
34	    \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} producing a non-constant
35	    rational function $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
36	    \bar k \cdot 1\( on a genus-\)0\( curve with a marked \)\bar k$-point.
37	  \item \texttt{RR.4} (\emph{this chapter}): the global classification ``a
38	    smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\)
39	    and a \(\bar k\)-rational point is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).''
40	\end{itemize}
41	This chapter consumes \texttt{RR.3} (the non-constant function) and produces
42	the Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} pinned at
43	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean} line~290 and restated as
44	the interface block \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} of
45	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}. The \texttt{RR.4}-closure of that target

49	\paragraph{Scope of the \(\bar k\)-base classification.} The chapter restricts
50	to the case where the base field \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed. Over $\bar
51	k\( every closed point is \)\bar k\(-rational, so the ``\)C\( has a \)\bar
52	k$-rational point'' hypothesis of Hartshorne's Exercise~IV.1.3 is automatic
53	(the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) is non-empty,
54	hence has closed points, all of which are \(\bar k\)-rational). The general
55	``\(k \to \bar k\) descent'' step ``genus-\(0\) curve over a non-closed \(k\) with

80	\section{The morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from two global sections}
81	\begin{lemma}
82	
83	
84	\leanok
85	[Morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from a globally generating pair of sections]
86	  \label{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}
87	  
88	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}
89	  Let \(X\) be a scheme over a field \(\bar k\), let \(\mathcal L\) be an invertible
90	  sheaf on \(X\), and let \(s_0, s_1 \in H^0(X, \mathcal L)\) be two global
91	  sections whose common zero locus is empty:
92	  \[
93	    \{x \in X : s_0(x) = 0 \text{ and } s_1(x) = 0\} \;=\; \emptyset.
94	  \]
95	  (Equivalently, \(s_0\) and \(s_1\) generate \(\mathcal L\) at every point.) Then
96	  there exists a unique morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
97	  \[
98	    \varphi_{(s_0, s_1)} \colon X \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
99	  \]
100	  whose pullback of \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(1)\) is \(\mathcal L\) and whose
101	  pullbacks of the standard coordinate sections $x_0, x_1 \in
102	  H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal O(1))\( are \)s_0\( and \)s_1$ respectively. On the
103	  basic-open chart $D_+(x_0) = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k} \subset
104	  \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\( the morphism \)\varphi_{(s_0, s_1)}\( is the map ``\)x
105	  \mapsto s_1(x) / s_0(x)\(''; on the chart \)D_+(x_1)\( it is the map ``\)x
106	  \mapsto s_0(x) / s_1(x)$''; the two definitions agree on the overlap
107	  \(D_+(x_0) \cap D_+(x_1) = \mathbb G_{m, \bar k}\) by the cocycle identity
108	  \((s_1 / s_0) \cdot (s_0 / s_1) = 1\).
109	\end{lemma}
110	\begin{proof}
111	
112	
113	
114	\leanok
115	  This is the standard linear-system construction in the form of
116	  Hartshorne's Theorem~II.7.1 (``Morphisms to \(\mathbb P^n\)''), specialised to
117	  \(n = 1\). Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the corresponding API
118	  under the name
119	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} in
120	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}:
121	  \begin{quote}
122	  \small
123	  \texttt{def Proj.fromOfGlobalSections \{\(\sigma\) A\} [CommRing A]
124	    [SetLike \(\sigma\) A] [AddSubgroupClass \(\sigma\) A] (\(\mathcal A\) :
125	    \(\mathbb N\) → \(\sigma\)) [GradedRing \(\mathcal A\)] \{X : Scheme\}\\
126	    \phantom{}\quad(f : A →+* X.presheaf.obj \(\top\))\\
127	    \phantom{}\quad(hf : Ideal.map f (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant \(\mathcal A\)).toIdeal = \(\top\)) :\\
128	    \phantom{}\quad(X \(\longrightarrow\) Proj \(\mathcal A\))}
129	  \end{quote}
130	  Specialising to \(\mathcal A = \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) with the standard $\mathbb
131	  N\(-grading by total degree, the inputs are a graded \)\bar k$-algebra
132	  homomorphism \(f \colon \bar k[x_0, x_1] \to H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) and a
133	  proof that the irrelevant ideal \((x_0, x_1) \cdot \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) maps to
134	  the unit ideal of \(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) (i.e.\ no common zeros). The
135	  resulting morphism lands in $\mathrm{Proj}\,\bar k[x_0, x_1] = \mathbb
136	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (Hartshorne~II, Example~7.1.3, the construction of \)\mathbb
137	  P^n\( as a \)\mathrm{Proj}\(). When \)\mathcal L$ is non-trivial the present
138	  lemma's signature is reduced to the Mathlib API by working on the open
139	  cover \(X = D(s_0) \cup D(s_1)\), where on each chart the sections become
140	  honest global functions (\(s_1 / s_0\) on \(D(s_0)\), \(s_0 / s_1\) on \(D(s_1)\),
141	  agreeing on the overlap). The chart-glue is performed by the
142	  project-bespoke wrapper
143	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}, whose body
144	  in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} chains
145	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} with the chart-restriction-and-glue
146	  pattern of \texttt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}.
147	  Uniqueness follows from the standard separation argument for morphisms to
148	  a separated scheme: two morphisms \(\varphi, \psi \colon X \to \mathbb P^1\)
149	  with \(\varphi^* x_i = \psi^* x_i\) for \(i = 0, 1\) agree on the basic-open
150	  charts \(D_+(x_0), D_+(x_1)\) (where they pull back the affine coordinates to
151	  the same rational function), and these charts cover \(\mathbb P^1\), so the
152	  two morphisms agree globally by gluing.
153	\end{proof}
154	\section{Degree of a morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via its pole divisor}
155	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase Pin 2 corrective; supersedes iter-182 narrative).
156	% `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \varphi` is the Weil divisor \(\varphi^*[\infty]\),
157	% defined as `positivePart (principal (\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))` in Lean.
158	% The supporting `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` def lives in
159	% `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` (\ref{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart}); the
160	% Hartshorne~II.6.9 identity that powers the existential degree statement
161	% lives there as \ref{lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}.
162	\begin{lemma}
163	
164	
165	\leanok
166	[Degree of a non-constant morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via the preimage of \(\infty\)]
167	  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}
168	  
169	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor}
170	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
171	  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
172	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
173	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
174	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
175	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
176	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.2, p.~299, opening of \S2.}
177	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism
178	  from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\). Then
179	  \(\varphi\) is finite, its function-field extension
180	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) (the canonical
181	  \(\varphi\)-induced map, supplied as an
182	  \texttt{[Algebra (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField C.functionField]}
183	  instance argument on the Lean side) is finite, and there exists a Weil
184	  divisor \(D \in \Div(C)\) such that
185	  \[
186	    D \;=\; \varphi^*[\infty]
187	    \quad\text{and}\quad
188	    \deg(D) \;=\; [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,].
189	  \]
190	  Equivalently, the morphism \(\varphi\) satisfies
191	  \[
192	    [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,] \;=\;
193	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
194	                  \ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [P]\,\right),
195	  \]
196	  where \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) is a local parameter at \(\infty\)
197	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
198	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)); and for any closed point
199	  \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) the same identification gives
200	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\).
201	  \emph{Convention on the pole divisor.} The Weil divisor
202	  \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) is exposed in Lean as
203	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \(\varphi\)}, a
204	  noncomputable definition introduced (as a typed \texttt{sorry}) at the
205	  iter-182 plan-phase signature strengthening of this lemma. The body of
206	  \texttt{poleDivisor} is queued for iter-183+ closure via the standard
207	  affine-chart construction (\texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia}
208	  in Mathlib, applied at each closed point \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
209	  to read off the local ramification multiplicity
210	  \(\ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty)\) from the maximal ideal at \(\infty\) in
211	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)). On the Lean side, the existential output of
212	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} packages
213	  \(D = \texttt{Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor}\;\varphi\) together with
214	  \(\deg(D) = \operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\),
215	  mirroring the OCofP pattern in which a typed-sorry def
216	  (\texttt{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}) is introduced
217	  upstream of its consumer lemma.
218	\end{lemma}
219	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase, supersedes iter-189 review NOTE):
220	% Pin 2 corrective COMMITTED — Option (a). `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` is
221	% being refactored to return the *positive part* of the principal divisor
222	% of `\varphi^{\#} t_\infty`, i.e. `(div(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))_0
223	% = \varphi^*[\infty]`. The supporting Weil-divisor `positivePart`
224	% definition lives in `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` as
225	% `def:WeilDivisor_positivePart`; the substantive Hartshorne~II.6.9
226	% identity that powers `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is now pinned as
227	% `lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` in `WeilDivisor.tex`.
228	% With these substrates in place, `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is
229	% mathematically correct as stated: the body's existential witness `D`
230	% is `Hom.poleDivisor \varphi = positivePart (principal (\varphi^{\#}
231	% t_\infty))`, and the degree identity is a one-line corollary of
232	% `lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`. iter-190 prover lane
233	% (Lane I) implements the refactor:
234	%   1. New def `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` in `WeilDivisor.lean`
235	%      (~10-15 LOC; lattice `D \sqcup 0`).
236	%   2. New lemma
237	%      `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` as a
238	%      named typed-sorry pin in `WeilDivisor.lean` (~5 LOC signature; body
239	%      ~50-80 LOC via affine-chart `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` route
240	%      owed iter-191+).
241	%   3. Refactor `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` body in `RationalCurveIso.lean`
242	%      from `principal (algebraMap _ _ t.val) halg` to
243	%      `positivePart (principal (algebraMap _ _ t.val) halg)` (~5 LOC).
244	%   4. Close `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` body via a 1-line
245	%      rewrite consuming (2) (~3 LOC).
246	% Net iter-190 Lane I: 1 axiom-clean closure (Pin 2 body, modulo named
247	% typed sorry on (2)); 1 NEW named typed-sorry pin
248	% (`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`) which is itself an
249	% iter-191+ Hartshorne II.6.9 substrate target. See iter-190 sidecar
250	% `iter/iter-190/plan.md` for the full rationale.
251	\begin{proof}
252	  \uses{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart, lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank, def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_degree, thm:principal_deg_zero}
253	  \leanok
254	  A non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is
255	  automatically finite. Indeed, the image of \(\varphi\) is a closed
256	  irreducible subset of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) that is not a point (else
257	  \(\varphi\) would be constant) and not empty, so it is all of $\mathbb
258	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (the only proper irreducible closed subset of \)\mathbb
259	  P^1_{\bar k}\( is a point); hence \)\varphi$ is surjective. A surjective
260	  morphism between smooth proper irreducible curves of equal dimension is
261	  finite (proper + quasi-finite, where quasi-finiteness comes from the
262	  fact that \(\varphi\) has zero-dimensional fibres: a positive-dimensional
263	  fibre would force a one-dimensional irreducible component in the fibre,
264	  contradicting irreducibility of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(C\) at
265	  generic points). Hence the function-field extension
266	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) = \varphi^\# K(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\)
267	  is finite.
268	  The identification \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\) for
269	  any closed point \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) is the multiplicativity of the
270	  degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9): for a finite morphism
271	  \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) of smooth proper curves and any divisor $D \in
272	  \Div(C')\(, \)\deg(\varphi^* D) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg(D)$. Specialising
273	  to \(D = [Q]\) (a divisor of degree \(1\) since \(\kappa(Q) = \bar k\)) gives
274	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = \deg(\varphi)\). Equivalently, the degree of
275	  \(\varphi\) equals the number of preimages of any closed point of $\mathbb
276	  P^1\(, counted with the ramification multiplicities \)\ord_P(\varphi^*
277	  t_\infty)\( at the preimage points \)P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)$.
278	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The lemma threads two auxiliary inputs:
279	  ``non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves is finite'' (the
280	  surjective-and-proper-and-quasi-finite argument above; Hartshorne~II.6.8)
281	  and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback'' (Hartshorne~II.6.9
282	  and the multiplicativity of \(\deg\) under composition of \(\div\) and
283	  \(\deg\); the closely related ``\(\mathrm{div}(f)\) has degree zero'' specialisation
284	  is \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}). Both are separable
285	  sub-lemmas inside \texttt{RR.4}'s Lean file
286	  (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) and are
287	  slotted for closure in a follow-up iteration after the
288	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} skeleton lands.
289	\end{proof}
290	\section{A degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism}
291	% NOTE (iter-181 Lane I signature refinement; see analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md
292	% Decision 2, verdict DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY). The iter-177 file-skeleton
293	% placeholder hypothesis "Nonempty (C'.functionField ≃+* C.functionField)"
294	% (an abstract function-field iso, unrelated to \(\varphi\)) was strictly
295	% weaker than the birational-extension body needs and was refined in
296	% iter-181 to the explicit function-field-extension-degree formulation
297	% [K(C):K(C')] = 1, i.e. Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField
298	% = 1, with C.functionField a C'.functionField-algebra via the canonical
299	% \varphi-induced map (Scheme.Hom.stalkMap at the generic point composed
300	% with IsFractionRing.lift). The proof-prose body below (the Hartshorne
301	% I.6.12 chain + scheme-theoretic alternative argument) is unchanged ---
302	% only the input shape changes.
303	% NOTE (iter-194 reviewer; refreshed iter-196): The Lean
304	% `iso_of_degree_one` body inline Step 1 was refactored iter-194 from
305	% 16 LOC of `Subalgebra.bot_eq_top_of_finrank_eq_one +
306	% Algebra.surjective_algebraMap_iff + RingHom.injective +
307	% RingEquiv.ofBijective` to 4 LOC consuming two new axiom-clean substrate
308	% helpers (`algebraMap_bijective_of_finrank_one` +
309	% `phi_left_functionField_algEquiv_of_finrank_one`).  Helper (a)
310	% `phi_left_locallyQuasiFinite_of_finrank_one` was reformulated iter-196
311	% via Mathlib's `LocallyQuasiFinite.of_finite_preimage_singleton`
312	% (`QuasiFinite.lean:295`) with in-scope derivation of `IsProper φ.left`
313	% + `LocallyOfFiniteType φ.left`; the residual gap is now the concrete
314	% topological claim `(φ.left ⁻¹' {x}).Finite` for arbitrary
315	% `x : C'.left`. The generic-point branch is closeable via
316	% `genericPoint_eq` functoriality + the algebra-iso substrate; the
317	% closed-point branch remains a Mathlib gap (\textit{smooth-dim-1}
318	% $\Rightarrow$ 0-dim fibre; iter-197+ candidate). Helper (d)
319	% `phi_left_fromNormalization_isIso_of_smoothProper_finrank_one` is
320	% gated on project-side `IsNormalScheme` substrate.
321	\begin{lemma}
322	
323	
324	\leanok
325	[Degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism]
326	  \label{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso}
327	  
328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one}
329	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field-to-curve
330	  % equivalence of categories) (read from
331	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 62, doc p.~45)
332	  % + [Hartshorne], IV \S2 opening paragraph (degree of a finite morphism
333	  % of curves is the function-field extension degree) (read from
334	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
335	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.12. \emph{The following three categories are
336	  % equivalent:}
337	  % (i) \emph{nonsingular projective curves, and dominant morphisms;}
338	  % (ii) \emph{quasi-projective curves, and dominant rational maps;}
339	  % (iii) \emph{function fields of dimension 1 over \(k\), and \(k\)-homomorphisms.}"
340	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
341	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
342	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
343	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, I.6 Corollary~6.12 (p.~45) + IV.2 opening
344	  (p.~299).}
345	  Let \(C, C'\) be smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over an
346	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) be
347	  a non-constant morphism whose induced function-field extension is of
348	  degree one,
349	  \[
350	    [\,K(C) : K(C')\,] \;=\; 1,
351	  \]
352	  formally
353	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(C')} K(C) = 1\) in the Lean signature.
354	  Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
355	  \emph{Convention on the function-field map.} At each call-site of this
356	  lemma, the \(K(C')\)-algebra structure on \(K(C)\) used to interpret the
357	  degree \([K(C) : K(C')]\) is the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
358	  function-field map, i.e.\ the composite
359	  \[
360	    K(C') \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{germ}\;}\;
361	    \mathcal O_{C', \varphi(\xi_C)}
362	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\varphi^\#\;}\;
363	    \mathcal O_{C, \xi_C}
364	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{frac.}\,\text{lift}\;}\; K(C)
365	  \]
366	  of the germ at the image of the generic point \(\xi_C \in C\) with the
367	  stalk-map of \(\varphi\) at \(\xi_C\) and the fraction-field lift at
368	  \(\xi_C\) (per \texttt{analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md}, Decision~2). On
369	  the Lean side this canonical instance is carried as a
370	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
371	  to \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one}; consumers
372	  supply it explicitly at each call-site.
373	\end{lemma}
374	% NOTE (iter-189 review): Pin 3 Step 1 (function-field iso K(C') ≃+* K(C)
375	% from `Module.finrank = 1`) closed axiom-clean inline iter-189 (~10 LOC via
376	% `Subalgebra.bot_eq_top_of_finrank_eq_one` + `Algebra.surjective_algebraMap_iff`
377	% + `RingHom.injective` + `RingEquiv.ofBijective`). Step 2 (scheme-level lift
378	% via `Scheme.Hom.toNormalization`) remains a typed sorry in the body, with 4
379	% sub-obligations (a)-(d) documented inline at the sorry. Iter-190+ closure
380	% budget ~80-150 LOC per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md`.
381	\begin{proof}
382	
383	
384	
385	\leanok
386	  The function-field hypothesis \([K(C) : K(C')] = 1\) states that the
387	  canonical \(\varphi\)-induced map \(\varphi^\# \colon K(C') \to K(C)\) is a
388	  finite extension of degree one; since \(K(C')\) is a field and the
389	  extension has dimension one as a \(K(C')\)-vector space, \(\varphi^\#\) is
390	  an isomorphism of function fields \(K(C') \cong K(C)\). (Equivalently, by
391	  the IV.2-opening identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C):K(C')]\), this is
392	  the statement \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\); the morphism \(\varphi\) is finite in
393	  this case by the standard argument that a non-constant morphism of
394	  smooth proper curves is finite, identical to the
395	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} preamble.)
396	  By Hartshorne's Corollary~I.6.12, the assignment \(C \mapsto \bar k(C)\) is
397	  an equivalence of categories between smooth projective curves over $\bar
398	  k$ (with dominant morphisms) and function fields of transcendence degree
399	  one over \(\bar k\) (with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms). Under this equivalence,
400	  the isomorphism \(\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)\) produced by the previous
401	  paragraph lifts to a unique isomorphism of smooth proper curves $C \to
402	  C'\(, and this isomorphism agrees with \)\varphi$ on the generic point (by
403	  construction: it is the inverse-image of \(\varphi^\#\) under the
404	  equivalence). The two morphisms \(C \to C'\) that agree on the generic
405	  point of the integral scheme \(C\) agree everywhere (a morphism out of an
406	  integral scheme to a separated scheme is determined by its restriction
407	  to the generic point, Hartshorne~II Ex.~4.2). Hence \(\varphi\) is itself
408	  the lift, and is therefore an isomorphism.
409	  \emph{Alternative direct argument.} Without invoking the
410	  equivalence-of-categories framing, the same conclusion is obtained
411	  scheme-theoretically: a finite degree-\(1\) morphism $\varphi \colon C \to
412	  C'$ between integral normal noetherian schemes is an isomorphism.
413	  Indeed, the direct image \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C\) is a coherent
414	  \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module that is generically of rank $\deg(\varphi) =
415	  1\(, and an inclusion \)\mathcal O_{C'} \hookrightarrow \varphi_*
416	  \mathcal O_C\( of coherent rank-\)1$ sheaves whose generic ranks agree
417	  is an isomorphism over the locus where the quotient
418	  \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C / \mathcal O_{C'}\) has trivial stalk. The
419	  quotient is a torsion coherent \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module of generic rank
420	  \(\deg(\varphi) - 1 = 0\), hence is itself torsion-free (a
421	  finitely-generated torsion module over a Dedekind domain of generic
422	  rank zero is zero), so vanishes. Therefore $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C =
423	  \mathcal O_{C'}\( and \)\varphi$ is the structure morphism of an
424	  isomorphism. Cf.\ Stacks tag~\textbf{0AVX} for the categorical version.
425	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The Lean signature
426	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one} consumes a
427	  non-constant morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) together with a
428	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
429	  (the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced function-field map) and the
430	  function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis
431	  \texttt{Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField = 1}, and
432	  produces an isomorphism \(C \cong C'\) in the slice category
433	  \(\mathtt{Over (Spec (.of\ kbar))}\). The proof body threads the Mathlib
434	  API \texttt{IsIntegralHom.iso\_of\_isUnit} (or the equivalent
435	  \texttt{IsAffineHom + Finite + RankOne} chain) and, where needed, the
436	  project-bespoke ``finite-morphism-of-degree-one is an isomorphism''
437	  helper. The Hartshorne~I.6.12 equivalence-of-categories framing is the
438	  intended high-level proof structure; the scheme-theoretic argument
439	  above is the spelled-out form for the Lean body.
440	\end{proof}
441	\section{Headline: genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)}
442	\begin{theorem}
443	
444	\leanok
445	[Genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)]
446	  \label{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}
447	  
448	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
449	  % already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely
450	  % suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two distinct
451	  % points of \(X\) and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor \(D = P - Q\).
452	  % Since \(\deg(K - D) = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have \(l(K - D) = 0\),
453	  % and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of degree 0, so by
454	  % (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words \(P \sim Q\). But this implies
455	  % \(X\) is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
456	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
457	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, and let \(C\) be a smooth
458	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\).
459	  Then there exists an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
460	  \[
461	    \varphi \colon C \stackrel{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}.
462	  \]
463	  Concretely, the Lean signature
464	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} (the existing pinned
465	  target at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290}) is
466	  closed by the proof below; the existing interface block
467	  \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} of
468	  \texttt{chap:avr_for_rr} is its consumer-side restatement,
469	  pinning the same Lean declaration.
470	\end{theorem}
471	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the
472	% statement block above (Hartshorne states the classification and its
473	% proof inside the single Example; the proof is the second half beginning
474	% "We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) \ldots"). The present
475	% proof body restates Hartshorne's argument in the project's notation
476	% (linear-system construction \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C
477	% ([P]))\(, degree-\)1$ identification, isomorphism).
478	\begin{proof}
479	  Over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) the smooth proper
480	  geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) has closed points, all of which are
481	  \(\bar k\)-rational (the residue field of a closed point of a curve over
482	  \(\bar k\) is a finite extension of \(\bar k\), hence is \(\bar k\) itself by
483	  algebraic closedness). Fix one such \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P \in C\);
484	  this is the marked point used to build the linear system below.
485	  \emph{Step 1: non-constant function \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\).}
486	  By Hartshorne's Riemann--Roch in genus zero
487	  (\texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}) applied to the
488	  effective divisor \([P]\) of degree \(1\), we obtain
489	  \(\ell([P]) = \deg([P]) + 1 = 2\). The constant section
490	  \(1 \in \bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) accounts for a
491	  one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-subspace; the two-dimensional quotient supplies
492	  a non-constant section $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
493	  \bar k \cdot 1$. This is the corollary
494	  \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
495	  chapter, \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OCofP.tex}).
496	  \emph{Step 2: morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via the linear system \((1, f)\).}
497	  The pair \((1, f) \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))^2\) generates the
498	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) at every point of \(C\): at points
499	  \(Q \in C \setminus \{P\}\) the section \(1\) does not vanish in
500	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])_Q \cong \mathcal O_{C, Q}\), and at the marked point
501	  \(P\) the section \(f\) does not vanish in \(\mathcal O_C([P])_P\) (because \(f\)
502	  has a pole of order exactly \(1\) at \(P\) in the rational-function
503	  picture: \(f \in K(C) \setminus \bar k\) with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = [P]\)).
504	  Therefore by \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections} there
505	  exists a unique morphism
506	  \[
507	    \varphi \;:=\; \varphi_{(1, f)} \colon C \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
508	  \]
509	  with \(\varphi^* x_0 = 1\) and \(\varphi^* x_1 = f\) (so the pullback of
510	  the standard ratio coordinate \(u = x_1 / x_0\) on $\mathbb P^1 \setminus
511	  \{\infty\}\( is \)f$, and the pullback of the inverse coordinate
512	  \(t_\infty = x_0 / x_1 = 1 / f\) is the local parameter \(1/f\) at \(P\)).
513	  \emph{Step 3: \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\) via the pole divisor of \(f\).} The
514	  morphism \(\varphi\) is non-constant (since \(f\) is non-constant as a
515	  rational function on \(C\), the pullback \(\varphi^* u = f\) takes
516	  infinitely many values, ruling out a constant \(\varphi\)). By
517	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} applied to \(\varphi\),
518	  \[
519	    \deg(\varphi)
520	    \;=\;
521	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{Q \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
522	                  \ord_Q(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [Q]\,\right).
523	  \]
524	  We compute the pole divisor of \(f\) as a rational function on \(C\):
525	  since \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) with \(f\) non-constant and
526	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) the line bundle of the effective divisor \([P]\), the
527	  rational-function \(f\) has pole divisor \((f)_\infty = [P]\) (the
528	  unique pole of order \(1\) at \(P\), no other poles; this is the standard
529	  identification of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\) with the rational functions
530	  \(g \in K(C)\) satisfying \(\mathrm{div}(g) + D \geq 0\), specialised to
531	  \(D = [P]\)). Hence \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty) = \{P\}\) with multiplicity~\(1\)
532	  and
533	  \[
534	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\; \deg([P]) \;=\; 1.
535	  \]
536	  \emph{Step 4: \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.} By Hartshorne's IV.2
537	  identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\), the
538	  degree-one conclusion of Step~3 rewrites as
539	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) = 1\) (with
540	  the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
541	  \texttt{[Algebra \,(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField\,
542	   C.functionField]} instance). By
543	  \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} applied to the non-constant
544	  morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (smooth proper
545	  geometrically irreducible curves over \(\bar k\) on both sides), under
546	  this function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis, \(\varphi\) is an
547	  isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
548	  This produces the required isomorphism \(C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)
549	  closing the headline target
550	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}.
551	  \emph{Comparison with Hartshorne's original argument.} Hartshorne's
552	  Example~IV.1.3.5 phrases the argument in terms of two distinct points
553	  \(P, Q \in C\) and the degree-zero divisor \(D = P - Q\), deriving \(P \sim Q\)
554	  and concluding via II.6.10.1 that \(C\) is rational; combined with I.6.12
555	  this yields \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\). The present proof restates the same
556	  combinatorial content via the more directly formalisable linear-system
557	  construction: instead of going through the \(P \sim Q\) statement and
558	  invoking II.6.10.1 as a black box, we produce the explicit morphism
559	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1\) from the two sections \((1, f)\) of
560	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) given by Riemann--Roch and identify its degree
561	  directly. The two arguments are mathematically equivalent and have the
562	  same dependency footprint (Riemann--Roch in genus zero \(\Rightarrow\)
563	  \(\ell([P]) = 2\) \(\Rightarrow\) non-constant \(f\) \(\Rightarrow\) degree-\(1\)
564	  map to \(\mathbb P^1\) \(\Rightarrow\) iso); the linear-system phrasing maps
565	  more cleanly onto the Mathlib \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} API and
566	  the project's existing Weil-divisor/degree pipeline of \texttt{RR.1}.
567	\end{proof}
568	\begin{remark}
569	[Drop of \texttt{sorryAx} on the genus-\(0\) Albanese keystone]
570	  \label{rmk:rr4_unblocks_sorryAx_on_rigidity_genus0}
571	  Closure of \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} retires the
572	  \texttt{sorryAx} propagation tagged on
573	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}
574	  (\texttt{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of
575	  \texttt{chap:avr_for_rr}, body landed iter-166), whose
576	  \emph{first} of two upstream \texttt{sorry} sources is the
577	  \texttt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} target this chapter closes. The
578	  \emph{second} upstream is the helper
579	  \texttt{morphism\_P1\_to\_grpScheme\_const}'s residual sorries, tracked
580	  separately under the genus-\(0\) Albanese arm of \texttt{Jacobian.tex}.
581	\end{remark}
582	\section{Out of scope}
583	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.4} and
584	are sequenced elsewhere:
585	\begin{itemize}
586	  \item The general ``smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) of
587	    genus \(0\) over a \emph{non-closed} field \(k\) with a \(k\)-rational point
588	    \(\cong \mathbb P^1_k\)'' statement is the \(\bar k \to k\) descent of
589	    \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso}. The descent is hosted in
590	    \texttt{Jacobian.tex} via \texttt{genusZeroWitness.key}; it consumes
591	    \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} together with Galois descent for
592	    smooth proper curves and is out of scope for \texttt{RR.4}.
593	  \item The Mathlib API \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}
594	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}) is
595	    consumed as a black box by
596	    \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}; its own proof inside
597	    Mathlib (the universal property of \(\mathrm{Proj}\), graded-ring
598	    presentation of \(\mathbb P^n\)) is not re-derived here.
599	  \item Multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9)
600	    is consumed as a black box by \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor};
601	    its proof is a separable sub-lemma threaded inside
602	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} and is
603	    \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.4}'s blueprint statement (it lives at
604	    the same logical level as the closely related
605	    \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}).
606	  \item The full equivalence of categories ``smooth projective curves over
607	    \(\bar k\) with dominant morphisms'' \(\cong\) ``function fields of
608	    transcendence degree~\(1\) over \(\bar k\) with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms''
609	    (Hartshorne~I.6.12) is consumed as a high-level framing in
610	    \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} but is \emph{not} re-formalised at
611	    the equivalence-of-categories level here. The proof body in the Lean
612	    file threads the spelled-out scheme-theoretic ``finite-degree-one
613	    morphism between integral normal schemes is an isomorphism''
614	    argument (Stacks~\textbf{0AVX}), which is the minimal-Mathlib-dependence
615	    pin compatible with the chapter's downstream needs.
616	  \item ``A smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)
617	    has at least two distinct \(\bar k\)-points'' (the non-empty / infinite
618	    cardinality of \(C(\bar k)\)) is consumed implicitly by the present
619	    chapter but its formal statement lives in the project's curve API
620	    (\texttt{Genus.tex} / \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus}); the chapter
621	    does not restate it.
622	  \item Cohomology of the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) on a curve
623	    (Hartshorne~III.5 + IV.1) is fully consumed via \texttt{RR.3}; in
624	    particular the \(H^1\)-vanishing of \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) on a genus-\(0\)
625	    curve is part of \texttt{RR.3}'s payload feeding
626	    \texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}, and is not
627	    re-derived here.
628	\end{itemize}
629

content:
1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form; Mumford \S4 in the abelian-variety case)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of schemes that agree on a non-empty open}. The classical motivation is Mumford's rigidity for abelian-variety morphisms, but the project's formalised statement is more general: the source-side group-object hypothesis is unused by the proof and is dropped.
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of \(\Jac(C)\). It is the load-bearing input for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-0 Albanese-witness argument (Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b), where the source is taken to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (which is \emph{not} a group scheme).
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}\leanok
11	[Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form)]
12	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen}
14	  Let \(k\) be a field. Let \(X\) be a reduced geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme and let \(Y\) be a separated \(k\)-scheme. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y\) be two morphisms of \(k\)-schemes, and let \(U \subseteq X\) be a non-empty open subset. If
15	  \[
16	    U.\iota \circ g_1 \;=\; U.\iota \circ g_2
17	  \]
18	  as scheme morphisms (where \(U.\iota \colon U \hookrightarrow X\) is the canonical open immersion), then \(g_1 = g_2\) as morphisms of \(k\)-schemes.
19	\end{theorem}
20	
21	\begin{remark}
22	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S4) usually states the special case where \(Y\) is an abelian variety and the conclusion is phrased as ``a morphism vanishing on a non-empty open is zero''. The two forms are interderivable in the abelian-variety case via the difference morphism \(\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}\) formed from the group law on \(Y\). The project formalises the more general statement above because the M2.a (genus-0 Albanese-witness) consumer specifically needs the source to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\), which is not a group scheme: the abelian-variety packaging is consumer-side and the scheme-level statement is what the proof actually establishes.
23	
24	  The refactor that landed the scheme-level form (iter-125; see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md}) dropped 8 unused hypotheses from the project's original group-scheme-flavoured declaration and weakened the target-side \([\mathtt{IsProper}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) to \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\).
25	\end{remark}
26	
27	\section{Proof sketch}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  \leanok
31	  We reduce to Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}), which gives the standard scheme-level fact that two morphisms from a reduced scheme to a separated scheme agreeing on a dominant subscheme agree everywhere.
32	
33	  The three load-bearing instances feeding that closure are:
34	  \begin{itemize}
35	    \item \emph{\(Y \to \Spec k\) is separated.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) provides this directly (no projection from \(\mathtt{IsProper}\) needed). The \texttt{OverClass.fromOver} instance transports it to the form \(\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ (Y.\mathrm{left} \downarrow \Spec k)\) that the closure step expects.
36	    \item \emph{\(X\) is an irreducible space.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ X.\mathrm{hom}]\) over the field-spectrum base \(\Spec k\) (which is a single point, hence subsingleton) yields \(\mathtt{IrreducibleSpace}\ X.\mathrm{left}\) via \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton}.
37	    \item \emph{The inclusion \(U.\iota\) is dominant.} A non-empty open in an irreducible space is dense, and a dense open immersion is dominant: \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι}.
38	  \end{itemize}
39	  With these three derived instances and the hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsReduced}\ X.\mathrm{left}]\), the closure \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} produces the underlying scheme-morphism equality \(g_1.\mathrm{left} = g_2.\mathrm{left}\) from the agreement-on-\(U\) hypothesis. The promotion to \(g_1 = g_2\) in \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\) uses \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext}.
40	
41	  \medskip
42	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
43	  \begin{itemize}
44	    \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} for the scheme-level closure step.
45	    \item \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton} to derive irreducibility of \(X.\mathrm{left}\).
46	    \item \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι} to derive dominance of the open immersion \(U.\iota\) from the non-empty hypothesis.
47	    \item \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext} to promote underlying scheme equality to \(\mathrm{Over}\)-morphism equality.
48	    \item No abelian-variety / group-object infrastructure is needed: smoothness, properness, and group-object hypotheses on \(X\) and \(Y\) are all dropped from the formalised statement.
49	  \end{itemize}
50	\end{proof}
51	
52	\section{Use in the project}
53	
54	The rigidity theorem is used in two places downstream:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{M2.a (genus-0 Albanese witness).} Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b reduces the genus-0 rigidity-over-\(\bar k\) argument (for \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}\) hitting the identity at a \(\bar k\)-point) to this theorem with \(X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(Y := A_{\bar k}\). The refactor of iter-125 (dropping the source-side group-object hypothesis) is what enables this application. The named project declaration packaging the C.2.a–C.2.e reduction is \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} in \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}; that chapter scaffolds the iter-126 Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} with the C.2.d cotangent-vanishing keystone residual.
58	  \item \textbf{Uniqueness in the Albanese property.} Used in the uniqueness half of Theorem~REF (the Albanese property of \(\Jac(C)\)): given two morphisms \(g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A\) with \(g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P\), scheme-level agreement on the image of \(\alpha_P\) (a non-empty open after passing to \(C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)\)) forces \(g_1 = g_2\).
59	\end{itemize}
60	
61	Note: the existence half of REF requires Phase~B/C infrastructure (Picard scheme, dual abelian variety). Rigidity alone yields \emph{uniqueness} only.
62	
63	\section{Mathlib status}
64	
65	The scheme-level form is a thin specialization of Mathlib's existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}); the project's contribution is the \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\)-bundled packaging plus the convenience derivations of \texttt{IrreducibleSpace} and \texttt{IsDominant} from the project-side hypotheses.
66	
67	An upstream-PR candidate is a thin corollary in Mathlib's \texttt{Separated} file:
68	\[
69	  \texttt{ext\_of\_eqOnNonemptyOpen} \;\colon\; \texttt{(non-empty open) + (reduced source) + (separated target) }\Rightarrow\texttt{ ext}
70	\]
71	packaging the dense-and-dominant chain into a single API call. The incremental value over the existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} is small (just the API convenience of "non-empty open" over "dominant morphism"); see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md} for the proposed statement and home file. The project does not pursue this upstream PR as part of the iter-125 work.
72

1	\chapter{Cotangent space at the identity (piece (i) --- Lean file)}
2	\label{chap:cotangent-grpobj}
3	
4	This chapter is a pointer to the Lean file
5	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/GrpObj.lean}. The mathematical content
6	itself lives in chapter~REF (\texttt{RigidityKbar.tex}
7	\S "Piece (i)") and is not duplicated here. This chapter exists to satisfy
8	the per-Lean-file blueprint convention (one chapter per Lean file).
9	
10	\textbf{Post-iter-145 disposition.} After the iter-145 refactor
11	\texttt{refactor-chart-algebra-skeleton-bundled-excise-iter145}, the
12	file contains the piece (i.a) trio
13	(\texttt{cotangentSpaceAtIdentity},
14	\texttt{cotangentSpaceAtIdentity\_eq\_extendScalars},
15	\texttt{cotangentSpaceAtIdentity\_finrank\_eq}, all closed
16	iter-128--iter-132) plus a small set of orphan helpers from the
17	iter-134--iter-136 bundled-route build
18	(\texttt{shearMulRight} with its \texttt{\_hom\_fst}/\texttt{\_hom\_snd}
19	companions, \texttt{schemeHomRingCompatibility}, and
20	\texttt{relativeDifferentialsPresheaf\_restrict\_along\_identity\_section}),
21	together with two private structural helpers
22	(\texttt{section\_snd\_eq\_identity\_struct} and
23	\texttt{isIso\_of\_app\_iso\_module}) that the surviving public
24	declarations use locally. Zero \texttt{sorry}-bodied declarations
25	remain in this file. The five excised bundled-route declarations
26	(\texttt{basechange\_along\_proj\_two\_inv\_derivation},
27	\texttt{basechange\_along\_proj\_two\_inv},
28	\texttt{basechange\_along\_proj\_two\_inv\_app\_isIso},
29	\texttt{relativeDifferentialsPresheaf\_basechange\_along\_proj\_two},
30	\texttt{mulRight\_globalises\_cotangent}) and their iter-138--iter-143
31	build history live in the git history of this file (per the iter-145
32	Q7 "auditable record IS git history" framing in \texttt{STRATEGY.md}
33	\S Iter-144 chart-algebra pivot). The active chart-algebra piece (ii)
34	work has moved to \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean}
35	with informal content in \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} \S "Chart-algebra
36	piece (ii) first-class decomposition" of \texttt{RigidityKbar.tex}.
37	
38	\section{Lean declarations in this file}
39	
40	\begin{itemize}
41	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity}
42	        --- the definition of the cotangent space at the identity as a
43	        \texttt{ModuleCat k}. Statement~\cref{lem:GrpObj_cotangentSpace}
44	        (piece (i.a); closed iter-128, body refactored through iter-131).
45	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity\_eq\_extendScalars}
46	        --- structural-shape acceptance lemma (piece (i.a); closed
47	        iter-131; supports both \texttt{change}-based and
48	        \texttt{obtain}+\texttt{rw} rewrite patterns downstream).
49	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.cotangentSpaceAtIdentity\_finrank\_eq}
50	        --- rank lemma (piece (i.a); closed iter-132).
51	        Statement~\cref{lem:GrpObj_lieAlgebra_finrank}.
52	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.shearMulRight} --- the binary-product
53	        shear isomorphism \(\sigma = \langle \pr_1, \mu \rangle\) in
54	        \(\Over\,(\Spec k)\) (piece (i.b) Step 1; closed iter-134). Built
55	        from \texttt{GrpObj} data only; modelled on
56	        \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrpObj.mulRight}. Companion \texttt{@[simps]}
57	        lemmas \texttt{shearMulRight\_hom\_fst} and
58	        \texttt{shearMulRight\_hom\_snd} expose the first/second-coordinate
59	        identifications \(\sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} \circ \pr_1 = \pr_1\) and
60	        \(\sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} \circ \pr_2 = \mu\). Closed iter-134;
61	        consumer (piece (i.b) Step 2 chain) DELETED iter-145 under the
62	        chart-algebra pivot, leaving these as standalone categorical
63	        helpers. Iter-146+ cleanup candidate; preserved iter-145 per the
64	        refactor directive's \texttt{private}-or-public-discipline rule.
65	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.schemeHomRingCompatibility} ---
66	        packaging helper around \(((\mathrm{adj}).\mathrm{homEquiv}\,\_\,\_).\mathrm{symm}\,f.c\),
67	        the adjunction-transpose compatibility morphism of structure presheaves
68	        of rings along a scheme morphism \(f \colon Y \to Z\). Note: this is

1	\chapter{The relative cotangent presheaf}
2	\label{chap:differentials}
3	
4	This chapter develops the relative cotangent \emph{presheaf} \(\Omega_{X/S}\) of a morphism of schemes \(f \colon X \to S\), starting from the ring-theoretic K\"ahler differential module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) already available in Mathlib. The output is a presheaf of \(\struct{X}\)-modules whose sections on each open \(V \subseteq X\) are the K\"ahler differential module of the ring map \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V) \to \struct{X}(V)\) induced by \(f\), where \(f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\) denotes the \emph{presheaf-level} inverse-image functor (left Kan extension along \((\mathrm{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base}).\mathrm{op}\)).
5	
6	The downstream role of this chapter is to provide the \emph{forward direction} of the standard smoothness criterion: smoothness of \(f\) implies that on every affine chart \(V \to U\) (with \(V \subseteq X\), \(U \subseteq S\) affine and \(f V \subseteq U\)) the appLE-algebra K\"ahler differential \(\Omega_{\Gamma(X, V)\,/\,\Gamma(S, U)}\) is a free \(\Gamma(X, V)\)-module of rank \(n\). This is the Jacobian-criterion direction and is what connects to the dimension hypothesis on the Jacobian: the smoothness of the curve \(C \to \Spec k\) in the Jacobian definition is the special case \(n = 1\), and the relative dimension of \(\Jac(C)\) is read off from the rank via the same forward implication. The bridge between this affine-chart-wise algebra-K\"ahler form and the section module of the relative cotangent presheaf \(\Omega_{X/S}\) was the former content of milestone~M1; the bridge declaration was excised iter-126 (zero in-tree consumers) and replaced in this chapter by the standalone K\"ahler-localization utilities of Section~REF, the off-loop Mathlib-PR work of which proceeds from \texttt{analogies/relative-differentials-presheaf-bridge.md}. The converse implication of the forward smoothness criterion is \emph{mathematically false} as stated and is documented separately in Section~REF.
7	
8	\section{The relative cotangent presheaf}
9	
10	\begin{definition}
11	
12	\leanok
13	[Relative cotangent presheaf of a morphism]
14	  \label{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}
15	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf}
16	  Let \(f \colon X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes. The presheaf-level inverse-image presheaf of rings \(f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S}\) on \(X\) (the left Kan extension of \(\struct{S}\) along \((\mathrm{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base}).\mathrm{op}\), given on each open \(V \subseteq X\) by the colimit \(\mathrm{colim}_{W : \,f V \subseteq W \subseteq S}\,\struct{S}(W)\)) is paired with \(\struct{X}\) by the canonical adjunction-transpose \(f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S} \to \struct{X}\) of the comorphism \(f^{\sharp}\colon\struct{S}\to f_{*}\struct{X}\). Applying the presheaf-of-modules K\"ahler differential construction \(\mathrm{relativeDifferentials}'\) to this map of presheaves of commutative rings produces a presheaf of \(\struct{X}\)-modules on \(X\), the \emph{relative cotangent presheaf} \(\Omega_{X/S}\).
17	\end{definition}
18	
19	\begin{lemma}
20	
21	\leanok
22	[Sections of \(\Omega_{X/S}\) on an open]
23	  \label{lem:relative_kaehler_presheaf_obj}
24	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_obj_kaehler}
25	  
26	  For every open \(V \subseteq X\), the sections of the relative cotangent presheaf are, by construction, the K\"ahler differential module of the ring map \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V) \to \struct{X}(V)\) induced by \(f\):
27	  \[
28	    \Omega_{X/S}(V) \;=\; \Omega_{\struct{X}(V) \,/\, (f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V)}.
29	  \]
30	  Here \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V) = \mathrm{colim}_{W : \,f V \subseteq W \subseteq S}\,\struct{S}(W)\) is the presheaf-level inverse-image section ring, \emph{not} \(\struct{S}(U)\) for an arbitrary affine open \(U \supseteq f V\). The relationship between this colimit-source K\"ahler module and the classical ring-theoretic \(\Omega_{B/A}\) for \(A = \struct{S}(U)\), \(B = \struct{X}(V)\) on an affine chart \(V = \Spec B \subseteq f^{-1}U\) with \(U = \Spec A \subseteq S\) affine is the content of the bridge documented in Section~REF.
31	\end{lemma}
32	
33	\begin{proof}
34	  
35	  \leanok
36	  By definition of \(\mathrm{relativeDifferentials}'\): the construction is built object-wise to be the Mathlib \texttt{KaehlerDifferential} module of the ring map at each open. The identification is therefore by \texttt{rfl} after unfolding the definition. The colimit description of \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V)\) is the definition of \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.pullback} as the left Kan extension along \((\mathrm{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base}).\mathrm{op}\) in Mathlib.
37	\end{proof}
38	
39	\begin{remark}[Comparison with Mathlib's ring-level \(\Omega_{B/A}\)]
40	  \label{rem:kahler_compatibility}
41	  Mathlib provides the ring-theoretic K\"ahler differential module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) together with the universal derivation \(\mathrm{d} \colon B \to \Omega_{B/A}\). The construction of \(\Omega_{X/S}\) on each open is, by definition, this Mathlib module applied at the ring level of the presheaf, so all ring-level Mathlib lemmas about \(\Omega_{B/A}\) transfer verbatim under Lemma~REF — but for the \emph{colimit-source} ring \((f^{-1}_{\mathrm{psh}}\struct{S})(V)\), not for \(\struct{S}(U)\). Transferring to the classical \(A = \struct{S}(U)\) source on an affine chart is the bridge of Section~REF.
42	\end{remark}
43	
44	\section{Smoothness criterion for \(\Omega_{X/S}\) (forward direction)}
45	
46	The forward direction of the textbook smoothness criterion identifies a necessary condition on the relative K\"ahler differentials: every point of \(X\) admits an affine chart \(V \to U\) over which the appLE-algebra K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) is free of the prescribed rank, with \(A = \struct{S}(U)\) and \(B = \struct{X}(V)\). This is the global version of the algebraic-side fact that an \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~algebra has a free K\"ahler differential module of rank \(n\). We state and prove the theorem directly on the appLE-algebra K\"ahler module, sidestepping the presheaf bridge of Section~REF. The converse implication (a local-freeness hypothesis on \(\Omega\) alone implying smoothness) is \emph{false}; see Section~REF.
47	
48	\begin{theorem}\leanok
49	[Smooth implies \(\Omega\) locally free (algebra-K\"ahler form)]
50	  \label{thm:smooth_locally_free_omega}
51	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.smooth_locally_free_omega}
52	  
53	  Let \(f \colon X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(n \in \mathbb{N}\). If \(f\) is smooth of relative dimension \(n\), then for every point \(x \in X\) there exist an affine open \(U \subseteq S\), an affine open \(V \subseteq X\) with \(x \in V\) and \(V \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\), such that the K\"ahler differential module
54	  \[
55	    \Omega_{\Gamma(X, V) \,/\, \Gamma(S, U)}
56	  \]
57	  (over the appLE algebra structure \(\Gamma(S, U) \to \Gamma(X, V)\) induced by \(f\)) is a free \(\Gamma(X, V)\)-module of rank \(n\).
58	\end{theorem}
59	
60	\begin{proof}
61	  
62	  \leanok
63	  Fix \(x \in X\). We use the abbreviation \(A := \Gamma(S, U)\) and \(B := \Gamma(X, V)\) for the section rings on the affine chart we will produce; the algebra map \(A \to B\) is the appLE map \((f.\mathrm{appLE}\,U\,V\,e).\mathrm{hom}\) induced by \(f\).
64	
65	  \emph{Step 1: extract a standard-smooth chart at \(x\).} The auto-generated \texttt{mk\_iff} bridge
66	  \[
67	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.smoothOfRelativeDimension\_iff} \quad [\text{verified}]
68	  \]
69	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Smooth}; produced by the \texttt{@[mk\_iff]} attribute on the class \texttt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}) unfolds the hypothesis \texttt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~\(f\) into: for every \(x \in X\), there exist affine opens \(U \subseteq S\) and \(V \subseteq X\) with \(x \in V\) and \(V \leq f^{-1}U\), such that the induced algebra map \((f.\mathrm{appLE}\,U\,V\,e).\mathrm{hom}\colon A \to B\) satisfies \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~\(A\)~\(B\).
70	
71	  \emph{Step 2: pass to plain standard-smooth.} The lemma
72	  \[
73	    \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.isStandardSmooth} \quad [\text{verified}]
74	  \]
75	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmooth}) yields \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth}~\(A\)~\(B\) from the relative-dimension-tagged form, unlocking the cotangent-module machinery in the next two steps.
76	
77	  \emph{Step 3: free K\"ahler module on the chart.} The Mathlib instance
78	  \[
79	    \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} \quad [\text{verified}]
80	  \]
81	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothCotangent}) synthesises \texttt{Module.Free}~\(B\)~\(\Omega_{B/A}\) from \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth}~\(A\)~\(B\).
82	
83	  \emph{Step 4: pin the rank.} The theorem
84	  \[
85	    \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} \quad [\text{verified}]
86	  \]
87	  (same file as Step 3) gives \(\rank_B\,\Omega_{B/A} = n\) under the hypotheses \texttt{Nontrivial}~\(B\) and \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}~\(n\)~\(A\)~\(B\).
88	
89	  \emph{Step 4.5: discharge the \texttt{Nontrivial}~\(B\) side condition.} The chart witness \(x \in V\) shows that the open \(V \subseteq X\) is non-empty as a set, so its structure-sheaf section ring \(B = \Gamma(X, V) = \struct{X}(V)\) is non-zero by \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.component\_nontrivial} (the structure sheaf of a scheme has \texttt{Nontrivial} sections on a non-empty open). This supplies the \texttt{Nontrivial}~\(B\) hypothesis required by Step 4.
90	
91	  Combining Steps 1--4.5, the chart \((U, V, e)\) produced by Step 1 satisfies \(x \in V\), \(\mathrm{IsAffineOpen}\,U\), \(\mathrm{IsAffineOpen}\,V\), and under the appLE algebra structure \(A = \Gamma(S, U) \to B = \Gamma(X, V)\), the K\"ahler module \(\Omega_{B/A}\) is a free \(B\)-module of rank \(n\). As \(x \in X\) was arbitrary, the statement follows.
92	
93	  This is the forward direction of the Jacobian criterion: Stacks Tag~02G1 (and Hartshorne~II.8, Theorem~II.5.2 in the Noetherian case).
94	
95	  \emph{Mathlib name summary.} The five \texttt{[verified]} closure pieces the proof relies on are
96	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.smoothOfRelativeDimension\_iff} (Step 1),
97	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.isStandardSmooth} (Step 2),
98	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} (Step 3),
99	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} (Step 4),
100	  and \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.component\_nontrivial} (Step 4.5).
101	\end{proof}
102	
103	\begin{remark}[Lean naming]
104	  \label{rem:smooth_class_naming}
105	  The Lean class for ``smooth of relative dimension \(n\)'' is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.SmoothOfRelativeDimension}; the spelling \texttt{IsSmoothOfRelativeDimension} that appeared in earlier iterations of this project is a deprecated alias in Mathlib \texttt{b80f227}. The \texttt{\textbackslash lean\{...\}} hint above names the non-deprecated form.
106	\end{remark}

108	\section{Standalone K\"ahler-localization utilities (post-iter-126 M1 excise)}
109	\label{sec:bridge}
110	
111	The presheaf-form \(\Omega_{X/S}\) of Definition~REF and the algebra-K\"ahler form \(\Omega_{B / A}\) on an affine chart of Theorem~REF are connected, on an affine chart, by a canonical \(B\)-linear isomorphism. Earlier iterations of the project (iter-121 through iter-125) hosted this isomorphism in the Lean tree as a \emph{bridge} declaration \texttt{relativeDifferentialsPresheaf\_equiv\_kaehler\_appLE} backed by a Mathlib gap-fill sub-lemma \texttt{appLE\_isLocalization} (the load-bearing localization-at-the-image-submonoid claim, packaged as a multi-LOC residual sorry whose closure was concrete but had no in-tree consumer). The forward-direction smoothness criterion (Theorem~REF) closed iter-120 in algebra-K\"ahler form \emph{independently} of the bridge, so the bridge was project-local infrastructure with zero downstream consumer.
112	
113	\textbf{Excise decision (iter-126).} Per the iter-126 strategy-critic CHALLENGE, ratified by the iter-126 user hint endorsing "shortest path qualified by your role is to fill mathlib gaps", the bridge declaration and its load-bearing sub-lemma were excised from the Lean tree iter-126: closing the bridge produced neither a downstream consumer nor a Mathlib PR (the extractable PR candidate is M1.d — Lemma~REF below — and is \emph{independent} of the bridge). The two retained declarations in this section are the standalone K\"ahler-localization utilities that survive as upstream-PR-quality material; off-loop PR work proceeds from \texttt{analogies/relative-differentials-presheaf-bridge.md}.
114	
115	\begin{lemma}\leanok
116	[K\"ahler module of a localization is trivial]
117	  \label{lem:kaehler_localization_subsingleton}
118	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.kaehler_localization_subsingleton}
119	  Let \(A \to L\) be a localization at a multiplicative subset \(M \subseteq A\). Then \(\Omega_{L / A}\) is subsingleton (the zero \(L\)-module).
120	\end{lemma}
121	
122	\begin{proof}
123	
124	\leanok
125	  This is a thin re-export of Mathlib's existing infrastructure: \texttt{Algebra.FormallyUnramified.of\_isLocalization}~\texttt{[verified]} (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Unramified.Basic}, line 303) produces the instance \texttt{Algebra.FormallyUnramified}~\(A\)~\(L\) from any \texttt{IsLocalization}~\(M\)~\(L\) predicate; the instance \texttt{Algebra.FormallyUnramified.subsingleton\_kaehlerDifferential}~\texttt{[verified]} (same file, lines 57--59) then yields \texttt{Subsingleton}~\((\mathrm{KaehlerDifferential}~A~L)\). Hence the project-side lemma is a two-line re-export (\(\mathtt{haveI} := \mathtt{Algebra.FormallyUnramified.of\_isLocalization}~M\) \texttt{; infer\_instance}); contra an earlier framing, no Mathlib gap-fill is required for this lemma.
126	\end{proof}
127	
128	\begin{lemma}\leanok
129	[Tower-cancellation equivalence for K\"ahler modules under localization]
130	  \label{lem:kaehler_quotient_localization_iso}
131	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.kaehler_quotient_localization_iso}
132	  
133	  Let \(A \to L\) be a localization (in the sense of \texttt{IsLocalization}), and let \(L \to B\) be a ring map forming a scalar tower \(A \to L \to B\). Then the canonical \(B\)-linear map \(\Omega_{B/A} \to \Omega_{B/L}\) induced by the tower is a \(B\)-linear equivalence.
134	\end{lemma}
135	
136	\begin{proof}
137	  
138	  \leanok
139	  The second fundamental exact sequence of K\"ahler differentials reads
140	  \[
141	    B \otimes_{L} \Omega_{L/A} \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{B/A} \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \Omega_{B/L} \;\longrightarrow\; 0.
142	  \]
143	  By Lemma~REF, the left term vanishes (since \(\Omega_{L/A}\) is subsingleton and tensoring against it gives the zero module). Therefore the right surjection has zero kernel and is a \(B\)-linear equivalence. The Mathlib closure pieces are \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.exact\_mapBaseChange\_map}~\texttt{[verified]} (the exact sequence above) and \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.map\_surjective}~\texttt{[verified]} (the right-hand surjection), both in \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Kaehler.Basic}; the project-side declaration packages these into a \texttt{LinearEquiv} via \texttt{LinearEquiv.ofBijective}. This is the most extractable Mathlib contribution candidate of milestone~M1: a clean generalisation of \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.tensorKaehlerEquivOfFormallyEtale} to the ``only the lower step is formally unramified'' case, candidate name \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.equivOfFormallyUnramified}.
144	\end{proof}
145	
146	\begin{remark}[Mathlib contribution candidate (preserved standalone post-excise)]
147	  \label{rem:bridge_mathlib_pr}
148	  Per the analogist's analysis (\texttt{analogies/relative-differentials-presheaf-bridge.md}, iter-121), the extractable Mathlib contribution candidate is Lemma~REF: a clean generalisation of \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.tensorKaehlerEquivOfFormallyEtale} to the ``only the lower step is formally unramified'' case (candidate name \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.equivOfFormallyUnramified}, candidate Mathlib home \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Kaehler.Basic}). This lemma survives the iter-126 M1 excise as standalone PR-ready material; the bridge declaration and its scheme-morphism-shaped \texttt{appLE\_isLocalization} sub-lemma were excised iter-126 because they had zero in-tree consumers, but the Mathlib-PR work proceeds off-loop from the analogist's file.
149	\end{remark}
