136	\section{The invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)}
137	
138	\begin{definition}\leanok
139	[Invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) of a Weil divisor]
140	  \label{def:sheafOf}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf}
142	  
143	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
144	  % sheaf \(\mathscr L(D)\) associated to a Cartier divisor on a scheme),
145	  % and Remark~6.17.1, Proposition~6.18 + Proposition~6.15, p.~145
146	  % (Cartier \(\leftrightarrow\) Weil correspondence on an integral
147	  % locally factorial scheme; identification of \(\mathscr L(D)\) as a
148	  % subsheaf of the constant sheaf at the function field \(K\)). Read from
149	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161--162,
150	  % transcribed character-by-character from rendered scanned page
151	  % images (the PDF has no text layer).
152	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(D\) be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
153	  % \(X\), represented by \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) as above. We define a subsheaf
154	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) of the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathscr K\) by
155	  % taking \(\mathscr L(D)\) to be the sub-\(\mathscr O_X\)-module of
156	  % \(\mathscr K\) generated by \(f_i^{-1}\) on \(U_i\). This is well-defined,
157	  % since \(f_i/f_j\) is invertible on \(U_i \cap U_j\), so \(f_i^{-1}\) and
158	  % \(f_j^{-1}\) generate the same \(\mathscr O_X\)-module. We call
159	  % \(\mathscr L(D)\) the \emph{sheaf associated to \(D\)}."
160	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.15. \emph{If \(X\) is an integral
161	  % scheme, the homomorphism \(\mathrm{CaCl}\, X \to \mathrm{Pic}\, X\) of
162	  % (6.14) is an isomorphism.} Proof. We have only to show that every
163	  % invertible sheaf is isomorphic to a subsheaf of \(\mathscr K\), which
164	  % in this case is the constant sheaf \(K\), where \(K\) is the function
165	  % field of \(X\)."
166	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Remark 6.17.1. Clearly this gives a 1-1
167	  % correspondence between effective Cartier divisors on \(X\) and
168	  % \emph{locally principal} closed subschemes \(Y\), i.e., subschemes
169	  % whose sheaf of ideals is locally generated by a single element.
170	  % Note also that if \(X\) is an integral separated noetherian locally
171	  % factorial scheme, so that the Cartier divisors correspond to Weil
172	  % divisors by (6.11), then the effective Cartier divisors correspond
173	  % exactly to the effective Weil divisors."
174	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (the definition of
175	  \(\mathscr L(D)\)), Proposition~6.15 (the constant-sheaf-at-\(K\)
176	  identification on an integral scheme), and Remark~6.17.1 (Weil
177	  divisors and Cartier divisors agree on an integral separated
178	  noetherian locally factorial scheme).}
179	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
180	  \(\bar k\) and let \(D = \sum_{Q} n_Q [Q] \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor
181	  on \(C\), with the formal sum indexed by prime divisors \(Q\) of \(C\) and
182	  supported on a finite set \(\{Q : n_Q \neq 0\}\)
183	  (REF). The \emph{invertible sheaf
184	  \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to \(D\)}, also written
185	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) := \mathscr L(D)\) in Hartshorne's notation, is the
186	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of the constant sheaf
187	  \(\mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\) (Proposition~6.15: on an integral scheme the
188	  sheaf of total quotient rings is the constant sheaf at the function
189	  field) defined section-wise by
190	  \[
191	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(D)\bigr) \;:=\;
192	    \{\, 0 \,\} \;\cup\;
193	    \bigl\{\, f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\;
194	      \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; -\,n_Q \text{ for every prime divisor }
195	      Q \text{ of } C \text{ with } Q \in U \bigr\}
196	  \]
197	  for each open \(U \subseteq C\). Restriction along \(V \hookrightarrow U\)
198	  is the identity on the underlying carrier \(K(C)\), which preserves
199	  membership (a smaller open imposes \emph{fewer} order constraints, so
200	  the constraint set on \(U\) embeds into the constraint set on \(V\)). The
201	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on
202	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) is restricted from the
203	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on \(K(C)\) (a \(\bar k\)-scalar multiple of a
204	  rational function has the same order at every prime divisor).
205	
206	  \paragraph{Equivalence with the Cartier-divisor description.} The
207	  formal sum \(D = \sum_Q n_Q [Q]\) packages, on a curve where prime
208	  divisors are exactly closed points and the local rings at prime
209	  divisors are DVRs (smoothness \(+\) dimension~\(1\)), the Cartier-divisor
210	  data \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) of Hartshorne's definition: locally on a small
211	  open \(U_i\) containing exactly one prime divisor \(Q\) in the support of
212	  \(D\), pick a uniformiser \(\pi_Q \in K(C)\) at \(Q\) and set
213	  \(f_i := \pi_Q^{n_Q}\); on opens disjoint from the support of \(D\), set
214	  \(f_i := 1\). The transitions on overlaps \(U_i \cap U_j\) are units in
215	  \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}^{\times}\) for each prime \(Q\) in the overlap (any
216	  two uniformisers differ by a unit, by the DVR structure), so the
217	  \(\{(U_i, f_i)\}\) define a Cartier divisor whose Weil-divisor image
218	  under Hartshorne's (6.11) is the original \(D\). The
219	  ``\(f_i^{-1}\)-generated'' subsheaf of \(\mathcal K_C\) from Hartshorne's
220	  definition then coincides with the section-wise description above:
221	  the local generator \(f_i^{-1} = \pi_Q^{-n_Q}\) has order \(-n_Q\) at \(Q\),
222	  so the local sections are exactly \(f \in K(C)\) with
223	  \(\ord_Q(f \cdot \pi_Q^{n_Q}) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q\), on
224	  \(U_i\).
225	
226	  Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) (quoted in
227	  REF of REF)
228	  then guarantees that the resulting subsheaf $\mathcal O_C(D) \subset
229	  \mathcal K_C\( is an invertible sheaf on \)C$, independently of the
230	  choice of uniformisers \(\pi_Q\) (any two choices differ by a unit, so
231	  they generate the same \(\mathcal O_C\)-submodule).
232	
233	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
234	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf} takes a Weil
235	  divisor \(D : C.\text{left.WeilDivisor}\) and returns a
236	  \(\texttt{Sheaf}~J~(\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\) on
237	  \(C.\text{left.toTopCat}\), where
238	  \(J = \texttt{Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat}\) is the
239	  standard topology on the topological space underlying \(C\). The
240	  closed-point specialisation \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) is
241	  REF of
242	  REF; their agreement at \(D = [P]\) is
243	  REF below. The construction follows
244	  the iter-182 \texttt{ocofp-sheaf-internalhom} recipe in
245	  \texttt{analogies/}: presheaf-by-hand via per-open
246	  \(\bar k\)-submodules of the function field, sheaf property reduced to
247	  the locality of the order conditions at the stalks of the prime
248	  divisors. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
249	\end{definition}

251	\section{Immediate corollaries}
252	
253	\begin{lemma}\leanok
254	[Sheaf of the zero divisor is the structure sheaf]
255	  \label{lem:sheafOf_zero}
256	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_zero}
257	  
258	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
259	  \(\bar k\). Then the invertible sheaf associated to the zero divisor
260	  \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is canonically the structure sheaf:
261	  \[
262	    \mathcal O_C(0) \;=\; \mathcal O_C
263	  \]
264	  as sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\), equivalently as
265	  objects in \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
266	\end{lemma}
267	
268	\begin{proof}
269	  
270	  At \(D = 0\) the coefficient \(n_Q = 0\) at every prime divisor \(Q\), so the
271	  section condition of REF reduces, on every open
272	  \(U \subseteq C\), to
273	  \[
274	    \Gamma\bigl(U, \mathcal O_C(0)\bigr)
275	    \;=\; \{ 0 \} \cup \{ f \in K(C)^{\times} \;|\;
276	      \ord_Q(f) \geq 0 \text{ for every prime divisor } Q \in U \}.
277	  \]
278	  By the local description of the structure sheaf on the integral
279	  scheme \(C\) (the regular functions on \(U\) are exactly the rational
280	  functions whose order is non-negative at every prime divisor of
281	  \(C\) landing in \(U\); this is the standard ``non-negative order \(=\)
282	  regular'' identification, e.g.\ Hartshorne~II~\S6 immediately before
283	  Proposition~6.11), the right-hand side is precisely
284	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)\). The restriction maps on both sides are
285	  the identity on \(K(C)\), so the equality of presheaves promotes to an
286	  equality of sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\).
287	\end{proof}
288	
289	\begin{lemma}\leanok
290	[Sheaf at a single closed point is the line bundle of
291	REF]
292	  \label{lem:sheafOf_singlePoint}
293	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_singlePoint}
294	  
295	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
296	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point, viewed as a Weil divisor
297	  \([P] \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) via REF. Then the
298	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) of REF agrees on
299	  the nose with the line bundle of the closed point
300	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) of REF:
301	  \[
302	    \mathcal O_C(D)\bigl|_{D = [P]}
303	    \;=\; \texttt{lineBundleAtClosedPoint}_{P}
304	  \]
305	  as sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\), equivalently as
306	  objects in \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
307	\end{lemma}
308	
309	\begin{proof}
310	  
311	  Unfold both definitions. On the \texttt{sheafOf} side, the formal sum
312	  \([P]\) has coefficients \(n_Q = 1\) if \(Q = P\) and \(n_Q = 0\) otherwise
313	  (where \(P\) is identified with the prime divisor it determines by
314	  REF). Thus
315	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) contains \(0\) and those
316	  \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) satisfying \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\) for every prime
317	  divisor \(Q \in U\) with \(Q \neq P\), plus \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\) if
318	  \(P \in U\).
319	
320	  On the REF side, the same conditions
321	  are exactly the ones extracted by
322	  REF of
323	  \texttt{RR.3}, at every open \(U\) (not only at the global section
324	  \(U = C\)): a rational function \(f \in K(C)\) is a section of
325	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) on \(U\) iff it lies in the structure sheaf at every
326	  \(Q \in U \setminus \{P\}\) and has at most a simple pole at \(P\) when
327	  \(P \in U\).
328	
329	  Restriction maps on both sides are the identity on \(K(C)\). The two
330	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\) are therefore equal
331	  section-by-section as subsets of \(K(C)\), and the equality propagates
332	  to an equality of sheaves of \(\mathcal O_C\)-modules. The
333	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on both sides is the restriction of the
334	  \(\bar k\)-module structure on \(K(C)\), so the identification is
335	  \(\bar k\)-linear on each section.
336	\end{proof}

338	\begin{lemma}\leanok
339	[Short exact sequence for \(D \rightsquigarrow D + [Y]\)]
340	  \label{lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add}
341	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_ses_single_add}
342	  
343	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, p.~296 (the inductive step of the proof
344	  % of Theorem~1.3, ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any
345	  % point...''). Read from
346	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313,
347	  % transcribed character-by-character from the rendered scanned page
348	  % image (the PDF has no text layer).
349	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
350	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
351	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
352	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
353	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
354	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
355	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\).
356	  % (Since \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by
357	  % it does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\).)"
358	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
359	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
360	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
361	  \(\bar k\), let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) be a Weil divisor, and let \(P \in C\) be
362	  a closed point (equivalently, a prime divisor
363	  REF on a smooth curve). Then the inclusion
364	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) of
365	  sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-modules of \(\mathcal K_C\) fits into a short exact
366	  sequence
367	  \[
368	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
369	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
370	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
371	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
372	  \]
373	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\) (equivalently, in
374	  \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\)), where \(k(P)\) denotes the
375	  skyscraper sheaf at \(P\) with stalk \(\bar k\) (the residue field of \(P\)
376	  is \(\bar k\) since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and \(P\) is a
377	  \(\bar k\)-rational closed point).
378	\end{lemma}
379	
380	\begin{proof}
381	  
382	  Hartshorne's argument (IV.1.3, the inductive step quoted above)
383	  proceeds in two beats:
384	
385	  \emph{Beat 1 (the ideal-sheaf SES at \(P\)).} The closed-subscheme
386	  inclusion \(P \hookrightarrow C\) has structure sheaf
387	  \(\mathcal O_P \cong k(P)\) (a skyscraper at \(P\) with stalk \(\bar k\)),
388	  and Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.18 identifies its ideal sheaf as
389	  \(\mathcal I_P = \mathcal O_C(-[P])\) (the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
390	  \(\mathcal K_C\) generated by a local uniformiser \(\pi_P\) at \(P\) and by
391	  \(1\) away from \(P\), i.e.\ the locally-principal ideal of the locally
392	  principal closed subscheme \(P\)). This produces the structural short
393	  exact sequence
394	  \[
395	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P])
396	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C
397	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
398	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0
399	  \]
400	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\).
401	
402	  \emph{Beat 2 (tensor with \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\)).} The line bundle
403	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is locally free of rank~\(1\)
404	  (Hartshorne~II.6.13(a), REF), so $- \otimes_{\mathcal O_C}
405	  \mathcal O_C(D + [P])\( is exact on \)\mathbf{Coh}(C)$ and preserves
406	  the short exact sequence of Beat~1:
407	  \[
408	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes
409	      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
410	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
411	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
412	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0.
413	  \]
414	  Three identifications collapse this to the claimed sequence:
415	  \begin{itemize}
416	    \item $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
417	      \mathcal O_C(D)$ (tensor product of line bundles is the
418	      \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\)-additive operation $D' + D'' \mapsto \mathcal O_C(D' +
419	      D'')$ via Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(b), which is in turn the
420	      ring-theoretic identity
421	      $\pi_P^{-1} \cdot \pi_P^{1 + n_P^{D}} \cdot \prod_{Q \neq P}
422	      \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}} = \prod_Q \pi_Q^{n_Q^{D}}$ on the generating
423	      rational functions, where \(n_Q^{D}\) is the coefficient of \(Q\)
424	      in \(D\));
425	    \item $\mathcal O_C \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
426	      \mathcal O_C(D + [P])$ (the structure sheaf is the tensor unit);
427	    \item \(k(P) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong k(P)\) (tensoring a
428	      skyscraper at \(P\) by a locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf preserves the
429	      skyscraper; concretely, at the stalk over \(P\) the line bundle
430	      \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])_P\) is a free \(\mathcal O_{C,P}\)-module of
431	      rank one, and $k(P) \otimes_{\mathcal O_{C,P}} \mathcal O_{C,P}
432	      \cong k(P)\( is just \)k(P)\(; away from \)P$ the skyscraper is the
433	      zero sheaf, on which the tensor is the zero sheaf).
434	  \end{itemize}
435	  Substituting yields
436	  \[
437	    0 \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D)
438	    \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
439	    \;\longrightarrow\; k(P)
440	    \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
441	  \]
442	  exact in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\), as required.
443	
444	  \emph{Compatibility with the structural inclusion.} The first map
445	  \(\mathcal O_C(D) \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is the
446	  inclusion of subsheaves of \(\mathcal K_C\): at the level of sections,
447	  the constraint \(\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}\) of
448	  \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) implies the weaker constraint
449	  \(\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D} - 1\) of \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))\)
450	  when \(P \in U\) (constraints on \(Q \neq P\) are unchanged), and the map
451	  is identity on \(K(C)\) at the carrier level. The second map
452	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \twoheadrightarrow k(P)\) is the evaluation
453	  ``leading coefficient at the pole at \(P\)'' (i.e.\ the
454	  \(\pi_P^{-(n_P^{D} + 1)}\)-coefficient in the Laurent expansion of \(f\)
455	  at \(P\)), which is well-defined on \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D + [P]))\)
456	  modulo the subgroup of sections with \(\ord_P(f) \geq -n_P^{D}\) ---
457	  i.e.\ modulo \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\).
458	\end{proof}

460	\section{Sheaf-property correctness}
461	
462	The construction of REF is presheaf-by-hand: a
463	per-open \(\bar k\)-submodule of \(K(C)\), with restriction along
464	\(V \hookrightarrow U\) given by the identity on \(K(C)\). This section
465	sketches the verifications that make the construction land in the
466	target category \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\).
467	
468	\paragraph{Functoriality of the carrier.} For \(V \subseteq U\), the
469	constraint set defining \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) is a \emph{subset}
470	of the constraint set defining \(\Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))\) (fewer
471	prime divisors to constrain at), so the identity on \(K(C)\) restricts
472	to a well-defined inclusion
473	\(\Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D)) \hookrightarrow \Gamma(V, \mathcal O_C(D))\).
474	Composition \(W \subseteq V \subseteq U\) is again identity-on-\(K(C)\), so
475	strict functoriality holds.
476	
477	\paragraph{\(\bar k\)-linearity.} A \(\bar k\)-scalar \(\lambda \in \bar k\)
478	acts on \(K(C)\) by multiplication, and
479	\(\ord_Q(\lambda \cdot f) = \ord_Q(f)\) for \(\lambda \neq 0\)
480	(units have order zero); for \(\lambda = 0\), \(\lambda \cdot f = 0\) is
481	included by convention. Thus the constraint set is a
482	\(\bar k\)-submodule and the restriction maps are \(\bar k\)-linear.
483	
484	\paragraph{Sheaf property.} The order condition
485	``\(\ord_Q(f) \geq -n_Q\) for every prime divisor \(Q \in U\)'' is
486	\emph{stalk-local} at each \(Q\): it depends only on the germ of \(f\) at
487	the point of \(C\) underlying \(Q\), equivalently on the image of \(f\) in
488	the DVR \(\mathcal O_{C, Q}\). Consequently the sheaf property reduces
489	to two ingredients:
490	\begin{enumerate}
491	  \item \emph{The presheaf-of-sets underlying \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is a
492	    subpresheaf of the constant presheaf at \(K(C)\).} The constant
493	    presheaf at \(K(C)\) is a sheaf (every locally-constant section of a
494	    constant presheaf on a topological space is globally constant when
495	    the space is irreducible, which the topological space underlying an
496	    integral scheme is). A subpresheaf of a sheaf is itself a sheaf iff
497	    its membership condition is local; the order conditions are local
498	    at each prime divisor's underlying point, so the membership
499	    condition is local across an open cover and a section glued from
500	    locally-defined sections inherits the order condition at every
501	    prime divisor.
502	  \item \emph{The forget-functor bridge from
503	    \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\) to
504	    \(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Set})\).} The categorical forget
505	    \(\mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k} \to \mathbf{Set}\) creates limits, hence
506	    reflects the sheaf property: a presheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules is a
507	    sheaf iff its underlying presheaf of sets is. Combined with~(i),
508	    this delivers the sheaf property for the
509	    \(\bar k\)-module-valued \(\mathcal O_C(D)\).
510	\end{enumerate}
511	
512	\paragraph{Invertibility.} On any open \(U \subseteq C\) disjoint from
513	the support of \(D\), the constraint set is exactly the structure-sheaf
514	constraint \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\) for every prime divisor \(Q \in U\); hence
515	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U = \mathcal O_C|_U\), and the trivialisation
516	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \cong \mathcal O_C|_U\) is the identity. On a small
517	open \(U \subseteq C\) containing exactly one prime divisor \(Q\) from the
518	support of \(D\), the local generator \(\pi_Q^{-n_Q} \in K(C)\) trivialises
519	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U\) as a free rank-\(1\) \(\mathcal O_C|_U\)-module: a
520	section \(f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C(D))\) satisfies $\ord_Q(f) \geq
521	-n_Q\( iff \)\pi_Q^{n_Q} \cdot f \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal O_C)$, and the
522	``multiplication by \(\pi_Q^{n_Q}\)'' map is an
523	\(\mathcal O_C|_U\)-linear isomorphism
524	\(\mathcal O_C(D)|_U \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal O_C|_U\). The two types
525	of trivialising opens cover \(C\) (any prime divisor lies in at most
526	finitely many supports across a covering, and the support of \(D\) is
527	finite); patching the trivialisations on overlaps amounts to changing
528	uniformiser, which is unit-times the original (by the DVR property), so
529	the cocycle is in \(\mathcal O_C^{\times}\) and the sheaf is invertible.
530	
531	The closure of (i)+(ii) in Lean follows the project's existing
532	\texttt{Scheme.toModuleKPresheaf} /
533	\texttt{toModuleKPresheaf\_isSheaf} template in
534	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/}, which
535	landed the analogous bottom-up
536	\(\mathbf{Sh}(C, \mathbf{Mod}_{\bar k})\)-valued construction for the
537	structure sheaf. The order-condition locality is closed via the project's
538	\texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} stalk bridge of
539	\texttt{RR.1} (REF and surrounding lemmas).
540

570	    \texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_sheafOf\_succ} of \texttt{RR.2}.
571	\end{itemize}
572	
573	The iter-183 file-skeleton lane will create these four declarations as
574	typed sorries; the iter-184+ prover lanes will close the bodies along
575	the recipe sketched in \
576	(via the project's existing \texttt{toModuleKPresheaf} template) and
577	unblock both \texttt{RR.2}'s helper sorries and the \texttt{RR.3}
578	\(\mathcal O_C([P])\) identification.
579	
580	\section{Out of scope}
581	
582	\begin{itemize}
583	  \item \textbf{Linear-equivalence isomorphism.} The natural
584	    isomorphism \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when
585	    \(D \sim D'\) (i.e.\ \(D - D' = \div(f)\) for some
586	    \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)) is \emph{not} formalised in this chapter.
587	    It is not needed for the genus-\(0\) critical path: \texttt{RR.2}
588	    consumes only \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D))\) and the SES additivity at
589	    closed points; \texttt{RR.3} consumes only the closed-point
590	    specialisation; \texttt{RR.4} consumes the function-field /
591	    degree bridge, which is divisorial in \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) rather than
592	    in \(\mathrm{Pic}(C) = \mathrm{Div}(C) / \sim\). The
593	    Cartier-divisor \(\leftrightarrow\) \texttt{Pic} isomorphism is
594	    queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter.
595	  \item \textbf{Sheaf-multiplication
596	    $\mathcal O_C(D) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D') \cong \mathcal O_C(D +
597	    D')$.} The tensor-product identity of Hartshorne's
598	    Proposition~6.13(b) is invoked inside the proof of
599	    REF at the single instance
600	    $\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \otimes \mathcal O_C(D + [P]) \cong
601	    \mathcal O_C(D)$, but the \emph{statement} of
602	    Proposition~6.13(b) as a separate lemma is not formalised in this
603	    chapter. The instance used inside the SES proof is local to that
604	    proof; a generalised tensor-additivity lemma is queued for the
605	    post-RR-bridge follow-up.
606	  \item \textbf{Pullback of \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) along a morphism.} The
607	    pullback \(f^* \mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_{C'}(f^* D)\) for
608	    a morphism of curves \(f : C' \to C\), used in degree-computation
609	    arguments on a base change, is \emph{not} formalised in this
610	    chapter and is not needed for the genus-\(0\) critical path.
611	  \item \textbf{Global-sections identification as the Riemann--Roch
612	    space.} The identification
613	    \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \cong L(D)\) for a general Weil divisor
614	    \(D\) (i.e.\ the analogue of
615	    REF for general
616	    \(D\)) is queued for the post-RR-bridge follow-up. The closed-point
617	    case \(D = [P]\) is the only specialisation consumed by the
618	    genus-\(0\) critical path, and it is handled in
619	    \texttt{RR.3} via REF.
620	  \item \textbf{Serre duality and the canonical sheaf.} As recorded in
621	    the \texttt{RR.2} and \texttt{RR.3} chapters, Mathlib (snapshot
622	    \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf \(\omega_X\) on a scheme
623	    and no Serre duality theorem. The genus-\(0\) critical path
624	    does \emph{not} require either; the present chapter accordingly
625	    does not engage them.
626	\end{itemize}

15	\[
16	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
17	  \,\colon\,
18	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
19	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}

52	k$-rational point'' hypothesis of Hartshorne's Exercise~IV.1.3 is automatic

80	\section{The morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from two global sections}
81	\begin{lemma}
82	
83	
84	\leanok
85	[Morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from a globally generating pair of sections]
86	  \label{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}
87	  
88	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}
89	  Let \(X\) be a scheme over a field \(\bar k\), let \(\mathcal L\) be an invertible
90	  sheaf on \(X\), and let \(s_0, s_1 \in H^0(X, \mathcal L)\) be two global
91	  sections whose common zero locus is empty:
92	  \[
93	    \{x \in X : s_0(x) = 0 \text{ and } s_1(x) = 0\} \;=\; \emptyset.
94	  \]
95	  (Equivalently, \(s_0\) and \(s_1\) generate \(\mathcal L\) at every point.) Then
96	  there exists a unique morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
97	  \[
98	    \varphi_{(s_0, s_1)} \colon X \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
99	  \]
100	  whose pullback of \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(1)\) is \(\mathcal L\) and whose
101	  pullbacks of the standard coordinate sections $x_0, x_1 \in
102	  H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal O(1))\( are \)s_0\( and \)s_1$ respectively. On the
103	  basic-open chart $D_+(x_0) = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k} \subset
104	  \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\( the morphism \)\varphi_{(s_0, s_1)}\( is the map ``\)x
105	  \mapsto s_1(x) / s_0(x)\(''; on the chart \)D_+(x_1)\( it is the map ``\)x
106	  \mapsto s_0(x) / s_1(x)$''; the two definitions agree on the overlap
107	  \(D_+(x_0) \cap D_+(x_1) = \mathbb G_{m, \bar k}\) by the cocycle identity
108	  \((s_1 / s_0) \cdot (s_0 / s_1) = 1\).
109	\end{lemma}
110	\begin{proof}
111	
112	
113	
114	\leanok
115	  This is the standard linear-system construction in the form of
116	  Hartshorne's Theorem~II.7.1 (``Morphisms to \(\mathbb P^n\)''), specialised to
117	  \(n = 1\). Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the corresponding API
118	  under the name
119	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} in
120	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}:
121	  \begin{quote}
122	  \small
123	  \texttt{def Proj.fromOfGlobalSections \{\(\sigma\) A\} [CommRing A]
124	    [SetLike \(\sigma\) A] [AddSubgroupClass \(\sigma\) A] (\(\mathcal A\) :
125	    \(\mathbb N\) → \(\sigma\)) [GradedRing \(\mathcal A\)] \{X : Scheme\}\\
126	    \phantom{}\quad(f : A →+* X.presheaf.obj \(\top\))\\
127	    \phantom{}\quad(hf : Ideal.map f (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant \(\mathcal A\)).toIdeal = \(\top\)) :\\
128	    \phantom{}\quad(X \(\longrightarrow\) Proj \(\mathcal A\))}
129	  \end{quote}
130	  Specialising to \(\mathcal A = \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) with the standard $\mathbb
131	  N\(-grading by total degree, the inputs are a graded \)\bar k$-algebra
132	  homomorphism \(f \colon \bar k[x_0, x_1] \to H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) and a
133	  proof that the irrelevant ideal \((x_0, x_1) \cdot \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) maps to
134	  the unit ideal of \(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) (i.e.\ no common zeros). The
135	  resulting morphism lands in $\mathrm{Proj}\,\bar k[x_0, x_1] = \mathbb
136	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (Hartshorne~II, Example~7.1.3, the construction of \)\mathbb
137	  P^n\( as a \)\mathrm{Proj}\(). When \)\mathcal L$ is non-trivial the present
138	  lemma's signature is reduced to the Mathlib API by working on the open
139	  cover \(X = D(s_0) \cup D(s_1)\), where on each chart the sections become
140	  honest global functions (\(s_1 / s_0\) on \(D(s_0)\), \(s_0 / s_1\) on \(D(s_1)\),
141	  agreeing on the overlap). The chart-glue is performed by the
142	  project-bespoke wrapper
143	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}, whose body
144	  in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} chains
145	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} with the chart-restriction-and-glue
146	  pattern of \texttt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}.
147	  Uniqueness follows from the standard separation argument for morphisms to
148	  a separated scheme: two morphisms \(\varphi, \psi \colon X \to \mathbb P^1\)
149	  with \(\varphi^* x_i = \psi^* x_i\) for \(i = 0, 1\) agree on the basic-open
150	  charts \(D_+(x_0), D_+(x_1)\) (where they pull back the affine coordinates to
151	  the same rational function), and these charts cover \(\mathbb P^1\), so the
152	  two morphisms agree globally by gluing.
153	\end{proof}
154	\section{Degree of a morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via its pole divisor}
155	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase Pin 2 corrective; supersedes iter-182 narrative).
156	% `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \varphi` is the Weil divisor \(\varphi^*[\infty]\),
157	% defined as `positivePart (principal (\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))` in Lean.
158	% The supporting `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` def lives in
159	% `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` (\ref{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart}); the
160	% Hartshorne~II.6.9 identity that powers the existential degree statement
161	% lives there as \ref{lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}.
162	\begin{lemma}
163	
164	
165	\leanok
166	[Degree of a non-constant morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via the preimage of \(\infty\)]
167	  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}
168	  
169	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor}
170	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
171	  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
172	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
173	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
174	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
175	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
176	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.2, p.~299, opening of \S2.}
177	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism
178	  from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\). Then
179	  \(\varphi\) is finite, its function-field extension
180	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) (the canonical
181	  \(\varphi\)-induced map, supplied as an
182	  \texttt{[Algebra (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField C.functionField]}
183	  instance argument on the Lean side) is finite, and there exists a Weil
184	  divisor \(D \in \Div(C)\) such that
185	  \[
186	    D \;=\; \varphi^*[\infty]
187	    \quad\text{and}\quad
188	    \deg(D) \;=\; [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,].
189	  \]
190	  Equivalently, the morphism \(\varphi\) satisfies
191	  \[
192	    [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,] \;=\;
193	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
194	                  \ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [P]\,\right),
195	  \]
196	  where \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) is a local parameter at \(\infty\)
197	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
198	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)); and for any closed point
199	  \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) the same identification gives
200	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\).
201	  \emph{Convention on the pole divisor.} The Weil divisor
202	  \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) is exposed in Lean as
203	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \(\varphi\)}, a
204	  noncomputable definition introduced (as a typed \texttt{sorry}) at the
205	  iter-182 plan-phase signature strengthening of this lemma. The body of
206	  \texttt{poleDivisor} is queued for iter-183+ closure via the standard
207	  affine-chart construction (\texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia}
208	  in Mathlib, applied at each closed point \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
209	  to read off the local ramification multiplicity
210	  \(\ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty)\) from the maximal ideal at \(\infty\) in
211	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)). On the Lean side, the existential output of
212	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} packages
213	  \(D = \texttt{Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor}\;\varphi\) together with
214	  \(\deg(D) = \operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\),
215	  mirroring the OCofP pattern in which a typed-sorry def
216	  (\texttt{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}) is introduced
217	  upstream of its consumer lemma.
218	\end{lemma}
219	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase, supersedes iter-189 review NOTE):
220	% Pin 2 corrective COMMITTED — Option (a). `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` is
221	% being refactored to return the *positive part* of the principal divisor
222	% of `\varphi^{\#} t_\infty`, i.e. `(div(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))_0
223	% = \varphi^*[\infty]`. The supporting Weil-divisor `positivePart`
224	% definition lives in `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` as
225	% `def:WeilDivisor_positivePart`; the substantive Hartshorne~II.6.9
226	% identity that powers `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is now pinned as
227	% `lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` in `WeilDivisor.tex`.
228	% With these substrates in place, `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is
229	% mathematically correct as stated: the body's existential witness `D`
230	% is `Hom.poleDivisor \varphi = positivePart (principal (\varphi^{\#}
231	% t_\infty))`, and the degree identity is a one-line corollary of
232	% `lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`. iter-190 prover lane
233	% (Lane I) implements the refactor:
234	%   1. New def `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` in `WeilDivisor.lean`
235	%      (~10-15 LOC; lattice `D \sqcup 0`).
236	%   2. New lemma
237	%      `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` as a
238	%      named typed-sorry pin in `WeilDivisor.lean` (~5 LOC signature; body
239	%      ~50-80 LOC via affine-chart `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` route
240	%      owed iter-191+).
241	%   3. Refactor `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` body in `RationalCurveIso.lean`
242	%      from `principal (algebraMap _ _ t.val) halg` to
243	%      `positivePart (principal (algebraMap _ _ t.val) halg)` (~5 LOC).
244	%   4. Close `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` body via a 1-line
245	%      rewrite consuming (2) (~3 LOC).
246	% Net iter-190 Lane I: 1 axiom-clean closure (Pin 2 body, modulo named
247	% typed sorry on (2)); 1 NEW named typed-sorry pin
248	% (`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`) which is itself an
249	% iter-191+ Hartshorne II.6.9 substrate target. See iter-190 sidecar
250	% `iter/iter-190/plan.md` for the full rationale.
251	\begin{proof}
252	  \uses{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart, lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank, def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_degree, thm:principal_deg_zero}
253	  \leanok
254	  A non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is
255	  automatically finite. Indeed, the image of \(\varphi\) is a closed
256	  irreducible subset of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) that is not a point (else
257	  \(\varphi\) would be constant) and not empty, so it is all of $\mathbb
258	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (the only proper irreducible closed subset of \)\mathbb
259	  P^1_{\bar k}\( is a point); hence \)\varphi$ is surjective. A surjective
260	  morphism between smooth proper irreducible curves of equal dimension is
261	  finite (proper + quasi-finite, where quasi-finiteness comes from the
262	  fact that \(\varphi\) has zero-dimensional fibres: a positive-dimensional
263	  fibre would force a one-dimensional irreducible component in the fibre,
264	  contradicting irreducibility of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(C\) at
265	  generic points). Hence the function-field extension
266	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) = \varphi^\# K(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\)
267	  is finite.
268	  The identification \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\) for
269	  any closed point \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) is the multiplicativity of the
270	  degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9): for a finite morphism
271	  \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) of smooth proper curves and any divisor $D \in
272	  \Div(C')\(, \)\deg(\varphi^* D) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg(D)$. Specialising
273	  to \(D = [Q]\) (a divisor of degree \(1\) since \(\kappa(Q) = \bar k\)) gives
274	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = \deg(\varphi)\). Equivalently, the degree of
275	  \(\varphi\) equals the number of preimages of any closed point of $\mathbb
276	  P^1\(, counted with the ramification multiplicities \)\ord_P(\varphi^*
277	  t_\infty)\( at the preimage points \)P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)$.
278	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The lemma threads two auxiliary inputs:
279	  ``non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves is finite'' (the
280	  surjective-and-proper-and-quasi-finite argument above; Hartshorne~II.6.8)
281	  and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback'' (Hartshorne~II.6.9
282	  and the multiplicativity of \(\deg\) under composition of \(\div\) and
283	  \(\deg\); the closely related ``\(\mathrm{div}(f)\) has degree zero'' specialisation
284	  is \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}). Both are separable
285	  sub-lemmas inside \texttt{RR.4}'s Lean file
286	  (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) and are
287	  slotted for closure in a follow-up iteration after the
288	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} skeleton lands.
289	\end{proof}
290	\section{A degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism}
291	% NOTE (iter-181 Lane I signature refinement; see analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md
292	% Decision 2, verdict DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY). The iter-177 file-skeleton
293	% placeholder hypothesis "Nonempty (C'.functionField ≃+* C.functionField)"
294	% (an abstract function-field iso, unrelated to \(\varphi\)) was strictly
295	% weaker than the birational-extension body needs and was refined in
296	% iter-181 to the explicit function-field-extension-degree formulation
297	% [K(C):K(C')] = 1, i.e. Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField
298	% = 1, with C.functionField a C'.functionField-algebra via the canonical
299	% \varphi-induced map (Scheme.Hom.stalkMap at the generic point composed
300	% with IsFractionRing.lift). The proof-prose body below (the Hartshorne
301	% I.6.12 chain + scheme-theoretic alternative argument) is unchanged ---
302	% only the input shape changes.
303	% NOTE (iter-194 reviewer; refreshed iter-196): The Lean
304	% `iso_of_degree_one` body inline Step 1 was refactored iter-194 from

321	\begin{lemma}
322	
323	
324	\leanok
325	[Degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism]
326	  \label{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso}
327	  
328	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one}
329	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field-to-curve
330	  % equivalence of categories) (read from
331	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 62, doc p.~45)
332	  % + [Hartshorne], IV \S2 opening paragraph (degree of a finite morphism
333	  % of curves is the function-field extension degree) (read from
334	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
335	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.12. \emph{The following three categories are
336	  % equivalent:}
337	  % (i) \emph{nonsingular projective curves, and dominant morphisms;}
338	  % (ii) \emph{quasi-projective curves, and dominant rational maps;}
339	  % (iii) \emph{function fields of dimension 1 over \(k\), and \(k\)-homomorphisms.}"
340	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
341	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
342	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
343	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, I.6 Corollary~6.12 (p.~45) + IV.2 opening
344	  (p.~299).}
345	  Let \(C, C'\) be smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over an
346	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) be
347	  a non-constant morphism whose induced function-field extension is of
348	  degree one,
349	  \[
350	    [\,K(C) : K(C')\,] \;=\; 1,
351	  \]
352	  formally
353	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(C')} K(C) = 1\) in the Lean signature.
354	  Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
355	  \emph{Convention on the function-field map.} At each call-site of this
356	  lemma, the \(K(C')\)-algebra structure on \(K(C)\) used to interpret the
357	  degree \([K(C) : K(C')]\) is the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
358	  function-field map, i.e.\ the composite
359	  \[
360	    K(C') \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{germ}\;}\;
361	    \mathcal O_{C', \varphi(\xi_C)}
362	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\varphi^\#\;}\;
363	    \mathcal O_{C, \xi_C}
364	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{frac.}\,\text{lift}\;}\; K(C)
365	  \]
366	  of the germ at the image of the generic point \(\xi_C \in C\) with the
367	  stalk-map of \(\varphi\) at \(\xi_C\) and the fraction-field lift at
368	  \(\xi_C\) (per \texttt{analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md}, Decision~2). On
369	  the Lean side this canonical instance is carried as a
370	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
371	  to \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one}; consumers
372	  supply it explicitly at each call-site.
373	\end{lemma}
374	% NOTE (iter-189 review): Pin 3 Step 1 (function-field iso K(C') ≃+* K(C)
375	% from `Module.finrank = 1`) closed axiom-clean inline iter-189 (~10 LOC via
376	% `Subalgebra.bot_eq_top_of_finrank_eq_one` + `Algebra.surjective_algebraMap_iff`
377	% + `RingHom.injective` + `RingEquiv.ofBijective`). Step 2 (scheme-level lift
378	% via `Scheme.Hom.toNormalization`) remains a typed sorry in the body, with 4
379	% sub-obligations (a)-(d) documented inline at the sorry. Iter-190+ closure
380	% budget ~80-150 LOC per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md`.
381	\begin{proof}
382	
383	
384	
385	\leanok
386	  The function-field hypothesis \([K(C) : K(C')] = 1\) states that the
387	  canonical \(\varphi\)-induced map \(\varphi^\# \colon K(C') \to K(C)\) is a
388	  finite extension of degree one; since \(K(C')\) is a field and the
389	  extension has dimension one as a \(K(C')\)-vector space, \(\varphi^\#\) is
390	  an isomorphism of function fields \(K(C') \cong K(C)\). (Equivalently, by
391	  the IV.2-opening identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C):K(C')]\), this is
392	  the statement \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\); the morphism \(\varphi\) is finite in
393	  this case by the standard argument that a non-constant morphism of
394	  smooth proper curves is finite, identical to the
395	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} preamble.)
396	  By Hartshorne's Corollary~I.6.12, the assignment \(C \mapsto \bar k(C)\) is
397	  an equivalence of categories between smooth projective curves over $\bar
398	  k$ (with dominant morphisms) and function fields of transcendence degree
399	  one over \(\bar k\) (with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms). Under this equivalence,
400	  the isomorphism \(\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)\) produced by the previous
401	  paragraph lifts to a unique isomorphism of smooth proper curves $C \to
402	  C'\(, and this isomorphism agrees with \)\varphi$ on the generic point (by
403	  construction: it is the inverse-image of \(\varphi^\#\) under the
404	  equivalence). The two morphisms \(C \to C'\) that agree on the generic
405	  point of the integral scheme \(C\) agree everywhere (a morphism out of an
406	  integral scheme to a separated scheme is determined by its restriction
407	  to the generic point, Hartshorne~II Ex.~4.2). Hence \(\varphi\) is itself
408	  the lift, and is therefore an isomorphism.
409	  \emph{Alternative direct argument.} Without invoking the
410	  equivalence-of-categories framing, the same conclusion is obtained
411	  scheme-theoretically: a finite degree-\(1\) morphism $\varphi \colon C \to
412	  C'$ between integral normal noetherian schemes is an isomorphism.
413	  Indeed, the direct image \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C\) is a coherent
414	  \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module that is generically of rank $\deg(\varphi) =
415	  1\(, and an inclusion \)\mathcal O_{C'} \hookrightarrow \varphi_*
416	  \mathcal O_C\( of coherent rank-\)1$ sheaves whose generic ranks agree
417	  is an isomorphism over the locus where the quotient
418	  \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C / \mathcal O_{C'}\) has trivial stalk. The
419	  quotient is a torsion coherent \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module of generic rank
420	  \(\deg(\varphi) - 1 = 0\), hence is itself torsion-free (a
421	  finitely-generated torsion module over a Dedekind domain of generic
422	  rank zero is zero), so vanishes. Therefore $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C =
423	  \mathcal O_{C'}\( and \)\varphi$ is the structure morphism of an
424	  isomorphism. Cf.\ Stacks tag~\textbf{0AVX} for the categorical version.
425	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The Lean signature
426	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one} consumes a
427	  non-constant morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) together with a
428	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
429	  (the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced function-field map) and the
430	  function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis
431	  \texttt{Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField = 1}, and
432	  produces an isomorphism \(C \cong C'\) in the slice category
433	  \(\mathtt{Over (Spec (.of\ kbar))}\). The proof body threads the Mathlib
434	  API \texttt{IsIntegralHom.iso\_of\_isUnit} (or the equivalent
435	  \texttt{IsAffineHom + Finite + RankOne} chain) and, where needed, the
436	  project-bespoke ``finite-morphism-of-degree-one is an isomorphism''
437	  helper. The Hartshorne~I.6.12 equivalence-of-categories framing is the
438	  intended high-level proof structure; the scheme-theoretic argument
439	  above is the spelled-out form for the Lean body.
440	\end{proof}
441	\section{Headline: genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)}
442	\begin{theorem}
443	
444	\leanok
445	[Genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)]
446	  \label{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}
447	  
448	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
449	  % already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely
450	  % suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two distinct
451	  % points of \(X\) and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor \(D = P - Q\).
452	  % Since \(\deg(K - D) = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have \(l(K - D) = 0\),
453	  % and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of degree 0, so by
454	  % (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words \(P \sim Q\). But this implies
455	  % \(X\) is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
456	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
457	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, and let \(C\) be a smooth
458	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\).
459	  Then there exists an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
460	  \[
461	    \varphi \colon C \stackrel{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}.
462	  \]
463	  Concretely, the Lean signature
464	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} (the existing pinned
465	  target at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290}) is
466	  closed by the proof below; the existing interface block
467	  \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} of
468	  \texttt{chap:avr_for_rr} is its consumer-side restatement,
469	  pinning the same Lean declaration.
470	\end{theorem}
471	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the
472	% statement block above (Hartshorne states the classification and its
473	% proof inside the single Example; the proof is the second half beginning
474	% "We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) \ldots"). The present
475	% proof body restates Hartshorne's argument in the project's notation
476	% (linear-system construction \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C
477	% ([P]))\(, degree-\)1$ identification, isomorphism).
478	\begin{proof}
479	  Over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) the smooth proper
480	  geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) has closed points, all of which are
481	  \(\bar k\)-rational (the residue field of a closed point of a curve over
482	  \(\bar k\) is a finite extension of \(\bar k\), hence is \(\bar k\) itself by
483	  algebraic closedness). Fix one such \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P \in C\);
484	  this is the marked point used to build the linear system below.
485	  \emph{Step 1: non-constant function \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\).}
486	  By Hartshorne's Riemann--Roch in genus zero
487	  (\texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}) applied to the
488	  effective divisor \([P]\) of degree \(1\), we obtain
489	  \(\ell([P]) = \deg([P]) + 1 = 2\). The constant section
490	  \(1 \in \bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) accounts for a
491	  one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-subspace; the two-dimensional quotient supplies
492	  a non-constant section $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
493	  \bar k \cdot 1$. This is the corollary
494	  \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
495	  chapter, \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OCofP.tex}).
496	  \emph{Step 2: morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via the linear system \((1, f)\).}
497	  The pair \((1, f) \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))^2\) generates the
498	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) at every point of \(C\): at points
499	  \(Q \in C \setminus \{P\}\) the section \(1\) does not vanish in
500	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])_Q \cong \mathcal O_{C, Q}\), and at the marked point
501	  \(P\) the section \(f\) does not vanish in \(\mathcal O_C([P])_P\) (because \(f\)
502	  has a pole of order exactly \(1\) at \(P\) in the rational-function
503	  picture: \(f \in K(C) \setminus \bar k\) with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = [P]\)).
504	  Therefore by \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections} there
505	  exists a unique morphism
506	  \[
507	    \varphi \;:=\; \varphi_{(1, f)} \colon C \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
508	  \]
509	  with \(\varphi^* x_0 = 1\) and \(\varphi^* x_1 = f\) (so the pullback of
510	  the standard ratio coordinate \(u = x_1 / x_0\) on $\mathbb P^1 \setminus
511	  \{\infty\}\( is \)f$, and the pullback of the inverse coordinate
512	  \(t_\infty = x_0 / x_1 = 1 / f\) is the local parameter \(1/f\) at \(P\)).
513	  \emph{Step 3: \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\) via the pole divisor of \(f\).} The
514	  morphism \(\varphi\) is non-constant (since \(f\) is non-constant as a
515	  rational function on \(C\), the pullback \(\varphi^* u = f\) takes
516	  infinitely many values, ruling out a constant \(\varphi\)). By
517	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} applied to \(\varphi\),
518	  \[
519	    \deg(\varphi)
520	    \;=\;
521	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{Q \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
522	                  \ord_Q(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [Q]\,\right).
523	  \]
524	  We compute the pole divisor of \(f\) as a rational function on \(C\):
525	  since \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) with \(f\) non-constant and
526	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) the line bundle of the effective divisor \([P]\), the
527	  rational-function \(f\) has pole divisor \((f)_\infty = [P]\) (the
528	  unique pole of order \(1\) at \(P\), no other poles; this is the standard
529	  identification of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\) with the rational functions
530	  \(g \in K(C)\) satisfying \(\mathrm{div}(g) + D \geq 0\), specialised to
531	  \(D = [P]\)). Hence \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty) = \{P\}\) with multiplicity~\(1\)
532	  and
533	  \[
534	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\; \deg([P]) \;=\; 1.
535	  \]
536	  \emph{Step 4: \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.} By Hartshorne's IV.2
537	  identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\), the
538	  degree-one conclusion of Step~3 rewrites as
539	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) = 1\) (with
540	  the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
541	  \texttt{[Algebra \,(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField\,
542	   C.functionField]} instance). By
543	  \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} applied to the non-constant
544	  morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (smooth proper
545	  geometrically irreducible curves over \(\bar k\) on both sides), under
546	  this function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis, \(\varphi\) is an
547	  isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
548	  This produces the required isomorphism \(C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)
549	  closing the headline target
550	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}.
551	  \emph{Comparison with Hartshorne's original argument.} Hartshorne's
552	  Example~IV.1.3.5 phrases the argument in terms of two distinct points
553	  \(P, Q \in C\) and the degree-zero divisor \(D = P - Q\), deriving \(P \sim Q\)
554	  and concluding via II.6.10.1 that \(C\) is rational; combined with I.6.12
555	  this yields \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\). The present proof restates the same
556	  combinatorial content via the more directly formalisable linear-system
557	  construction: instead of going through the \(P \sim Q\) statement and
558	  invoking II.6.10.1 as a black box, we produce the explicit morphism
559	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1\) from the two sections \((1, f)\) of
560	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) given by Riemann--Roch and identify its degree
561	  directly. The two arguments are mathematically equivalent and have the
562	  same dependency footprint (Riemann--Roch in genus zero \(\Rightarrow\)
563	  \(\ell([P]) = 2\) \(\Rightarrow\) non-constant \(f\) \(\Rightarrow\) degree-\(1\)
564	  map to \(\mathbb P^1\) \(\Rightarrow\) iso); the linear-system phrasing maps
565	  more cleanly onto the Mathlib \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} API and
566	  the project's existing Weil-divisor/degree pipeline of \texttt{RR.1}.
567	\end{proof}

582	\section{Out of scope}
583	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.4} and
584	are sequenced elsewhere:
585	\begin{itemize}
586	  \item The general ``smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) of
587	    genus \(0\) over a \emph{non-closed} field \(k\) with a \(k\)-rational point
588	    \(\cong \mathbb P^1_k\)'' statement is the \(\bar k \to k\) descent of
589	    \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso}. The descent is hosted in
590	    \texttt{Jacobian.tex} via \texttt{genusZeroWitness.key}; it consumes
591	    \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} together with Galois descent for
592	    smooth proper curves and is out of scope for \texttt{RR.4}.
593	  \item The Mathlib API \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}
594	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}) is
595	    consumed as a black box by
596	    \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}; its own proof inside
597	    Mathlib (the universal property of \(\mathrm{Proj}\), graded-ring
598	    presentation of \(\mathbb P^n\)) is not re-derived here.
599	  \item Multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9)
600	    is consumed as a black box by \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor};
601	    its proof is a separable sub-lemma threaded inside
602	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} and is
603	    \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.4}'s blueprint statement (it lives at
604	    the same logical level as the closely related
605	    \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}).
606	  \item The full equivalence of categories ``smooth projective curves over
607	    \(\bar k\) with dominant morphisms'' \(\cong\) ``function fields of
608	    transcendence degree~\(1\) over \(\bar k\) with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms''
609	    (Hartshorne~I.6.12) is consumed as a high-level framing in
610	    \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} but is \emph{not} re-formalised at
611	    the equivalence-of-categories level here. The proof body in the Lean
612	    file threads the spelled-out scheme-theoretic ``finite-degree-one
613	    morphism between integral normal schemes is an isomorphism''
614	    argument (Stacks~\textbf{0AVX}), which is the minimal-Mathlib-dependence
615	    pin compatible with the chapter's downstream needs.
616	  \item ``A smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)
617	    has at least two distinct \(\bar k\)-points'' (the non-empty / infinite
618	    cardinality of \(C(\bar k)\)) is consumed implicitly by the present
619	    chapter but its formal statement lives in the project's curve API
620	    (\texttt{Genus.tex} / \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus}); the chapter
621	    does not restate it.
622	  \item Cohomology of the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) on a curve
623	    (Hartshorne~III.5 + IV.1) is fully consumed via \texttt{RR.3}; in
624	    particular the \(H^1\)-vanishing of \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) on a genus-\(0\)
625	    curve is part of \texttt{RR.3}'s payload feeding
626	    \texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}, and is not
627	    re-derived here.
628	\end{itemize}
